Chapter1: AnUnsportingAttack art by catherine7mk, poster by nikitajobson ~ As a man of means, Draco Malfoy could have chosen to live a life of leisure, political meddling, and casual blackmail, like his father before him. However, his acquittal by the Wizengamot was accompanied by strong recommendations that young Mr. Malfoy strive for such laudable pursuits as the Common Good, Altruism, and Redemption in the Public Eye. And so, after a few years of sowing his wild oats (and a great many curses) on the Continent, Draco had returned to London, where he made short work of the Auror training programme Å three years down to one and a half, if you please Å and joined that noble Office. Draco had been strategic in his choice of career, of course: being an Auror offered just enough heroics for positive coverage in the news and just enough Ministry-sanctioned murders to keep him interested in the job. Draco was an excellent Auror Å something about very nearly becoming a Dark wizard himself gave him rather useful insights into the minds of naughty wizards and witches. The problem with competence, however, was that it was rewarded with increasingly complex cases by the Head of the Auror Office, a certain Madam Nymphadora Tonks. And so, our opening scene: a Monday morning, sometime in January. Amidst the greying cubicles of the Auror Office, Tonks was doling out the monthís Class A assignments to her top Aurors like a vindictive Father Christmas. ØMontjoy Å youíre off to Hethpool. Three Muggle children found dead with their livers removed. That hag coven from Stow may have regrouped.Æ A folder containing the case material was slapped onto Montjoyís desk. ØBuckley Å suspected necromancy and other foul play, Isle of Man.Æ Buckley accepted the proffered casefile with a grimace. ØYouíre to take Humphreys with you. Mind you be a good mentor and donít traumatise her too much.Æ Tonks rounded the corner to the next cubicles. ØPotter, Weasley Å youíre to continue with the vampires in the Dales, but if you donít make further headway, I will get personally involved. Half of Yorkshire will be sucked dry at this rate. Goggin Å some idiot is experimenting with Transmogrifian Torture on Muggle prostitutes in Glenluce. I wonít notice if you bring him in with a few missing appendages.Æ Tonks now came to a halt in front of Dracoís desk. ØMalfoy. Since you did so well with the Lanark Lunatic last week, Iíll let you pick your poison.Æ Draco eyed Tonks guardedly Å poison was unlikely to be an exaggeration. ØWhat are my options?Æ Tonks dropped two files onto Dracoís desk. ØOption one, a wizard accused of inappropriate acts with trolls Å a real delight for the senses, that one. Or, option two Å a request from the Minister for Auror protection of a high-profile target.Æ ØInappropriate acts?" repeated Draco, pulling the folders towards himself. ØI donít know about your tolerance level, but Iíve quite lost my appetite.Æ Tonks jutted her chin towards the rightmost folder. ØThere are photographs for your edification.Æ Draco made the mistake of opening the troll folder. He closed it again with a strangled sound of disgust. ØIíll take the protection assignment.Æ ØRight-o,Æ said Tonks, swiping the troll folder and its hideous contents from Dracoís desk. ØThe troll-buggerer will go to Fernsby. Fernsby! Come here.Æ Fernsby emerged from a distant cubicle. Tonks slapped the folder into his chest. ØYou are off to Morpeth. I hear the North Sea is lovely this time of year.Æ If Fernsby had reservations about the loveliness of a January sojourn by the North Sea, he kept them to himself. Tonks was rarely worth arguing with. ØProgress reports on my desk by Monday morning,Æ called Tonks to the office at large. A grumble of assent from the Aurors followed the request. Tonks gave Draco a sharp look. ØLooking forward to yours, Malfoy. Iíve a degree of curiosity about that one Å the target is working on some top secret project. They wouldnít even tell me what it's about.Æ Tonks made her way back to her office, managing to tread on an unsuspecting colleagueís foot only once. Draco, now rather curious, pulled the folder towards himself. The protection request came straight from the Ministerís Office and Shacklebolt had requested a security audit, defensive warding, every confidentiality-enhancing measure known to wizardkind, escorting, if you please, and protective surveillance Å in sum, the bloody works. Draco was preemptively irritated Å this sounded rather a lot like effort. And who, pray, merited this extravagant treatment? He flipped over a few more pages of Ministerial demands to find, finally, the Principal. And it was Hermione. Bloody. Granger. Her photograph was pinned to the top of a brief biographical note Å as though anyone alive today didnít know her and her hair. She looked seriously at Draco, blinked at him once, then left the frame. Draco seized the folder and headed for Tonksí office. She was rarely worth arguing with, but this casefile merited an especial attempt. ØTonks Å I canít take this one. Youíll have to give it to someone else.Æ Tonks looked up from the parchment sheíd been attacking with a quill. Her hair turned a quizzical mauve. ØWhyever not?Æ ØItís Granger. Thatís the Principal. Hadnít you seen?Æ ØAnd?Æ ØWe donít exactly get along,Æ said Draco in a vast understatement. ØAre you telling me that some school-time unpleasantness from fifteen years ago will interfere with your ability to carry out this assignment?Æ asked Tonks. In the Foe Glass behind her, shadowy silhouettes clustered about, as though keen to eavesdrop on the drama. ØWe have a rather unhappy history,Æ said Draco. ØWorse than you and Potter?Æ This, Draco considered for a moment. Finally, he answered, ØIn some ways.Æ ØFine,Æ sniffed Tonks. ØSwap with Fernsby. Iím sure heíll only be too happy to change out a cushy protection jobbie for the troll aficionado.Æ Ø...Isnít there anything else I could take?Æ Tonks gave him a quelling look, emphasised by her eyes turning a dangerous, hawkish kind of yellow. ØIíve just assigned the monthís missions, Malfoy, and I wonít have your complex about Granger domino its way through the entirety of it.Æ ØI donít have a complex about Granger.Æ ØGood. Then youíll do fine. Off you go.Æ Tonks waved her hand and her office door closed slowly, squeezing Draco out. Draco strode back to his desk, half intending to ask Fernsby for the swap Å however, the gurgle of horror emanating from Fernsbyís cubicle was sufficient to change his mind. Fine. Heíd do the Granger thing. It was, at any rate, not troll pornography. ~ Draco sent Granger a coldly professional note stating that he would be pleased to meet with her at her earliest convenience to discuss the Ministerís protection request. Granger sent back an equally cold note indicating that the Ministerís request was an overblown reaction on the Ministerís part and that she would be dealing with it shortly, and to please disregard it. Draco did not respond, but enjoyed an afternoon off instead of informing Tonks of this fortunate development immediately. Then Granger ruined everything by writing again, indicating that, to her disappointment, the Minister had not changed his mind, and was forging ahead with this (disproportionate and illogical, in her opinion) plan of action. Would Draco be available to meet at nine oíclock this Thursday? The Granger Laboratory. Trinity College, Cambridge. As he tossed the missive into the fire, Draco thought, Cambridge, of course. How could we expect anything less from Hermione Granger? ~ That Thursday, Draco arrived at Trinity College at the beastly hour of nine o'clock. The porter at the gate didnít glance twice at his robes Å many of the Muggles wandering about were wearing long black gowns Å but he did give Draco a sharp look when he said he was there to see Granger. ØDoctor Granger,Æ said the porter. ØHave you got an appointment, sir?Æ ØYes.Æ ØName?Æ ØMalfoy,Æ said Draco. The porter consulted a chart. He found whatever he was looking for, apparently, because Draco was waved in towards the verdant quad at Trinity College. (ØItís not a quad, we call them courts at Cambridge,Æ said the porter to some tourists, but Draco paid him no mind Å he knew a quad when he saw one.) Grangerís note had included a few directions on how to enter the wizarding part of the College, which brought Draco to a magically concealed door at the south end of the quad. A Muggle plaque indicated that Kingís Hall had once stood here, but that it had been destroyed in the sixteenth century. Draco tapped the bronze plaque with his wand, as instructed by Granger, and the ostensibly destroyed Kingís Hall appeared before him. Draco decided that Granger earned a two out of ten in his initial security assessment Å at least rogue Muggles wouldnít immediately be able to find her. And, with that generous thought, he strode into Magical Cambridge. At nine oíclock on a weekday, Kingís Hall was a roiling bustle of scholarly witches and wizards, off to advance magical knowledge. Draco had spent years at the Universit de Paris to earn his Bachelor's in Alchemy and his Mastery in Martial Magic (Duelling), but heíd never set foot in an institution of higher learning in the UK. Kingís Hall retained its sixteenth century ambience Å dark, an excess of over-carved wood, and candlelight Å and vacillated somewhere between pure Gothic and early Renaissance in dcor. As he surveyed the crowd before him (varyingly studious or eccentric-looking), Draco wondered how much of wizarding Britainís brain power was located within these hallowed halls. At any rate, there was at least one big brain on the premises. Quite lost amongst five staircases on the first floor, he decided to enquire for directions towards that brain. ØYou there,Æ said Draco, jutting his chin towards a spotty youth. The boy looked about twenty-two, serious, and clutched a text on Advanced Theoretical Arithmancy to his chest. ØYes?Æ asked the youth. ØIím looking for Granger,Æ said Draco. The boy frowned at him. ØProfessor Granger. Her offices are on the third floor, with the other Fellows.Æ ØCheers,Æ said Draco, wondering how many more times he was going to be corrected regarding precious Grangerís title today. He climbed the stairs and passed corridors where he spotted a variety of interesting things: classrooms, lounges, reading rooms, offices, an apothecary, a caf, and what appeared to be a small zoo. Finally, he came to a door which simply said, ØGRANGER. Ring for attention.Æ See? There. No overzealous titles. Draco rang for attention. Then he peered into the narrow window that flanked the door and almost turned around to leave again, because the laboratory beyond seemed decidedly Muggleand he mustíve taken a wrong turn somewhere, only it said ØGRANGER,Æ right there. His ring was answered by a Being in a bright white coat and strange translucent face-coverings. ØCan I help you?Æ asked the Being. ØIím looking for Granger,Æ answered Draco. ØHealer Granger doesnít take walk-ins,Æ said the Being, with a rather stiffened back. ØIs she expecting you?Æ ØShe is,Æ said Draco, adding this new title to the increasingly ridiculous running list. ØAll right,Æ said the Being, with what was probably a suspicious look, but Draco couldnít tell behind the goggle-things. ØHer office is down to the right.Æ The Being moved out of the way. From the voice, Draco, was now relatively certain it was a human female, but the accoutrements made it difficult to say. In any case, Draco was in. His initial assessment of Grangerís security measures plummeted to a strong one out of ten. It pleased him to give Granger a well-deserved horrid mark; it didnít please him to think of the work that would be involved bringing this place up to snuff. He knocked on the office door. ØCome in,Æ said Grangerís voice. A blast from the past Å crisp, prissy, impatient. Draco entered the office. Granger was sitting behind a tidy, if over-stacked, desk. They stared at one another in a decidedly Awkward Moment, something that Draco, now a fully qualified and rather dangerous Auror, was not used to anymore Å and perhaps, judging by the unhappy set of her mouth, neither was Granger. Time heals all wounds, but between himself and Granger, there were a great many to heal, and right now, fifteen years felt like a rather short time since they'd been children fighting each other on opposite sides of a war. Draco couldnít recall when he had last spoken to her directly, and he certainly knew heíd never been alone in a room with her. Granger rose to greet him with the following display of eloquence: ØMalfoy.Æ ØGranger,Æ said Draco, with equal eloquence. She gestured to a chair across the desk. As he stepped towards it, Draco found himself being assessed by her. Her gaze flitted from his hair to his face, to the Auror insignia on his chest, and down his black robes to his boots. Seeing that they were dispensing with the niceties, Draco shamelessly assessed her in return: the hair (a curling pile coiled high at her crown), the face (thinner, more severe than he remembered), the same strange white cloak as the Being, the black jeans (so Muggle), the casual trainers. Draco opened his mouth to make a few vague opening remarks Å some chatter about Cambridge, or Potter and Weasley, or other such fluff Å but Granger jumped straight to the point. ØThis is an absolute waste of Auror resources.Æ The lack of finesse was quite typey for Granger. Some things didnít change. Draco settled himself into his chair. ØGive me a bit more to go on and I can make a case to Shacklebolt to withdraw the request. Iíve no more desire to be here than you.Æ Granger pursed her lips at him. Draco wondered when McGonagall had Apparated into Grangerís chair, and where Granger had got to. ØAll right,Æ said Granger at length. ØA fortnight ago, I updated Shacklebolt on the progress of a certain research project. A research project which is not under the Ministryís purview, nor funded by it, by the by. I was sharing what I thought was a bit of good news with a long-time friend and mentor Å who happens to be the Minister of Magic. Apparently, the news was too good. Shacklebolt fears repercussions, as the project will have implications for a certain segment of the population.Æ ØWhat implications?Æ asked Draco. ØWhich segment?Æ ØIíd rather not say, as my hope is that you wonít be involved any further than this meeting. Shacklebolt is overreacting. I shall speak with him again this week and convince him that putting me under Auror surveillance is utterly unnecessary.Æ ØAuror protection,Æ corrected Draco. Aurors of his calibre werenít assigned to two-bit surveillance jobs, thank you. ØCall it what you will,Æ said Granger. ØShacklebolt has his flaws, but a propensity for overreaction isnít one of them,Æ said Draco. (There wasnít much love lost between himself and the Minister, but there was a certain respect.) ØNo, it isnít one of his propensities. Which is why I was rather surprised Å dismayed, really Å by his decision to involve your office.Æ ØIs it possible that he isnít overreacting?Æ The look Granger levelled at him was decidedly unfriendly. ØNo.Æ ØYou donít think that this Å breakthrough, or discovery Å of yours is putting you at any new risk whatsoever?Æ ØNot at the moment. First, no one knows about this development, other than Shacklebolt himself and Å to varying degrees Å my staff, all of whom I trust implicitly. And, secondly, though Iíve made a breakthrough, I havenít quite solved the issue yet. That will be the work of at least another year. I wonít be on the front page of The Prophet asking to be murdered tomorrow.Æ Draco's eyebrow twitched upwards. ØShacklebolt thinks youíre going to be murdered?Æ ØHe thinks Å probably rightly Å that some people wonít be pleased about my breakthrough.Æ Draco decided that he needed to speak to Shacklebolt. Perhaps heíd be less cagey than Granger and disclose something useful to the Auror assigned to her. He found himself truly curious, now, about the nature of this Good Discovery. His next question was carefully phrased. He didnít want to cast aspersions on Grangerís heritage (gods forbid; he was already on thin ice everywhere on that front), but there were things she mightnít know, as a Muggle-born. ØMight Shacklebolt be aware of certain wizarding predilections or biases that you arenít, that would be a cause for concern?Æ Granger took a breath, as one might if one was summoning oneís remaining patience. ØIf I told you Iíd solved world famine, or something equally wonderful, would you pause to worry about the actions of a few naysayers?Æ ØOne naysayer would be enough to dispatch a do-gooding researcher, especially one who keeps her laboratory secured with a third-rate locking charm and some chicken wire.Æ One of Grangerís knees began to bounce. It brought to mind a cat twitching its tail in annoyance. ØSo have you?Æ asked Draco. ØHave I what?Æ ØSolved world famine.Æ ØNothing so grand. That was an example.Æ ØWhere do you keep your findings?Æ asked Draco. Now it was Grangerís turn to raise an eyebrow, which was the entirety of her response. Draco gestured to the office around him and the laboratory on the other side of the door. ØIíve identified a dozen vulnerabilities already Å and thatís only what I saw in the five minutes it took to walk up here. If I wanted to work it out, I rather think I could.Æ ØDo you?Æ ØYes.Æ Seeing Granger smirk wasè something. However, it rapidly disappeared. ØIf weíre talking of physical security, I havenít exactly had a reason to increase it beyond the usual measures until recently. I can assure you that Iím capable of warding my laboratory beyond a locking charm Å and keeping my data safe.Æ ØPerfect,Æ said Draco. ØProceed with that. Iíll be back in a few days to do a penetration test. If you satisfy that Å and implement any additional measures I recommend Å we may be able to convince Shacklebolt that you and your research are safe, and weíll be able to put this behind us.Æ This challenge was dolled out with a Å quite laudable, Draco thought Å minimum of arrogance on his part. Grangerís eyes grew hard: the challenge was recognised and accepted. ØFine. And when will this penetration test take place?Æ ØIím not giving you a warning,Æ said Draco, rising. ØDo you think a real-world threat would?Æ ØBrilliant,Æ said Granger, rising too. Sarcasm roughened the edge of her words. ØI do love surprises.Æ They did not shake hands and she did not see him out. ~ Draco scheduled a visit with the Minister of Magic later that week. He sauntered past the Ministerís sour-faced assistant on the designated day, wondering who had pissed in her Pixie Puffs. Shacklebolt was as reticent with the details as Granger had been, but impressed upon Draco the importance of keeping Granger safe to complete her project, for the benefit of all wizardkind. It was all very grand and extremely vague. The only positive was Shackleboltís evident pleasure that it was Draco who had ended up with the assignment. ØI know you wonít hesitate to get nasty, Malfoy, if any malicious individuals were to make a move against her.Æ Draco accepted the backhanded compliment with a mock bow. ØYouíre warming the cockles of my heart, Minister.Æ Shacklebolt returned the bow with an inclination of his head. Then he grew sombre. ØShe could change the lives of hundreds Å thousands Å for the better.Æ ØAnd yet, neither she nor you will tell me what the project entails. Did she make you take a bloody Vow of Secrecy before she disclosed anything?Æ Shacklebolt raised his hands, not responding one way or another, and thus gave Draco his answer. ØShe would have the foresight,Æ said Draco, throwing a fistful of Floo powder into Shackleboltís fireplace. ØCambridge.Æ This was it. Heíd given her long enough to prepare. ~ It was late on Monday evening. King's Hall was quiet. Draco supposed that Granger was off having dinner or browbeating innocent undergraduates. He stood at the door of her laboratory, tapping his wand to his chin thoughtfully. However, before he had cast any kind of revelation charm or begun any sort of snooping, Granger rounded the corner. ØMalfoy,Æ she said, looking a little dishevelled and out of breath. Draco filed her timely arrival away for future analysis. She was too clever for it to be coincidence Å and yet, he hadnít cast a single spell that wouldíve made his presence known. Granger had forsaken her Muggle clothing for green Healer robes. She looked both irritable and impatient, and quickly confirmed both of those conditions by asking: ØTime for your vaunted test, is it? How long will it take?Æ Draco did not appreciate her tone, which suggested that this might be an affair of several hours. ØThat depends on your warding Å Iím thinking a quarter of an hour at the upper end.Æ Grangerís eyebrow rose at the cockiness of this rejoinder. ØGood. Just did a shift at A&E and Iím positively knackered.Æ She waved her wand and, with a rather impressive display of Transfiguration (not that Draco gave any sign that he was impressed), she transformed one of her hairpins into a glossy wooden chair, upon which she perched herself to observe him. Draco didn't mind an audience, especially when he was going to systematically dismantle the audienceís attempts to keep him out, and teach her some humility. Draco turned his attention back to the door. ØA&E? I thought you were a researcher.Æ ØThe MNHS is chronically understaffed. I take shifts at St. Mungoís to help out. Keeps my Healing skills sharp.Æ ØGood of you.Æ ØMm.Æ After a few revelation spells, Draco had to hand it to Granger Å sheíd done her homework. Not a surprise, really. The protective enchantments that now warded the door to her laboratory were many, quite complex, and well-cast. Draco got to work, but not without taking the piss just a little. ØCaterwauling Charm? Insulting.Æ ØIíve learned to work from the lowest common denominator up,Æ was the dry response. The basic intruder charms that followed were dismissed with a few wand waves. The Salvio Hexia was a good warmup. Then Draco got into the good stuff: Foribus Ignis, Custos Portae, a hair-trigger Confundus aimed directly at his head, revealed only when heíd peeled away the other two wards, a sneaky Blinding Hex that just seemed mean, a Balding Jinx that was decidedly unsportsmanlike, and a concealed Confringo on the door handle itself for anyone stupid enough to touch it. Draco disarmed the latter Å a little touch and go, admittedly, and he did break a sweat Å telling himself that at least if his face was blown off, there was a Healer nearby who would be able to assist. The door unlocked. It had taken all of four minutes. And yet, Granger looked unimpressed. Draco swung open the door to reveal Å a stone wall. ØFunny,Æ said Draco. His face showed none of his disquiet, but heíd been wasting his time on an absolutely impeccable decoy. He waved his wand a metre further down the wall and the real door to the laboratory appeared. Granger shrugged. ØI needed my staff able to get in. They arenít experts at disarming wards, but they can handle a Finite Incantatem.Æ Draco entered the laboratory to continue his assessment, his neck rather stiff. His audience waved her chair back into a hairpin and followed. "Normally I would insist upon us donning the proper PPE, per Trinity's wet laboratory protocols," said Granger. "But we've tidied for the day. I don't think you can hurt yourself on anything." Once again, Draco didn't care for her tone, which, this time, suggested that he might otherwise off himself by accident. He ignored the sterile white and steel surfaces that made up most of the space and moved to the shelves and cupboards at one end of the laboratory, which looked like a likely place for an active laboratory to store data. However, the well-organised contents were useless Å it was mostly Muggle scientific literature, including some of Grangerís own publications. Words jumped out at Draco without meaning: cytokines, monoclonal antibodies, chimeric antigen receptors, T-cells... ØI realise the purpose of this test is to see how far youíd get and what you can discover about my research Å but do put things back in an orderly fashion,Æ came Grangerís voice, irritation lacing her words. Draco, his back to her, permitted himself a healthy roll of his eyes Å one text was half an inch out of place. He pushed it back in. He waved his wand at the entirety of the collection to uncover Transfigurations or concealment spells, but there were none. Then he systematically did the same with the rest of the laboratory, seeking any hidey holes or caches or Å as he grew annoyed Å any magical trace whatsoever. There was nothing magical except the contents of the various vials and test tubes clustered in tidy groups along the laboratoryís workbenches. ØIf I stole these and had them analysed, what would I discover?Æ asked Draco. The glow of his spell illuminated the vials of interest. Granger walked towards them and pointed. ØGamma delta T cells. Antigens: MART-1, Tyrosinase, GP100, Survivin. All of magical provenance, which is why your spell is revealing them, but not otherwise noteworthy.Æ ØI see,Æ said Draco, who did not see at all. ØI donít know who your hypothetical analysis would be conducted by, in the event that these were to be stolen to uncover what Iím working on, but I should tell you that very few people in the UK would be able to pull meaningful conclusions out of this.Æ Draco felt the false modesty in the words; by very few, she meant none at all Å Iím surrounded by idiots and Iím the only one who can make sense of any of these horrifically named extracts. ØAnd those?Æ asked Draco, pointing to larger, rather more familiar looking vials along the back row. ØYour hypothetical analysts would discover perfectly brewed Sanitatem,Æ said Granger. ØThatís a healing potion,Æ she added, quite unnecessarily. ØA find of critical importance, in the laboratory of a Healer,Æ said Draco, his annoyance lapsing into sarcasm. There was the tiniest quirk at the corner of Grangerís mouth Å amusement, rapidly stifled. Draco was doing his own stifling, but in his case, it was exasperation. She had wasted his time on a wild goose chase with those door wards, knowing that there was nothing of real use in the laboratory itself, unless one was in possession of about twelve doctorates to put it all together. But she had to be recording findings Å she was too methodological and meticulous not to. Now Draco turned to a corner of the laboratory that heíd ignored as a matter of course. It was the most Muggleish area of the entire place Å a corner desk cluttered with glowing boxes of light. Granger might as well have cast a Notice-Me-Not on the lot. Had she? No, his detection spells showed nothing. That had been a feature of his own built-in habits Å his eyes almost naturally averted themselves from the unmagical, the Utterly Mundane, the Terribly Muggle. Heíd have to watch that Å clearly, a weakness. He walked towards the desk. And, for the first time since Draco had entered the laboratory, Granger actually perked up and looked interested in the proceedings. Now he was getting somewhere. ØComputers,Æ said Draco, pulling up some distant memory from Muggle Studies. ØWell done,Æ said Granger, with the tone one would take to praise an especially slow child who had correctly identified a barn animal. Draco favoured her with a dark look. Her face was impassive, but her eyes betrayed her Å she was curious about what he was going to do next. And, of course, he hadnít the faintest sodding clue where to go from here, other than jinxing the computers into submission Å but from what he recalled, these devices weren't sentient. He stood before the glowing boxes, upon which slow lines were moving in random patterns. Ø...Iíd need to bring in a Muggle-born,Æ said Draco at length. ØO, yes, that would be a start,Æ said Granger. She looked at her nails. ØYouíd want to find one who is a decent hacker, too. Iím not sure many of those exist amongst wizardkind, but perhaps one or two in the UK.Æ ØA Hacker.Æ ØYes,Æ said Granger, offering no further explanation of the violent term. ØIf Å as I suspect Å your findings are in these things Å whatís to stop me, a baddie, from destroying the lot, and stopping your research in its tracks?Æ asked Draco. Granger shrugged. ØIt wouldn't matter. Itís all in the cloud.Æ ØThe cloud.Æ ØYes. Iíd be out the cost of the equipment, thatís all.Æ ØSo your bog standard Dark wizard, up to no good, wouldnít have much to discover here.Æ ØIím afraid not,Æ said Granger. ØThe wards at the door were an amusing puzzle. Thank you for wasting my time.Æ ØI wanted to see if youíre as good as they say.Æ Draco gave her a quick look, wanting to know who they was, because he did like to hear how good he was. Granger did not indulge him. ØI had a few other ideas for other hexes and things,Æ she said, gesturing to the door, Øbut I hadnít the time.Æ ØSo, no evidence of concealment, no written findings, computers, cloudsèÆ Draco looked at Granger. ØIf Iím a baddie who needs information, what do I do next?Æ Granger looked at him inquiringly. ØWhat do you do?Æ ØI go after you,Æ said Draco. He raised his wand and, a split second later, his spell hit her in the chest. Chapter 2: Draco Malfoy,GeniusInventor art by gingerhuneybee The Lumos dissipated harmlessly into Grangerís robes, but her shock was nevertheless evident. ØThat was unnecessary,Æ she gasped, a hand at her breast. Draco made his way towards Grangerís office with a bit of a saunter. ØI promise you other spells wouldnít be so friendly.Æ ØNo oneís going to be casting unfriendly spells at me for no reason,Æ said Granger, following him. ØThey donít have a reason now, but if your Big Breakthrough is as significant as Shacklebolt thinks, and if Å when Å it gets out, thenèÆ He turned to her again, his wand raised. She was readier this time and spat out a Protego. ØBetter,Æ said Draco. ØHowís your resistance to the Imperius Curse?Æ Granger grew still, her hand gripping her wand. ØIf you cast that on me in my own laboratory, I shall drown you in Sanitatem and enjoy the irony.Æ Draco glanced above him. Every vial of Sanitatem had levitated off the benches and was hovering over his head. In a real situation, heíd Vanish the lot and blast Granger through two walls for the cheek. But, nevertheless Å it was an impressive bit of nonverbal magic. ØIíll concede that your research is more-or-less safe, physically, from most wizarding intruders,Æ said Draco. The vials settled back into place. ØBut it all lives in your head and can therefore be read Å or tortured Å out of you, or any of your staff.Æ ØIím the PI on the project in question. My staff consists of five undergraduates and eight graduate students whose combined understanding of the project is probably fifteen percent, scattered through thirteen minds. They arenít much of a vulnerability.Æ Draco gave her a hard look. ØThen youíre the vulnerability.Æ She, predictably, looked offended. ØHowís your Occlumency?Æ asked Draco. The question was accompanied, of course, by a friendly bit of Legilimency. Draco was granted a clear view of Grangerís perception of him at that precise moment Å tall, arrogant ponce with good hair Å and then he was mentally slapped out of her mind. He pressed a finger to the centre of his forehead; this witch was making his brain sting. Meanwhile, Granger looked like she wished to double down and slap him in the material world for good measure Å and wouldnít that just be a lovely throwback to their school days? ØI thought we were assessing my laboratory, not me,Æ said Granger, her eyes flashing at him. åWeíre assessing risk exposures,Æ said Draco. ØAnd itís quickly becoming obvious that youíre a significant one. Is your home warded?Æ ØModerately. I can enhance it.Æ ØIíll enhance it,Æ said Draco. ØHow do you travel?Æ ØFloo, Apparition...Æ ØThose are trackable, you know. Broom?Æ ØI detest flying,Æ said Granger. Draco made a valiant effort not to curl his lip. What a terrible position to take. What a dreadful thing to hate. What a sad circumvention of one of the greatest joys of being Magical. Granger fell in his esteem quite irredeemably. ØSince when is Apparition trackable, other than the Trace?Æ asked Granger. ØTop secret,Æ said Draco, now in Grangerís office. He riffled through the various stacks of paperwork and books, encountering, again, nothing but that highly specialised, utterly incomprehensible Muggle jargon, and no sign of recent developments, note-taking, record-keeping, or anything of a useful nature that might point him to Grangerís precious findings. There was another computer in the office, which Draco eyed with a resigned kind of vexation. How stupid to be flummoxed by a device that any Muggle off the street could probably operate. Perhaps he shouldíve kidnapped the porter at the gate and brought him in to assist Å Statute of Secrecy notwithstanding. He stared at the computer intimidatingly, waiting for it to confess its sins, but it merely offered him wobbly lines. As Draco snooped, scanned and searched for interesting magical giveaways in the rest of the office, Granger pulled off her Healer robes and dropped into the chair that Draco had occupied upon his first visit. She let out a sigh of unadulterated fatigue. Draco glanced at her. Muggle clothing again, underneath. This time a long-sleeved top and some trousers that barely merited the name, more like opaque black tights, really. Was this decent public attire by Muggle standards? Shocking. He could see the precise outline of her calf and the exact shape of her knee. He didnít spend too long musing upon the foibles of Muggle fashion, however, as the witch herself was a bit of a concern. He could see now how thin she was, how her collarbones shadowed, how her neck seemed too dainty to hold the mass of hair pinned upon her head. She was pale, peaky, and generally looked overdrawn. ØWhatís your schedule like, Granger?Æ Draco asked, as though continuing his querying about her travel patterns, but really wanting to get a sense of what exactly this woman did with herself, day in and day out. Typically, Granger had a schedule ready Å colour-coded and planned to the hour. She waved her wand in the direction of her desk and the schedule floated to Draco and deposited itself into his hands. Using his wand as a makeshift quill, Draco drew circles around her moments of exposure, when sheíd move between places and be most vulnerable to attack. And there were many Å Granger was everywhere and did everything. She had dedicated laboratory hours, clinic hours, teaching hours, volunteering for a horrid amount of Good Causes, tutoring sessions, mentoring sessions, Healing at St. Mungoís and what sounded like a local Muggle surgery, one (1) pub night every fortnight with Potter and Friends, College dinners, something called ØyogaÆ at unholy hours in the morning, something called ØCrooks VetÆ that recurred every three months, and then occasional days, here and there, marked only with an asterisk. ØWhat are these?Æ asked Draco, pointing at one of the blocks with an asterisk. Ø...Holidays,Æ said Granger. ØYour Occlumency might be passable, but your lying isnít.Æ ØTheyíre days off.Æ Granger grew snippy. ØAnd I shanít be divulging more details of my personal life than I already have, thank you.Æ Draco dropped the subject Å and the schedule, back onto her desk. Overdrawn wasnít even the right word for Granger: exhausted, or depleted, maybe. Draco recalled some vague rumour that young Granger had been granted a Time-Turner during their Hogwarts years, to squeeze more classes into her school days. Potter and Weasley had quickly dismissed that bit of Auror lunch-hour chatter. Looking at the overzealous, overachieving, overtired witch before him, Draco found himself rather inclined to believe the tale. He continued his search, though he doubted there would be much else to find. The wall at the rear of the office was covered in frames of various sizes, certificates, diplomas, awardsè ØNice mosaic,Æ said Draco. Granger gave him a look. Well, he found himself funny, even if Granger didnít. The mosaic informed Draco that Granger didnít quite have twelve doctorates, but her combination of Muggle and Magical diplomas probably approached that number. Again, the Muggle ones were a mystery, awarded by Muggle universities he hadnít heard of: Bachelorís in Biomedical Sciences, Masterís in Microbiology and Immunology, joint M.D.-PhD in Oncology, some minor certificate in Genetics. He recognised the Healerís Seal, at least (Cambridge, specialising in Magical Diseases). Her other magical certifications were a Masterís in Transfiguration (Edinburgh; an earlier degree, just after the War, probably) and a Specialised Study in Healing (Blood Magicks) from the Sorbonne. A smattering of other certificates and qualifications completed Grangerís educational oeuvre. A box on a low shelf revealed a few dusty older frames. The things he knew her for in her brilliant Hogwarts days Å the record-breaking O.W.L.s, the absurd amount of N.E.W.T.s Å didnít merit a place upon her wall of adult achievements. He spotted an Order of Merlin, First Class. Potter had similar, proudly hung upon his cubicle wall, but Granger hadnít the room, apparently. Granger excused herself to make tea, and, in a show of civility that appeared moderately challenging to verbalise, asked if heíd like a cup. Draco said no. Granger looked relieved. After sheíd left, Draco, being a pragmatic and sneakish kind of person, took advantage of the moment to cast a few discreet tracking spells upon a handful of her personal items: the trainers under the desk, hairpins (the blarmed things were everywhere), a half-finished mug of tea. He rifled through the paperwork on her desk and found nothing of interest (conference invitations, Muggle grant application results, notes from students. Useless tat). The computer made a sound like a small ping. Draco turned to it. Its dark surface and wiggling lines challenged him to touch it and die of Elektik Shocks. Then Draco gasped and said, ØHang on!Æ ØWhat?Æ asked Granger, who had just reentered the room. ØThis whole place is so Muggleish that I hadnít even thought to ask, but Å how are these computers working? Weíre in a magical building.Æ ØOh, that,Æ said Granger. She made what Draco presumed was meant to be a casual shrug (it wasnít very casual). ØI found ways to circumvent the issue.Æ ØHow?Æ ØWays,Æ said Granger. ØWhat ways?Æ asked Draco. She stared at him as though assessing his worthiness for this knowledge. In the face of her open eye-contact, Draco was sorely tempted to attempt Legilimency again. Just as the thought passed his mind, her eyes lost some of their sparkle. She was Occluding. ØI found a solution,Æ said Granger with another vague gesture. ØI couldnít possibly work with only quills and parchment; thatís positively archaic. Not to mention the hundreds of thousands of calculations and projections Iíve needed to doè Anyway, you neednít preoccupy yourself with it. I can assure you that itís nothing dangerous.Æ Draco stepped closer to the computer, observing the various gadgets connected to its periphery by long smooth fibres. Only a few things werenít connected to the Principal Organ (as he named the glowing box part), including three smallish metallic pucks set around the thing. Rather how one might set up a perimeter, really. To keep things in or out. He strode to the collection of computers in the laboratory proper, Granger following with a kind of polite curiosity. There, too, were the metallic pucks. Six of them, this time, creating a jagged circle. ØIíd be careful handling those,Æ said Granger. Draco, whose hand had been hovering above one of the pucks, pulled back. ØItís not dangerous, but you wonít like the feeling.Æ She came beside him and held one up. ØIím calling it an Anti-Magical Forcefield, for lack of a better term. Rather challenging to create, but it serves my purposes.Æ Draco stared at her. Blocking magic was a tricky bit of work Å a thing mostly relegated to abstruse theoretical discussions. The handful of magic-inhibiting artefacts heíd heard of were things of distant legend, lost to the passage of time. And yetè ØI got the idea from wifi hotspots in cafs and airports, only, of course, this is the contrary,Æ said Granger. Then, seeing from his face that that explained nothing, she said: ØNever mind.Æ ØIím not entirely certain that those are legal,Æ said Draco, looking at the pucks. ØBetter report me to Shacklebolt,Æ said Granger. Her eyes met his, unfriendly, unafraid. Draco decided that Granger had balls, possibly rivalling Tonksí enormous pair. The beginnings of a Plan were germinating in his head. ØI need a copy of your schedule,Æ he said, leading the way back to Grangerís office. A quick Duplicatus sorted that, paired with a Protean Charm to ensure that changes to her version would be reflected on his. ØRight. I shall prepare a tidy little report with some recommendations to ensure Healer Grangerís continued safety and well-being,Æ said Draco, scribbling out a few notes. ØIím also going to see what I can do to reassure Shacklebolt that youíre not going to be murdered tomorrow, and that I neednít be your minder on a daily basis.Æ ØA relief for all parties,Æ said Granger. ØWatch for my owl in a few days. Also, please stop giving him treacle tart, it makes him unruly.Æ ØUnderstood,Æ said Granger, looking only slightly abashed. ØIs the test over, then?Æ ØYes.Æ ØFinally,Æ said Granger. Then, because she was a normal, well-adjusted individual, she sat down at her desk to work some more. Draco saw that he had, for all intents and purposes, ceased to exist, and decided to show himself out without further ceremony. ØMind the tile just in front of the door Å Quicksand Curse,Æ said Granger absently. ØIt was to catch the baddies on the way out.Æ ØSaw it, Granger.Æ ØOf course you did.Æ ~ A few exchanges with Shacklebolt ensued, during which Draco outlined his Plan and convinced the Minister that it was the correct approach, and that, moreover, no other approach would do because the Principal would be too uncooperative. Draco studied Grangerís schedule in quiet moments, puzzling over the asterisk Øholidays.Æ His first thought was that the days were a personal indicator of some private thing. They were too scattered to be a reminder for her period. The pattern wasnít lunar, either Å good to know Granger wasnít a secret werewolf. Dates for some romantic entanglement, perhaps? Was that why she hadnít marked down details? Was he looking at Grangerís sex schedule? Would she really take entire days off? Draco felt that he ought to shake the hand of the man responsible. He also surreptitiously checked the off-day requests book at the Auror Office, and neither the Weaselís nor Potheadís upcoming holidays coincided. The mystery endured. Draco spent a few days tinkering with the key element of his Plan. And by åtinkering,í we do mean, of course, mucking about with ancient magicks best left untouched. ~ ØRecommendations,Æ said Draco, slapping a roll of parchment onto Grangerís desk. ØFairly standard stuff for fairly obvious vulnerabilities. Iíve run them past Shacklebolt. Heís agreed to withdraw the protection request if you comply with them.Æ Granger unrolled the parchment and found that it reached the floor. She gave him a slow blink. ØAnything youíd like to draw my particular attention to, in the interest of saving time?Æ ØYes,Æ said Draco. ØItem fifty-six.Æ Granger ran down the list to the line in question. ØThe Principal must agree to wear the Ring at all times, until completion of the Project.Æ ØThatís the one,Æ said Draco. ØWhat ring?Æ asked Granger. ØThis one,Æ said Draco, tossing a ring towards her. The small silver band landed on the parchment, spun once, and was still. ØI donít care to train you on Imperius and Veritaserum resistance, or personal protection magicks, or Advanced Occlumency, or drill you on physical self-defence (gods forbid; you look like your punches might concuss a gnat, at best) Å and nor, I think, do you want to endure these things.Æ ØCorrect,Æ said Granger, her suspicious look moving from the ring to Draco. ØNor do I want to stand sentry at your door like some glorified bodyguard, waiting for whatever Shacklebolt expects to happen, to happen.Æ ØYes,Æ said Granger with enthusiasm. ØContinue.Æ ØSo I presented Shacklebolt with this option, which will allow me to Å in essence Å be alerted if anything were to happen to you, and Apparate to you instantly. I can find better uses for my time, and you can carry on with your Å distressingly full, by the way Å schedule, unimpeded.Æ Draco waited to be praised for the simple elegance of this brilliant solution. Instead, Granger poked the ring with her wand. ØIt isnít going to kill you,Æ said Draco. Granger met his eyes seriously. ØMy dataset is, admittedly, rather small, but I saw the aftermath of the last piece of jewellery that Draco Malfoy handed out, and it was Å alarming. Youíll have to forgive me if I donít immediately put this on. Iíd like to analyse it.Æ Ah, yes. The Katie Bell Incident. If Draco had any feelings, they wouldíve been a little hurt, probably, by this display of mistrust stemming from the actions of an idiot boy being manipulated by the Darkest wizard of the century, a decade and a half ago. But he didn't, so the point was moot. ØIím happy to see that you've got some self-preservation instincts,Æ said Draco. He swept his hand towards the ring. ØAnalyse away.Æ Granger cast a few revelation spells, which set the ring aglow with slow-rotating, translucent spellwork. ØSo Å whatís all this?Æ ØTelling you would rather spoil the fun, wouldnít it? You tell me,Æ said Draco. And with that, he settled back into his chair into a relaxed pose. Now it was his turn to watch her unpuzzle a thing. She flicked through the spells with some adeptness, quickly picking out the more critical ones. Draco supposed that diagnostic magic would come easily to her as a Healer. She listed her findings. ØA Locator Charm, miscellaneous protective runes (thoughtful, thank you), a distress beacon, heart rate monitoring...Æ Now her lips quirked. ØWhatís funny?Æ asked Draco. ØYouíve invented a Wizarding FitBit.Æ ØI beg your pardon?Æ Unless he was misunderstanding, Granger was suggesting that his exceptional creation was a knock-off of a Muggle thing? What? ØNever mind. Whatís this unfinished mess here?Æ asked Granger, holding her wand-point to a ghostly green knot of Arithmantic calculations. Draco felt his nostrils pinch: that unfinished mess was the result of many frustrating hours of work. ØI havenít got round to finishing that yet.Æ ØWhat was it meant to be?Æ ØPortkey. For moments when you couldnít Apparate, or if you were trapped in an Anti-Apparition Ward. I havenít worked out the calculations.Æ Granger looked mildly impressed. Draco supposed that she was surrounded by the nationís greatest magical brains on the daily, and that he ought to be pleased that she was mildly impressed by a mere Aurorís paltry creation. ØAn on-demand Portkey would be something,Æ said Granger. ØPortus is a pain in the arse of an enchantment,Æ said Draco, trying to sound resigned, rather than sullen. ØHave you ever thought of making more of these rings? You could monetise these easily,Æ said Granger, holding the ring aloft. ØDo I look like I need money?Æ asked Draco. Granger levelled a stare at him. Her back straightened. They had been dangerously close to lapsing into a civil conversation and she seemed to have forgotten who she was talking to. She sniffed in lieu of responding. ØAnyway, I canít mass-produce the ring.Æ ØRight.Æ Granger was weighing the ring in the palm of her hand. ØBecause this isnít just some trinket you put a few neat charms on.Æ ØNo.Æ ØThis is an Artefact.Æ ØIndeed.Æ ØA family heirloom, if I were to hazard a guess.Æ ØYes.Æ Of course sheíd spotted the concealment charm that made the ring look like a plain silver band. Now she tapped her wand to reveal the ringís true appearance Å an ornate silver ouroboros, ever eating its own tail. And on the inside, the family motto: Sanctimonia Vincet Semper. Purity will always conquer. ØYouíre certain this ring wonít immediately attempt to amputate my finger? Iím not Pure, after all,Æ said Granger. Draco felt that the temperature in Grangerís office had dropped rather suddenly. ØDid you see a sign of Dark magic?Æ asked Draco. Too quickly Å heíd sounded defensive. Blast. ØIf there was Dark magic, itís gone now,Æ said Granger. She tapped the ring again, reverting it back to the plain silver band. She looked thoughtful. ØIíll need some time to go through this extremely comprehensive list of recommendations,Æ she said at length. ØTake the time you need,Æ said Draco. ØBut know that the alternative is Shacklebolt setting up a camp bed for me, for overnights in your laboratory.Æ She eyed him, then seemed to decide that he must be joking. ØIíll need to think about item fifty-six in particular. Do you want the ring back in the meantime?Æ ØKeep it,Æ said Draco. ØHave your friends analyse it Å isnít one of the Weasley brothers meant to be good at that stuff? Å and when youíve quite settled any doubts, owl me, and we can get on with our lives.Æ Granger perked up, as though getting on with her life without a Draco-shaped barnacle attached to her was the kindest hope he couldíve offered her. ØI will,Æ she said. Two of her students, kitted up in their strange white cloaks and goggles, knocked at the door, excited to share some new development with dear Professor Granger. Draco rose to leave as Granger donned her own white coat to join the students in the laboratory. There was an awkward, conflicted look on her face. Draco, never one to make things easy, merely raised an eyebrow at her. ØI suppose I want to say thank you. For working through this as you are. I havenít exactly been pulling my weight trying to find a solution to Shackleboltís request. The ring is a good idea.Æ ØI think youíre more than pulling your weight elsewhere,Æ said Draco. He left; she muttered something that mightíve been a goodbye. Chapter3: House Call by GeniusInventor Dracoís eagle owl was given a decent workout in the coming days as Draco and Granger negotiated back and forth on a few of the recommendations that heíd made. She suggested that some of the measures were positively draconian (Øpun intended; do forgive meÆ) and tried to push back on them, with an especial focus on the home visit for personalised warding. Eventually, Draco pulled out his most severe quill, and composed the following: Granger Å Shackleboltís orders on the warding of the Granger domicile arenít up for negotiation. Do let me know when would be convenient to come by this week for the warding. If you donít, I shall drop by at an inconvenient time by default. - D (for Draconian) Malfoy Å Unsure if you heard my sigh of exasperation from London, so I am recording its occurrence here for your information. I am more than capable of improving the warding on my own property, or of hiring a warding firm. But, if Shacklebolt is insisting on your particular expertise, so be it. A modest sort of cottage in Cambridgeshire, as it happened. Three bedrooms, at Dracoís best guess. Granger stood at the gate. As he approached from his Apparition point, she waved her wand to allow him to pass whatever preliminary wards she had set up. ØWhatís wrong with your face?Æ she asked as Draco neared the gate. Always to the point, was Granger. ØBludger,Æ said Draco. ØOh. It looks bad.Æ (It probably did, too; Zabini had a mean swing.) As he neared the gate, Draco saw Granger scrutinising the injury with a well-practised eye. She vacillated for a moment, then, apparently unable to resist Do-Gooding, blurted out, ØDo you want me to have a look at it?Æ ØNo. Iíve already put a salve on,Æ said Draco, brushing his fingers against his slowly bruising jaw. ØThatís going to make a lovely hematoma.Æ ØIím fine. I came here to ward your house, not for a consult.Æ Grangerís mouth pressed into a thin line. Now he felt like some kind of vampire angling for an invitation over the threshold. ØCome in, then,Æ said Granger, a little snappish, pulling the gate open. Draco saw that she was dressed in another version of the white coat, this time accessorised with a dangling contraption wrapped around her neck. ØItís a temporary disaster.Æ She gestured to a Muggle machine in the centre of it all, connected to a flattish version of a computer. ØDigitising?Æ ØYes. Preserving magical knowledge through Muggle means, as Iím growing tired of lugging enormous books about, of finding irreparably damaged or lost material because some idiot spilled tea on a page twenty years ago, and of having to search for things through ancient record cards like itís 1855. Itís a pet project for my rarest volumes. There mightíve been something magical slowly brewing in a cauldron at the hearth, but she swept him past it. ØConservatory?Æ asked Draco as they moved to the next room. Granger looked at him as though he had just confirmed what a posh twat he was. ØA conservatory? This isn't Ascott House. The letting agent called it a sun-room.Æ That seemed an optimistic appellation to Draco, who watched January sleet begin to drizzle against the glass ceiling with scepticism. Then an odd, orange, squash-faced creature appeared, and wound its way around Grangerís ankles. In another moment of wild optimism, Granger referred to it as a cat. ØWhatís wrong with your cat?Æ asked Draco, bending over to regard the creature with concern. ØNothingís wrong with him,Æ said Granger. Both she and the creature looked at Draco with great offence. ØHeís part Kneazle, and very intelligent. Arenít you, my darling? My sweetums? My angel boy?Æ As its ears were massaged by Granger, the cat regarded Draco with an expression of utmost disdain. Then it decided that it had had enough of Grangerís attention and turned to leave, its absurd tail held high, so that Draco got a full view of its bumhole. ØCharming,Æ said Draco. The tour continued to the cramped upstairs space. Three small bedrooms, as Draco had guessed, with predictable points of entry that he would have to ward. The first bedroom appeared to be used as a study. Draco noted a kind of plinth in the middle of the space. On it rested a grimoire, very old and damaged, surrounded by the glow of stasis charms. Granger saw what had caught his attention. ØA tragedy. Donít ask me about it or I shall cry.Æ Draco did not wish to deal with bookish snivelling and did not pursue the subject, but he made a mental note of the object, for future prying. The second bedroom was quite bare, save for a long mat on the floor, candles, and a cluster of orchids. What ritual was Granger preparing to cast here? He tried to make sense of the candle arrangement, but it didnít match the geometry of anything he recognised. Finally, they came to Grangerís bedroom, which she permitted him a glance into with evident unease. Draco couldnít find a civil way to say, stop bloody fidgeting, I only need to see how the baddies might try to kidnap you; Iím not here to rifle through your knickers, so he said nothing. An obnoxious jingle began to play somewhere in Grangerís vicinity. She pulled a palm-sized Muggle thingie out of her pocket and spoke into it. From what Draco understood, she was being summoned to the surgery by the means of this device. She confirmed this by rushing past Draco to the stairs. ØI have to go. I think youíve seen enough to orient yourself Å please set the wards to let Crookshanks in and out. He likes to roam. Iíll be back in a few hours.Æ ØCrookshanks?Æ called Draco as Granger tripped down the stairs. ØThe cat!Æ said Granger. She disappeared outside, but instead of the crack of Disapparition, Draco heard the sound of a motor. Granger was driving. A Muggle car. The absolute weirdo. Or perhaps not, he thought upon reflection, as he made his way back to the garden. If she was going to a Muggle surgery, sheíd have to show up by Muggle means; an instantaneous Apparition at the door would raise questions. As he pondered the overfilled dual life that Granger led, Draco began to ward. ~ After about two hours of work, Draco pronounced himself satisfied. The wards would need to be recast every week or so, but no one would be able to waltz in sans Grangerís permission. Points of entry and egress were all reinforced with the Aurorís standard kit and a few of Dracoís own inventions; underground approaches would be flummoxed by a robust Depellens Penetrationem and aerial attacks would be rebuffed by a Caeli Praesidium. The usual assortment of intruder alarms was scattered about. Frankly, for a witch of Grangerís relative fame, whose two closest chums were now Aurors, her protection measures had been paltry. But then, it was peacetime, and she was a scholar, now Å not a child chasing Dark Objects to murder an evil wizard seven times over. The half-Kneazle stared balefully at Draco through the sleet from the shelter of the stoop. Draco added the creatureís magical signature to the wards and told him so. The creature blinked at him. Draco was unnerved. Just as the rain began to lessen, a car made its way up the drive and behind the cottage. Granger rounded the corner a moment later. ØStill here, are you?Æ ØI've just finished,Æ panted Draco. Warding was a magically exhausting task. The half-Kneazle was given a great many kisses on his ugly head as Draco stood by and tried not to look wet and sweaty. And where was his thank you, if you please? art by nikitajobson ØI shall have to ward your car,Æ said Draco. ØIf you use it to get around a lot. And the Muggle surgery, if youíre there regularly.Æ Granger frowned at him. ØMy car is brand new. You canít ward it; youíll muck something up.Æ In the face of Dracoís confused offence, she added, ØCars have electrical components in them, now. Maybe they didnít when you were in Muggle Studies.Æ This was said as though Draco was approximately 120 years old and had last taken Muggle Studies when cars were called horseless carriages. ØIíll bung a Sneakoscope into the glovebox,Æ said Granger. For someone so clever, she certainly was an idiot. ØExcellent,Æ said Draco. ØThatíll definitely ward off a Bombarda Maxima from twenty metres out. Iíll be able to tell Shacklebolt that weíd taken all necessary measures to protect you, when we pull your charred remains out of the wreckage.Æ The violent imagery was successful. Granger gave in. ØFine. You can ward it. But do try to stay away from the Å the centre bits, with all the buttons. Next to the steering wheel.Æ Dracoís moment of triumph was ruined by a long and echoing hungry growl, unmistakably from his stomach (unfortunate Å he was prepared to blame the cat). There was a pause. Grangerís eyes flitted to Dracoís midriff. She appeared to be struggling between her natural feelings for him and her manners. Then, finally: ØYou must be famished. Do you want to come in? Iíve a few snacks. We can go over the recommendations Å and the ring.Æ Why yes, Draco was starving. Two hours of warding really did take it out of a man. However: there was a five course meal waiting for him at the Manor. However (bis): he wanted to wrap up this affair and have his next communication with Granger be regarding the return of the ring, however-many months hence. ØAll right,Æ said Draco. Draco popped into the loo to refresh himself, which mainly involved Scourgifying his armpits (the height of class; mother would be proud) and attempting some drying charms on his robes. His hair he deemed a lost cause tonight. Not that he had anyone to impress here. And besides, in this cottage, with Granger the Human Anemone and her orange toilet brush of a familiar, his hair still easily won best-in-show. His entire look was complemented by the magnificent bruise starting to develop along his jaw. He pressed more of the salve into it, annoyed that Granger had been right about how bad it was going to get. He trudged to the kitchen, where Granger had the scroll of recommendations and the ring set out upon the kitchen table. She removed her white coat and stuffed it into a Muggle machine at the end of the worktop (judging by the folded piles around it, a washing apparatus). Another long-sleeved top underneath Å who knew that Granger had such an aversion to exposing her elbows? The kitchen table was pushed into a corner. Draco therefore took a chair next to Granger. From this vantage Å far closer than heíd been to her at any point previously Å he noted that she was in possession of a decent pair of tits. However, Granger chose that moment to unravel the scroll, now liberally scribbled with question marks and counter-suggestions, and Draco was unable to feel attracted because he was being suffocated by waves of Swot. ØSome of my principal concerns,Æ said Granger, nodding her chin towards the parchment, which promised a long and arduous evening of argument. ØBut first, letís eat something.Æ She rummaged about in a pathetically empty cupboard and popped some options onto the table. As far as Draco was concerned, the principal article of diet was cat hair. He pulled a few orangey strands from his mouth as the cat (damn the creature) wound its way around his chairís legs, looking smugly at him. Granger did him the courtesy of looking abashed when she noticed. ØSorry!Æ She waved her wand in Dracoís direction, Vanishing most of the fur. ØIt does get everywhere. Sometimes I think he can actually will it into existence Å into unspeakable places.Æ Draco, pulling at a hair, said ØPthtÆ in response, but what he really wanted to say was, If I find orange fur on my balls tonight, I shall skin that animal with my bare hands. Granger tore open a package of something and passed it to him. ØWhat is this?Æ asked Draco, holding up one of the things. ØCheesy Wotsits.Æ Which explained everything, obviously. For her part, Granger ate tuna, directly from the tin. ØGrim, Granger,Æ said Draco. ØItís protein,Æ said Granger. She looked at the mediocre spread that Draco was scowling at and got a bit defensive. ØI havenít had time to go to the shops.Æ ØWhy donít you send a house-elfÅÆ As the words came out of his mouth, Draco cut himself off, but it was too late. Granger was looking at him like heíd just confirmed, for the second time that evening, what an over-privileged wanker he was. She rose, tight-jawed, to make tea. It seemed an excuse to get away from his immediate vicinity. But, whatever Å Draco wasnít here to make friends. Granger banged about with the kettle. She looked like she was holding back a certain quantity of Draco-oriented vitriol. He surreptitiously checked his pockets. He did have a bezoar on him, in case his tea had any special additives courtesy of the House-Elf Vigilante. Granger set their mugs on the table with rather more firmness than was necessary. There was no immediate evidence of poison. She had found a packet of biscuits to go with the tea. Draco ate two thirds of it like a famished thing, and if they were poisoned, then so be it. Then Granger straightened out the parchment, seemed to Å with an effort Å compartmentalise her feelings about Draco the Wanker, and became all business. She queried him on the recommendations as though he were an apprentice Auror who had submitted this for review, and ought to be grateful for the feedback. And so they argued through the list: on item 14, whether he would add caretaking staff to the laboratoryís wards (he conceded); on item 26, whether she really needed to give him notice when she was leaving town (yes) and if so, how much notice (24 hours); on item 33, what constituted a åpublic eventí (over 40 people); on item 34, why did she have to advise him of her attendance at Muggle ones? (because he said so); could he not make her home Unplottable, she had Muggle friends who might want to visit? (no); and so on and so forth until they came to item 56. Granger refilled their tea and pulled out another packet of biscuits, given that Draco had stress-eaten the entirety of the first one. ØSo. The ring,Æ said Granger. ØThe ring,Æ repeated Draco. The crux of the thing Å the object that meant he could continue his life in happy, Granger-less freedom and still satisfy the Minister and Tonks. ØIíve had it looked at by a few experts. Where is your FitZit now? He sipped his tea self-righteously instead. ØIíve also had a chat with Tonks,Æ continued Granger. ØShe probably told you how much she likes the idea, too. Means youíll be able to take on other assignments while monitoring me at a distance. So Å all told, glowing reviews all around, with minimal cons, and I am willing to proceed. I do have a question for you, however.Æ ØYes?Æ said Draco, even though he had a fair guess about the question. In fact, he was surprised she hadnít asked earlier. ØHow does the information tracked by the ring come to you?Æ Draco held up his hand and waved his wand at it, cancelling the Notice-Me-Not Charm there. ØAh,Æ said Granger, as the silver ring on Dracoís finger came into view. Her gaze flicked from Dracoís ring to the one on the table. Then, after some private deliberations in that overlarge brain of hers, she said, quite intelligently in Dracoís opinion: ØI shanít ask more questions about the original use of these things. I feel that further details might put me off the whole affair.Æ ØGood shout,Æ said Draco. Because, yes, these ancient rings had long been worn by married couples in the Malfoy family. His mother had removed hers many years ago, following Lucius Malfoyís death in Azkaban Å the ringís silence was a constant reminder of the loss and she could no longer bear to wear it. Draco had modified the rings so that there would only be one-way communication between Grangerís and his. He certainly didnít need her being alerted every time his heart rate spiked when he was having a morning wank, thanks. Living on in happy ignorance of these thoughts, Granger asked, ØIs anything special required or do I just Å just put it on?Æ ØIíll do it,Æ said Draco. ØIt needs to be done by the person wearing the ringís Å er, mate.Æ He tried to be gruff and businesslike about it, but there are very few things in the world as un.businesslike as a man putting a ring on a womanís finger, and it was awkward despite his best attempts. He wondered if Granger found it as awkward as he did. She was studying the kitchen wallpaper, a tint of pink high on her cheeks. Her hand was small in his, and delicate. The ring went on effortlessly. He felt a kind of enlivening in the ring on his own hand Å it had someone to talk to, now. ØThe distress beacon is activated by twisting it three times around your finger,Æ said Draco to break the silence. ØDo that and Iíll Apparate to you immediately.Æ Granger snapped away from her fascination with the wallpaper. ØAll right.Æ ØDo reserve it for critical situations, Granger Å not because youíve found tea spilled on a book.Æ ØIím very much hoping Iíll never have to use it at all.Æ She looked at the ring glinting on her hand. ØAt least the thing didnít immediately try to kill me.Æ ØDonít get too comfortable. It could be playing a long game.Æ Draco tapped the scroll they had discussed ad nauseum, integrating the scribbled results of their back-and-forth into a clean version. Then he created a duplicate for her. ØNow that weíve finalised this, you must stick with it. Weíve established a duty of care and Iíd rather not be dragged in front of the Wizengamot for professional negligence resulting in the death of the great Hermione Granger.Æ ØI understand,Æ came the great Hermione Grangerís serious reply. ØGood. Now, before I go, one last thing.Æ Draco dug a hand into his pocket. ØMy owlís lost half a pound of weight since weíve been communicating, so IÅÆ ØFeed him more treacle tart,Æ interjected Granger. Her cat was on her lap, finishing the tin of tuna. ØÅIíve decided to give in to the trend and buy these things,Æ finished Draco. He placed a pair of Weasleysí Jabbering Jotters on the table. ØYouíve probably heard of them Å all the rage amongst the younger generation. Owls arenít quite the thing anymore. Not immediate enough.Æ In Dracoís opinion, a sad end to a long Wizarding tradition. One couldnít write a strongly worded letter on a Jabbering Jotter Å one simply couldnít. ØI am familiar with those,Æ said Granger. She was very obviously holding back a smile. Draco weighed the pros and cons of asking for the reason behind the smile. He decided against: between her and the cat, the levels of smugness in the room would soon asphyxiate him. ØSo you know how they work?Æ asked Draco, passing her the small magical notepad. ØO, yes,Æ said Granger, accepting the object. ØThank you. I feel bad about your owl.Æ ØHeíll recover and soon grow fat from lack of exercise.Æ His job here done, Draco rose with a general mutter of thanks for the tea. Granger replied with some inaudible words of gratitude for the warding. The cat attempted to trip him and break his neck on his way out of the kitchen. Draco decided that that was a suitable end to an unpleasant evening. Chapter4:Imbolc In his handful of years working with Potter and Weasley, Draco had developed a cool, professional kind of rapport with them, which Weasley demonstrated the next morning by calling, ØOi! Dickhead!Æ and hanging over Dracoís cubicle wall like a disjointed ginger muppet. ØWhat do you want, Weasel?Æ ØWe heard Hermioneís been assigned Auror protection Å and that the blokeís a tosser,Æ said Weasley. ØWas that her description, or yours?Æ Potter, whose disastrous hair and vivid green eyes now popped up over the cubicle wall, said, ØOurs. She says youíve been quite professional. We know the truth.Æ ØLucky bugger,Æ said Weasley. ØHow come Tonks gives us the vampires, and you the Hermione-minding? You donít even like her.Æ ØI understand that it was a question of competence,Æ said Draco. ØTonks said she needed to assign the finest Auror to protect the finest mind in the UKÅÆ Weasley scoffed; Potter laughed. ØÅAnd the nuisance Aurors to deal with the nuisance vampires,Æ finished Draco. ØI said no such thing,Æ said Tonks, waddling by in the form of a short, overweight man. ØShouldnít you all be off working, you blatherskites? Youíre all nuisance Aurors, as far as Iím concerned.Æ Potter and Weasley chortled. Draco was offended. ØWhatís Hermione working on, anyway, thatís got old Shack so worked up?Æ asked Weasley. ØShe wonít tell us.Æ ØThat informationís on a need-to-know basis,Æ said Draco, tapping his nose. He hadnít a clue either, but winding up the Nuisance Duo was always a good time. The two of them looked suitably annoyed that Draco seemed to know something they didnít. ØWork!Æ shouted Tonks from her office. ØYes, boss,Æ replied Weasley. ØWord to the wise, Malfoy,Æ said Potter as they left. ØDonít insult Hermioneís cat.Æ ØToo late,Æ said Draco. ~ Two weeks passed, during which all was quiet on the Granger front. Her ring had been calibrated to alert Draco to extreme physiological or emotional shifts that might indicate immediate danger: significant spikes of fear, panic, pain, or an unusually high heart rate. In general, Granger seemed to be miraculously even-tempered. There was one day when Dracoís ring tingled at him throughout the morning, signalling that Grangerís pulse was elevated at various points Å but not quite at the threshold signalling a wild panic. He set it out of his mind and joined Goggin and a few junior Aurors for a hand-to-hand combat session. Tonks insisted that her Aurors not only maintained their Duelling expertise through rigorous practice, but also their abilities as physical fighters. Many had moaned about having to learn to fight like Muggles. Tonks had set them straight. A disarmed Auror with hand-to-hand training could still outmanoeuvre, disarm, or maim an opponent, if he kept his wits about him. A wandless Auror without those things was a very dead Auror. Grangerís elevated pulse Å the fourth such incident that morning Å interrupted Dracoís spar. His momentary distraction earned him a solid uppercut from Goggin. He called for a pause, clutching at his jaw, and used the Jotter to send Granger an annoyed message, consisting solely of punctuation: ??? She responded with a brief note: Losing a patient. Draco didnít respond, mostly because he didnít know what to say, but also because Goggin had decided that the break was over and was now attempting to concuss him. A while later he received the following missive from Granger: By the way Å going out of town tomorrow morning, just for the day. I know our agreement said 24 hours notice for departures and this is more like 12. Sorry, itís been hellish. Where? was Dracoís response. Somerset, was Grangerís. Why? Holiday. One of those asterisk holidays? Granger didnít respond. So, yes. That evening, while Draco was at dinner, his ring signalled pain. But it wasnít physical pain. It was the heart-pain of grief, from somewhere in Cambridgeshire. The poignancy of the feeling surprised him. The sincerity of it. Granger truly was a Do-Gooder to the core. He supposed that she had got home and was giving way to the loss of her patient. ØDraco? Is everything all right?Æ Draco found himself being observed by the thoughtful blue eyes of Narcissa Malfoy. He realised heíd stopped eating when the ghostly grief had suffused his senses. ØI'm fine,Æ said Draco. ØJust thinking about work.Æ Draco hadnít told his mother that heíd requisitioned the Malfoy rings. He was certain that she wouldnít agree with his repurposing, nor with his choice of recipient. He cast about for a safe subject for discussion and remarked on the nicer-than-usual flower arrangement in the centre of the table. Floristry was one of his mother's hobbies. ØDo you like it?Æ asked his mother, leaning over to touch a few delicate petals. She seemed in a pensive mood. ØItís Imbolc tomorrow.Æ ØImbolc?Æ The word was vaguely familiar to Draco Å some Pagan festival or other. Narcissa pulled up an already perfectly placed blossom and replaced it even more perfectly in the bouquet. ØYes Å it marks the end of winter. Your grandmother used to observe those old traditions when I was a little girl. The house would be decorated with snowdrops and daffodils on every surface, weíd have a feast Å and weíd feel hopeful, knowing that spring was finally on its way.Æ Draco made some polite reply. His mother watched him eat, her own hands folded onto her lap. She had something else to say. ØWhat is it?Æ asked Draco. ØAre you going to be home tomorrow? His mother had grown rather less subtle about her matchmaking since heíd turned thirty. Unfortunately for Narcissa (and the eligible young ladies), Dracoís own interest in anything longer term than dirty weekend escapades in Paris was nil. Heíd done the longer term thing once Å a two-year engagement to Astoria Å and it had been sufficient to confirm that, no matter how Pure-blooded and well-bred the witch, he wasnít ready for marriage. Grangerís note earlier that day offered a convenient lifeline. Draco grimaced and said, ØIíll be working. Business in Somerset tomorrow.Æ Granger herself didnít know sheíd have company, but too bad for her. Heíd call it a spot check. Her safety against threats Å real or imagined by Shacklebolt Å was his highest priority, after all. Narcissa seemed unsurprised at the ready excuse. ØA pity. Next time, then.Æ Dinner concluded. Draco retreated to his chambers, where he took a long bath and nursed his training wounds. His Jotter buzzed. He summoned it to find a note from Granger, a delayed response to his earlier question. Yes, one of the asterisk holidays. A spot of sightseeing. Iíll turn the ring if I need you. That last sentence was Granger-speak for ØI donít need you, do not come, you are not invited.Æ No doubt she would get shirty when he turned up. The thought elicited an unexpected tingle of amusement. Then something that had been percolating in the back of Dracoís mind since dinner clicked into place. He got out of the tub, dried himself off with a few waves of his wand, and summoned Grangerís schedule. Tomorrow was Å what had his mother said? Imbolc? And that coincided with one of Grangerís asterisks. Were there other such interesting coincidences? He ran through the rest of the dates. The next asterisk was a weekend in late March. Then one at the beginning of May. Then June. Then early August. Abuzz with anticipatory triumph, Draco descended to the Manor's library, where he pulled out a few volumes on Celtic and Germanic Pagan traditions. He was right. Grangerís dates matched the old calendars. Draco rolled the old words out on his tongue. Imbolc. Ostara. Beltane. Litha. Lughnasadh. Mabon. Samhain. What was Granger up to? Draco was officially intrigued. ~ Draco gave Granger the morning to set out on her Somerset adventure before he joined her. That permitted him an exquisite lie-in, some invigorating flying in the February wind, and the opportunity for a luxurious brunch. When he arrived, Draco understood why. Granger had been moving at a rather rapid pace, given that she was belting down a country lane in her car. Granger shrieked as Draco materialised in the seat beside her. His head was in the passenger footwell and his boots were, by the feel of it, in Grangerís face. It was altogether not his most graceful arrival. Granger swerved onto a verge and brought the car to a halt. Draco turned himself the right way up with difficulty as a barrage of questions came his way, including what the hell he thought he was doing, who did he think he was, how dare he, and whether or not he was actually insane? Grangerís voice could be quite shrill. Penetrating, really. ØYou just Apparated to a moving target! Have you completely lost the plot? ØWhy are you driving?Æ ØBecause you told me Apparition and Floo were traceable.Æ ØWho cares if theyíre traceable? Youíre allowed to be on holiday. ØInteresting.Æ Granger said nothing. Her displeasure at his presence continued and she was not hiding it. It mattered little to Draco Å a rainy drive through the English countryside with an angry Granger was a refreshing change from the usual too-small sandwiches and coquettish fortune-hunters. Honestly, the winding drive, the Austrian music, the fuming witch Å it was absurd, it was amusing, it was fun. Draco reached to press another button on the carís central panel, out of curiosity.
