She got out of the car. ØIím going for a walk. You may wait in the car.Æ May he? So generous. Draco Å after a brief struggle with the handle Å let himself out of the vehicle. He withheld groans as he stamped some feeling back into his legs. Granger observed his emergence from the Mini with her hands on her hips. He felt her observing his choice of clothing (his Auror robes over his perennial suit) and footwear (perfectly functional dragonhide boots). She mustíve concluded that it would have to do Å or otherwise, that it wouldnít do, and would put him in peril, and that that was perfect. At any rate, she turned around and began to walk towards the woods. Draco saw her cast some rain-repelling and warming charms on herself. He imitated her; it seemed a good idea. As they entered the Mendip Way, Draco cast a few detection spells, looking for evidence of other beings, magical or Muggle. However, it seemed that only he and Granger were mad enough to go for a ramble on a day like this. Save some roe deer in a nearby clearing, they were alone. Satisfied that no madmen were about to vault out and attack Granger, Draco caught up to her in a few long strides. It quickly became obvious that this wasnít just a walk for Grangerís health. She was looking for something. Or several somethings. She peered into the underbrush, touched the trunks of trees, gently caught the fronds of ferns in her palm and studied them. She took nothing, however, and so quashed any theories about ingredient gathering that Draco might have been entertaining. They progressed in this manner for a good half hour, marked by a pause to refresh their fading Impervius charms. Finally, Granger stopped, and pulled out a list. Draco unashamedly peeked over her shoulder. Singing Sedge Greater Bladderwort Royal Fern Ophioglossun vulgatum Wood-sorrel Mellifluous Honewort Helianthemum apenninum Helianthemum nummularium Spiny Restharrow Tassel Moss Granger used her wand to cross out the majority of the list. Only the Tassel Moss remained. ØWhatís Tassel Moss?Æ asked Draco. Granger flinched away from him. Apparently, sheíd been so much in her own head that sheíd quite forgotten that Draco was there, much less noticed that he was skulking over her shoulder. Her hand flew to her fast-beating heart (Draco felt faint echoes of it through the ring). He expected to be told off. However, her bad mood seemed to have been replaced by tentative excitement related to this list. ØOne of the rarer mosses in this part of England,Æ said Granger. ØWhy are you looking for it?Æ Granger began to walk again, her attention focused, this time, on dead logs, old stumps, and other likely habitats. ØBecause it will confirm that Iím in the right place.Æ ØThe right place for what?Æ Granger waved the question away. ØIím merely confirming a theory.Æ ØWhat theory?Æ (Draco, too, could be relentless.) ØSomething related to my project,Æ said Granger with irritating ambiguity. ØWhatís moss got to do with your Chimaera cells, or whatever?Æ ØNothing Å at least, not directly.Æ She turned to look at him through the rain, as though to gauge what was worth telling him. ØIím retracing the steps of an old, long-forgotten witch whose work included Å amongst many things Å descriptions of certain sacred sites in the British Isles.Æ ØSo, the Vale of Avalon?Æ ØSpecifically, Glastonburyís wells. Or at least, thatís my educated guess. Not much of her work is still extant today. All we have are fragments. She tended to wax lyrically on flora, which helps me narrow down possible locations by cross-referencing the rarer plants. Of course, she was writing hundreds of years ago, so things may have changed. But few places on the island will support both Singing Sedge and Mellifluous Honewart. They typically thrive in radically different ecosystems, as you no doubt knowèÆ No, Draco didnít know Å in fact, heíd never even heard of these plants Å but he nodded instead of admitting it. When Draco next looked up, for a heart-stopping moment, Granger had disappeared Å he snatched his wand Å then he saw her backside poking over the edge of the path. She was on her hands and knees, examining a rather wet ditch. Whatever had caught her eye, it wasnít what she was looking for. She regained her feet. She didnít look disappointed, however Å she looked determined. And muddy. ØTassel Moss looks as youíd imagine,Æ said Granger. ØTiny tassels across the top. Itís the sporangia Å unusually big in the genus. They turn pink in the summer Å of course, weíre a little too early for that.Æ Was this woman a genius at Herbology, on top of everything else? Draco wondered how much of Potter and Weasleyís limited scholarly success was due to absorbing her knowledge by intellectual osmosis. She was, frankly, overwhelming. Granger carried on along the path, squatting down occasionally to observe things. It was altogether rather a peaceful ramble, with the charms keeping him dry, the sound of the rain and the occasional brave songbird, and verbalisations from Granger telling off various mosses because they werenít the right one. For the first time since heíd taken the Granger casefile from Tonksí hands, Draco felt glad of the decision. This was certainly more pleasant than most of his work as an Auror Å fewer hexes and eviscerations coming his way, for a start. And, bonus, it got him out of tea with the ladies, and promised many more opportunities to do so. That set would be tut-tutting at Granger over their teacups Å Granger with her hat askew, her face smeared with dirt, clambering about in ditches instead of finding herself a rich husband. But she was apparently doing something great for wizardkind, and what, pray, had they achieved? ØI think I found it!Æ called Granger. Draco pushed through some brambles to be, once again, presented with a view of Grangerís bum. Familiarity breeds fondness Å he was rather developing an appreciation for it. For reasons known only to herself, Granger had all but pressed her face into a patch of moss and was breathing deeply into it. ØGranger, whatÅÆ ØItís meant to smell like candy-floss. And it does!Æ said Granger, rising with a leap. There was dirt on the tip of her nose. In the shadows of the great oaks around them, her dark eyes shone with excitement. A curl of hair clung damply to her lip. Her cheeks were pinched pink by the February wind. Her smile flashed at him, a brief, rare thing. Draco realised with a shock that Granger was pretty. She clapped her hands together and squealed at the clump of moss, as though it was a treasure worth thousands upon thousands of Galleons. Before Draco could process his realisation, a hoarse scream echoed from some distant corner of the woods. To his amusement, Granger leapt to his side immediately, her wand raised. The queer screaming continued. When Granger saw that he hadnít reacted and didnít seem alarmed, she asked, ØWhat is that horrid racket?Æ ØThatís a fox,Æ said Draco. ØOh.Æ ØSome slag of a vixenís asking to get her back blown out.Æ ØI see,Æ said Granger. Another scream. Draco wanted to laugh Å Grangerís expression had gone rather prim. She pulled out her list of plants and crossed the final line out. ØThis is an excellent development. The moss, I mean, not the slaggy fox. Letís go back to the car.Æ ØThatís it?Æ asked Draco. It had seemed rather easy. ØOh, no,Æ said Granger. ØIf only. I have about three thousand other things to do before thatís it.Æ Knowing her, that was probably not an exaggeration. They walked back to the car. Without Grangerís constant hops into the vegetation, it was rather quicker than the way in. ØWhy did you have to do this on Imbolc?Æ asked Draco. In his opinion, this wouldíve been better planned for Beltane, for more congenial weather. She ignored the question in favour of posing one of her own. ØDo you think your motherís guests have left?Æ Draco conjured a pocket-watch. ØNo,Æ he lied. ØAre you sure? Rather a long tea, isnít it?Æ ØSociety teas are multiple hour affairs. My motherís favourites will probably stay for dinner and drinks.Æ Grangerís moment of smiling amongst the oak trees was fading and being replaced by the annoyance that seemed a chronic condition in Dracoís presence. ØWhy donít you go somewhere else? She wonít know that you arenít strictly working.Æ ØIím not leaving,Æ said Draco. ØIf you were to be attacked while out and about on project work, Shacklebolt would have my hide.Æ ØWhat are you protecting me from?Æ asked Granger with a sweeping gesture at the nothingness around them. ØRandy foxes?Æ ØIf youíd tell me what you were doing, Iíd be better able to establish potential threats.Æ ØIf thereís one thing Iíve learned from the enormous mistake I made telling Shacklebolt, itís that Iím not sharing another word on my work.Æ Granger crossed her arms. Her posturing was rather undermined by the single leaf stuck in her hat, waving in the wind. ØBrilliant. Iíll just continue to wave my wand about, waiting for the nameless baddies, shall I?Æ ØNo. You can Apparate to the nearest pub, have a cosy drink, and go home when youíre safe from the ladies.Æ ØIím not the one who needs to stay safe,Æ said Draco. Granger made a sound of frustration. ØYou canít come. You complicate things.Æ ØComplicate things how? I can stay out of the way Å didnít I just stay out of the way?Æ ØIím visiting the Chalice Well Gardens next. That involves passing as a Muggle. Which you donít.Æ ØI can very well pass as a Muggle,Æ said Draco, indignant. ØThe Auror programme includes a substantial unit on concealment and disguise, and I passed with a distinction, thank you.Æ Had he just been thinking that Granger-minding had ended up being a good decision? Why must she fight him on everything? Granger rubbed at her temples. ØWeíre wasting time Å time I havenít got.Æ ØThen letís go,Æ said Draco. ØShow me your best attempt at a Muggle disguise,Æ said Granger. There was a desperate kind of hope in her eyes, as though she knew it was going to be rubbish, but wanted to see, just in case. Draco shrank his Auror robes into a handkerchief, which he pocketed. Then he modified his suit to fit the current Muggle fashion, a little more relaxed in its tailoring. His boots he made into shiny menís dress shoes. His wand was concealed in a holster at his wrist. His hair he didnít touch: it was the height of perfection, magical or Muggle. ØAnd?Æ he asked, rotating slowly under Grangerís critical gaze. ØItíd be ideal if we were going to the Dorchester for dinner,Æ said Granger. She sighed. ØBut Å Iíll take it. Maybe we can make you look like a spiffy young professor, rather than a banker whoís lost his way...Æ She approached and made her own modifications, removing his tie and Transfiguring his shoes into Muggle trainers. Then she reached up and undid the top button of his shirt. (Queer sensation, to have Granger do that. Draco filed it away for further analysis later.) ØThatíll have to do,Æ said Granger, though she looked cynical. ØIf weíre critiquing each othersí appearances, youíre in need of a Scourgify,Æ said Draco. Granger Transfigured her car window into a mirror to discover, with an Øoh, my,Æ quite how mud-caked she was. She made quick work of the stray leaf and the dirt, then gave Draco an odd look. ØWhat?Æ asked Draco. ØNothing,Æ said Granger. ØTell me,Æ said Draco. ØNo.Æ ØYes.Æ ØI just Å I mightíve expected some joke about the mud, from you,Æ said Granger. Draco stilled. ØThose days are long past.Æ Granger arranged her hat and shrugged. Draco frowned. This wasnít the time for this conversation, but one day she would need to know how he had seen, firsthand, the horrors of those hideous attitudes, and how they still lived in his head in the dead of night, and how much he wished he could take back. ØIím not that person anymore,Æ said Draco. Seeing that he was so solemn, Granger, too, grew serious. ØAll right. I shouldnít have brought it up.Æ ØI shouldnít have insisted,Æ conceded Draco. ØThat too.Æ Granger waved her wand and her erstwhile mirror became a car window again. She grew brisk in her movements. ØShall we?Æ ØLetís,Æ said Draco. Then he ruined the serious moment by needing help with opening the car door. Granger came round to help him with saintly patience. She did not, to her credit, cast any aspersions on his ability to behave like a Muggle. Chapter5:TheKeepers They drove in silence for a little while. Granger looked preoccupied. Her thumb tapped at the steering wheel and she was worrying her lip. ØItís going to be busy this afternoon,Æ said Granger at length. ØAt the Gardens, I mean. Letís try to keep a low profile. We have to go through the gift shop to buy tickets to go in, but after that weíll be able to go into the gardens themselves and avoid the worst of the crowds.Æ ØI can keep a low profile,Æ said Draco. Granger gave him a side-eye in lieu of response. ØDoes the water have magical properties?Æ asked Draco. ØWhy do the Muggles even know about it?Æ Granger sat up straighter and took a breath, and Draco realised that had activated Swot Mode. ØThe wellsprings in this area have been in use by both Muggles and magical folk for millennia,Æ said Granger. ØIt wouldíve been too difficult to wipe the entire thing from so many minds after the Statute of Secrecy, I suppose. But, to answer your question, Muggles only know of two water sources in Glastonbury: one they call the White Spring, and one they call the Red Well. No real magical properties in either, though Muggles have ascribed their own spiritual and mythological significance to both. They have stories linking them to the Holy Grail, and King Arthur (heís meant to be buried in Glastonbury Abbey), and other bits of legend.Æ They were now approaching the outskirts of town. Granger turned at a sign pointing to the Chalice Well Gardens. ØBut,Æ she continued, Øthereís a third wellspring, one that you wonít find in the Muggle brochures. Itís called the Green Well. That one has bona fide magical properties. I needÅÆ here Granger hesitated, but seemed to decide that Draco would work it out anyway ØÅI need a sample from it.Æ ØFor your project.Æ ØYes.Æ ØAnd why at Imbolc, specifically?Æ ØYouíre being rather too inquisitive,Æ said Granger. Draco felt that she meant meddlesome, but had chosen the more polite option. ØI suppose the well reaches its highest magical potency at Imbolc,Æ said Draco. Granger made no answer. ØIím right, arenít I?Æ He saw her glance at the glovebox, wherein lay the Sneakoscope, promising to give away blatant lies. ØStop being so curious,Æ said Granger. ØThatís a bit rich, coming from you.Æ She scoffed. ØBeing curious is literally my job. Iím a researcher. Your job is to protect me from Forces Unknown, not interrogate me on a highly confidential, proprietary project.Æ Granger pulled into a parking spot, turned off the car, and waited for his retort. This witch was Å something. Draco had never endured such unrelenting points and counterpoints. He rather felt that, if heíd been keeping track of the score, heíd be the losing party. ØIím not a bodyguard. I wasnít assigned to you to clomp along brainlessly behind you,Æ said Draco. ØNo. Youíre a highly trained, highly competent Auror and this is an utter waste of your time.Æ Granger took a breath, visibly suppressing her irritation at the entire situation. The opening compliment elicited a tiny spark of delight, quickly suppressed by Draco. He didnít care what Granger thought of him. A group of Muggles passed the car, distracting the both of them. Mutually deciding on an unspoken truce Å very temporary, Draco was sure Å they climbed out of the car. The car park was busy. Muggles in families, Muggles pushing prams, Muggles in outfits that seemed exceptionally outlandish, even for Muggles. ØIíll warn you now, there are a lot of New Agey types here,Æ said Granger as they joined the crowd headed towards the entrance. ØNew Agey?Æ ØHippies. Wiccans. Pagans. Woo-woo types.Æ Granger seemed to be struggling for a definition. ØMuggles who are very spiritual and believe in magic Å or greater powers, anyway Å to some extent. Some of them even call themselves witches. They donít realise that there are actual witches and wizards, of course, and actual magic. They collect crystals and things, and perform rituals they read about in old books.Æ ØAh,Æ said Draco, though he didnít really understand. ØI thought Muggles were meant to be relentlessly logical.Æ ØSome are,Æ said Granger. ØSome are Å rather less than logical. Or perhaps some part of them remembers magic. Or subconsciously knows it exists. Or maybe they just want to believe in somethingèÆ They entered the busy gift shop Å bustling, cloyingly over-scented. Granger saw Draco wrinkle his nose and said: ØThatíll be the essential oils. The New Agers love those.Æ Draco examined some offensively perfumed candles, labelled åFor Relaxation.í ØWhy doesnít someone tell them theyíve over-synthesised these things to the point where any minor magical property is utterly lost?Æ Draco now found himself being steered by Granger and parked in a corner of the shop, like a Draco-shaped Mini Cooper. ØStay here,Æ she said. ØIíll get us tickets. Donít break anything.Æ Thank goodness for that last tip; he mightíve begun to pulverise things out of sheer excess of spirit, otherwise. Shoving his hands into his pockets, Draco stood in the corner and watched Granger go. The crowd around her didnít look at her twice. She really did blend in. As for him, he was the subject of more than a few upward glances Å his height, his white-blond hair, his åspiffyí suit. Granger had now joined the slow-moving queue for tickets. Having his Principal away from him in a busy spot was not something that Draco was keen on, from a purely professional standpoint. He performed some surreptitious Legilimency on a random sampling of the people in the shop. The crowd was comprised mostly of Muggles. There was one wizarding couple, but they had no ill-intent Å nor any idea that Granger was here. Would they recognise her if they saw her? Maybe Å but Draco couldnít delve into their minds so precisely from this distance. Grangerís instructions to keep a low profile were rather hypocritical, given that she had just struck up a conversation with the Muggles in the queue behind her. Draco, annoyed, cast a surface-level Legilimency on the family to check for sinister intentions. Nothing of interest Å they were just friendly tourists. He grew aware of a presence lurking about him, peeking at him from around one shelf, then around the other. He pretended to be interested in the smelly candles. Eventually, she showed herself. She was a shop assistant, heavily draped in diaphanous scarves, observing Draco with bulbous eyes. A name-tag was pinned to her jumper: Eunice. ØHello,Æ she said to Draco. ØMight I help you find something?Æ Draco caught her gaze and read her immediate thoughts. Nothing ominous, save for the fact that she thought him dreadfully handsome. ØNo, thanks,Æ said Draco, turning back to watch Granger between candles. She was finally nearing the front of the queue. Instead of taking this as the firm dismissal it was, Eunice fluttered closer to Draco, her eyes glued to his face. ØYour aura is... disturbed,Æ she said. Draco felt like he was being addressed by a Muggle incarnation of Trelawney crossed with a large moth. ØI donít think these candles will do you good,Æ said Eunice. ØI agree with you there,Æ said Draco. The sarcasm was lost on her. She nodded to herself and palpated the air around Draco, as though grasping at something. ØIíd suggest something stronger, like one of our cleansing incenses,Æ said Eunice, drifting down to point at a different shelf. Draco watched Granger make a beeline to the caf-bar. Would she kindly hurry up and save him from the moth? Eunice was now holding her hand towards him with her eyes closed. She shook her head gravely. ØYour heart chakra is underactive.Æ ØIs it?Æ ØThe Venus Incense, I think,Æ said Eunice. She grasped a packet and waved the pungent thing under Dracoís nose. ØAlthough, with your need for grounding, perhaps the SaturnèÆ She rummaged around the shelf and said things about transmuting energy and ascending to the celestial plane. Draco spied Grangerís hat bobbing in his direction through the crowd. ØI have to go,Æ he said, making his escape. ØOh, do you?Æ Eunice seemed put out. She slipped something into Dracoís hand. ØMy card. I do chakra realignments. Do reach out Å our energies are quite compatible...Æ Eunice floated away just as Granger arrived, bearing coffees. ØWho was that?Æ asked Granger, observing the retreating flutter of scarves. ØEunice,Æ said Draco. ØShe gave me this. Do you need your chakras realigned?Æ Granger exchanged one of her coffees for the proffered card. Something had been scribbled hastily on it. ØOoh, she gave you her number.Æ ØWhatís that mean?Æ ØThat Eunice fancies you,Æ said Granger, looking amused. ØMost women do.Æ Granger snorted, like this was a wickedly funny joke instead of a universal truth. She caught herself, sobered up, and looked at him with fresh wonder. ØYou're funny, Malfoy.Æ ØI live to serve,Æ said Draco, to cover his vexation. Granger returned the card to him. ØToo bad you donít even know what a mobile is. Poor Eunice was quite barking up the wrong tree.Æ ØShe thought me dreadfully handsome.Æ ØShe also thinks your chakras need realigning. Letís not get too wrapped up in the soundness of Eunice's judgements,Æ said Granger crisply. Let it be known that if any man needed his ego checked, a simple exchange with Granger would quite set him to rights. Draco sipped at the coffee Granger had brought. It was, remarkably, not terrible. ØHow did you know I like double espressos?Æ Granger shrugged. ØIt seemed your style.Æ ØBold? Bitter?Æ ØOverpriced.Æ Draco hid his scoff in the cup. Granger set them on a course towards the gardens. The rain began to let up and make way for tentative sunlight. The gardens were unexpectedly lovely, even if the Muggles in charge didnít have access to the warming charms and magical additives that made wizarding gardens such a spectacle through winter. Draco thought his mother might even appreciate the place. Though it was February, there was colour about, thanks to careful plant selections. Musical gurgles of water from wellsprings everywhere added auditory interest and the whole thing was gently illuminated by hundreds upon hundreds of candles tucked away in stony recesses. Signposts here and there asked visitors to maintain silence, out of respect for those meditating. Granger cast a silencing charm around the two of them so that they could talk. The Red Well at Glastonbury. (Photo: britainexpress.com) They came upon the Red Well Å aptly named, with its rust-coloured water. Draco read the plaque with passing interest. As Granger had noted earlier, the Muggles had fabricated some fanciful bit of Christian mythology suggesting the Holy Grail was buried here. There were also a few references to Arthurian legend. ØThe Muggles know about Morgan le Fay?Æ asked Draco, an eyebrow rising at the sight of such a famous witchís name on a Muggle placard. ØYes Å but sheís a figure of myth,Æ said Granger. ØMost of them donít think she really existed.Æ Draco tutted. Imagine. Next, they strolled through the well house that contained the White Spring Å a dark, wet-smelling place, where Muggles had decorated the rough stone walls with candles and small shrines to deities real and imagined: Saint Brigid, the Lady of Avalon, the King of the Faeriesè ØHere we are,Æ said Granger, as they made their way down a quieter, less-used path round the back of the well house. ØThere should be a sort of platform to take us down to the Green Well. Weíll have to use our wands to get in Å letís Disillusion ourselves in case any Muggles pass by.Æ Granger was now a Granger-shaped patch of garden in front of Draco, glimmering in the weak February sun. They stopped (well, Granger stopped, and Draco ran into her) at what looked like a manhole cover, tucked halfway under a bush. Across its weathered, cast-iron surface, were two large circles, intersecting under dead leaves and moss. ØThat symbolises the interplay between the physical and spiritual worlds,Æ said Granger. Draco could make out her ghostly wand gesturing at it. ØYou might recognise the shape Å the Red Well is constructed the same way. Letís get on. Itís the platform down.Æ They stood together on the manhole cover, rather squished. ØIncantation?Æ asked Draco, getting a mouthful of Grangerís invisible hair for the trouble. ØVesica piscis,Æ said Granger, mimicking the circular symbol with a wand-wave. The manhole cover shuddered. Granger crept closer to him. She smelled like a gorgeous combination of rain, wet forest, cappuccino, and soap. Then, without a by-your-leave, the platform dropped out from under them. The gorgeous-smelling witch clung to Draco and pierced both of his eardrums with her shriek. Thank the heavens for those silencing charms, thought Draco as they fell. A thick cushioning spell met them at the bottom of the drop. Which was excellent, as Draco hadnít intended to break both of his ankles today. He and Granger landed, bounced painfully into each other Å he was quite certain he elbowed her in the tit; she narrowly avoided his groin with her knee Å and collapsed, spread-eagled, on a thick bed of glowing fungi. ØWow. A first class voyage,Æ drawled Draco in the dark. ØGah,Æ responded Granger with something less than her usual acumen. Draco rose. Granger was somewhere on his left. She didnít seem to be making out quite as well as he was Å she was rather shocked. ØC-couldnít they set up a levitation charm?Æ she asked weakly. ØI thought that thing was a lift. I didnít expect a h-harrowing plummet to my death.Æ Draco groped about in the dimness to find that his coffee was a lost cause. A pity. They dismissed their Disillusionments and, when Granger managed to find her feet, began to walk down a passage illuminated by large, bioluminescent mushrooms. The sound of trickling water echoed throughout. Draco saw that even the walls were wet with a constant stream of moisture. As they entered a kind of long, low-ceilinged cave, Draco saw that there were other witches and wizards about. In a corner was what looked like a kind of bookshop, which Granger eyed longingly. There was also a counter that served as an apothecary. The entire place was lit solely by the glow of the mushrooms, which were everywhere Å the floor, the walls, dangling from the ceiling. ØOmphalotus luxaeterna,Æ said Granger. ØPretty, in a slimy sort of way.Æ If she added a ØLike you,Æ Draco was going to hex her Å his ego had taken enough abuse today. She didnít. (It was almost disappointing that sheíd let the occasion slip.) They came at last to the Green Well Å a bubbling green-lit wellspring, flanked by two statues in the penumbra. At least, Draco had thought they were statues Å until they moved. ØThe Keepers of the Well,Æ said Granger, who seemed unsurprised at the sight. ØRight. You stay here. I need to do the talking. They have to be dealt with politely. And respectfully.Æ Ignoring the insinuation that he couldnít be polite or respectful, Draco said, ØI think Iíd rather come.Æ His eyes strained to get a sense of what, exactly, lurked in the mushroom-speckled darkness. Grangerís irritation flared immediately. ØYou said you wouldnít get in the way. Youíre not even meant to be here. This is delicate. And critically important.Æ ØFine,Æ hissed Draco. ØIíll stay here.Æ He was within hexing range, anyway. Granger advanced. Draco peered at the two black-draped, hunch-backed forms. Were they witches? It was hard to tell in the dark. If they were witches, they most certainly had Hag blood, somewhere up the family tree. As well as a few other things, no doubt. Their twin pale stares, as luminescent as the mushrooms around them, disconcerted him. He found himself gripping his wand as Granger stepped up to the nearest of the Keepers. His first thought, as he processed this situation, was that Granger was either stupidly brave, or absolutely fucking reckless. Secondly, he didnít like this at all. These beings felt Dark. Old. Dangerous. Yes, Tonks, she was killed by a Hag. Yes, I was right there. Yes, I let her walk right up to it. Yes, she was disembowelled right in front of me. She wanted to pop by for some fancy water from this well, you know; nothing else would do. ØHere for a fill, dearie?Æ croaked the Keeper to Granger. The husky, dry voice echoed eerily. ØYes Å if I might? I have an offering,Æ said Granger. Her figure was a slight silhouette, backlit by the luminescence of the Green Well. ØShow me,Æ said the Keeper. The creature leaned towards Granger. There was something hungry in her movements. Dracoís wand-hand twitched. If the thing moved any closer to Granger, he had a decapitation curse ready to be unleashed. Granger, as always, was well-prepared. From somewhere in her anorak (where?!) she produced three large satchels, which she passed into the claws of the creature. ØGrain, offal, gold.Æ The second Keeper shuffled over, stuck her talon-like fingers into one of the bags, and pulled out a handful of glinting Galleons. (And where had Granger come by an entire sack of Galleons, by the by?) The goldís provenance did not seem to worry the second Keeper, at any rate. She crooned her satisfaction. ØVery nice. Lovely. Let the good girl through.Æ The first Keeper gestured Granger forward. ØHavenít you got a vessel, child?Æ Granger produced a large flask, whose golden stopper shone in the dim light. ØYes Å will this do?Æ The thing wheezed in assent. At a gesture from the Keeper, Granger plunged the flask into the Green Well. The second Keeper stared at Draco, as though aware of his tightly gripped wand and the well.practised curses that awaited on his tongue. She sniffed the air in his direction. ØPut the wand away, little boy. This girl wonít be meeting her demise here.Æ The first Keeper looked up from where she stood beside Granger. ØThe wizard is worried, is it?Æ ØIt is.Æ The first Keeperís white eyes caught Dracoís. There was ancient magic in them. He dared not perform Legilimency on this old mind. She cackled as though he had spoken aloud. ØThatís right, you wonít. Silly boy. Iíd make your brain soup and drink it while it's still warm, wouldn't I?Æ ØBut look at his eyes,Æ sighed the other Keeper. ØEyes like the rain-troubled skies...Æ Cold dread trickled down Dracoís spine, though the creature hadnít spoken a direct threat. He wondered whether his Darker curses would even be of use against these things Å perhaps he should be thinking Light. ØDonít you start with the rhyming,Æ said the first Keeper to her sister. ØWe donít want to mess with his melon.Æ ØEr Å Iím finished,Æ said Granger, who was now holding up her dripping flask. It was a blessed interjection. Draco was genuinely beginning to feel spooked and trigger-happy. ØGood girl,Æ said the first Keeper. ØMind you use it wisely.Æ ØI will,Æ said Granger, stepping away from the two of them. ØTh-thank you.Æ ØLove and light, my girl,Æ said the first Keeper. She and her sister cackled, as though that was the most riotous thing they had ever heard. Granger gave them a kind of bow and came back to Dracoís side. He kept his grip on his wand until theyíd walked well out of the Keepersí line of sight. Even then, he felt the twin pairs of white eyes touching at the back of his head. ØNo,Æ he said, holding Granger to him when she darted towards the underground book shop. ØBut I wanted toÅÆ ØNo,Æ said Draco, his grip on her elbow unyielding. ØLetís go.Æ Granger seemed to sense Draco's anxious anger and did not argue further. Granger was able to partake in the library and the orangery, and Draco enjoyed a poppyseed cake from the caf, and they watched the sunset together, and only quarrelled four times. Chapter 6: FindingSerenity Chapter Notes This chapter contains some French. I am half French but I didnít want to annoy readers with excessive use of another language, nor encumber the text with translations. Iíll indicate that they are speaking in French and put the dialogue in English. Please read with much nasalness, uvular fricatives, and overuse of åeuh.í After their Imbolc outing, Granger all but disappeared from Dracoís life. He visited her laboratory and home once a week to recast the wards, but their schedules rarely coincided, and he saw her cat more often than he saw her. Occasionally, his Jotter would buzz, and inform him that Granger was attending X public event at Y location. As her assigned Auror, she left his attendance to his discretion, though she made it clear his presence would be superfluous at best and bothersome at worst. Most of the events took place in secure magical locations Å panels at St. Mungos or Huntercombe, symposia at magical universities Å so Draco had rarely seen a need to exert himself and attend. In the unlikely event of a research panel devolving into a Situation, they had the rings. Tonks, seeing that Dracoís reports had become rather rote and that the Granger assignment was taking up only a little of his time, gleefully piled on additional missions. The cruel reward for competence was More Work and Draco wondered whether Potter and Weasel and their general bumbling wasn't the better plan, after all. And so, Draco found himself bunking with Buckley in a dingy hotel in Manchester, where they were gathering intelligence on a group of Dark artefact smugglers. Buckley was a good sort. He was a newish Auror, over-eager and keen to prove himself, which meant that Draco could take on a more Å well, he would call it managerial Å role, and delegate the majority of his surveillance shifts to the lad. This would, as Draco nobly explained to Buckley, permit him to garner more hands-on experience. Buckley nodded with enthusiasm and put Draco in mind of a puppy. He thus foisted off the three A.M. watch onto his zealous young colleague and went to bed. Draco felt as though he had just fallen asleep when his ring burnt him awake: pain and an elevated heart rate, reverberating from Cambridgeshire. It was half four. Nothing good ever happened at half four. Draco leapt out of bed and pulled his cloak over his pyjamas. He was too far across the country for a direct Apparition to Granger. He blasted a fire into the hotel foyer's dusty grate and Flooed to a Cambridge pub, and from there Apparated to Grangerís ring. Draco materialised in Grangerís spare bedroom, the one he called the ritual room. Granger was contorted into a dreadful, sweaty knot on the floor. Draco cast a flurry of Homenum Revelio and Finite Incantatem, looking for the invisible assailant who was obviously casting Crucio on her. ØMalfoy?Æ came Grangerís strangled voice from the floor. Dracoís revelation spells showed absolutely nothing. ØWhat the bloody hell are you doing?Æ asked Draco. Granger collapsed out of the horrid tangle and found her knees. ØYoga. What the bloody hell are you doing?Æ Draco had seen this mysterious term on Grangerís schedule. ØThatís yoga? What kind of self-inflicted martyrdomÅ?Æ Now that he had ascertained that there was no immediate threat, Draco could take in the scene. There were candles flickering in a corner and soft music was playing. Granger was outfitted in those ridiculously form-fitting Muggle clothes, khaki green this time. Her hair was pulled into a French braid, thick as Dracoís wrist. Granger was looking at him like he was an absolute bellend. ØI was trying a TaraksvasanaÅÆ ØA what?Æ ØA scorpion handstand Å Iíve been working towards it for weeks, and I almost had it, until you came in like a bolt from the blue and frightened me out of my wits!Æ Draco was feeling increasingly foolish. He pulled his cloak closed to cover his pyjamas. There was little he could do about his bare feet. ØWhat, pray, is the point of yoga?Æ ØFlexibility. Strength. Balance. Finding serenity.Æ Draco eyed Granger with cynicism at the last bit. ØHave you found it?Æ ØNo,Æ said Granger. She got to her feet with evident irritation. ØKindly recalibrate your ring so you only show up in a real crisis.Æ She flicked the Elektik lights on. Her cheeks were flushed. A trickle of perspiration was running down her neck. Her chest still heaved from her exertion. Draco could smell salt, female sweat, and the burnt wick of a candle. His idiot brain took this image and immediately created several new neural pathways that had never existed previously, connecting the idea of Granger with the concept of sexy. It was an extremely unwelcome development and Draco wondered whether he should lobotomise himself on the spot. A gravelly meow interrupted his thoughts. The bandy-legged cat had wandered in. It trotted up to Granger and then, upon noticing Draco, favoured him with a hiss. Draco did not hiss back, but it was a near thing. ØIíll just leave then, shall I?Æ said Draco. ØDo,Æ said Granger. ØGoodbye.Æ Draco Disapparated out. ~ Draco had expected (and rather desired) nothing but annoyed silence from Granger after heíd barged in on her like an unhinged maniac. However, he was surprised to receive a message from her the next day Å and not just a message, a bona fide apology. Malfoy: Iím sorry about my behaviour yesterday. I shouldíve been more appreciative that you arrived so fast when you thought something was the matter. Will warn you next time I try a Taraksvasana. -Hermione Apologies werenít part of Dracoís natural lexicon. His upbringing, both at home and at school, didnít encourage the practise. Sorry was an admission of wrongdoing, a sign of guilt, an obvious weakness. There was something nice about receiving one, however. It warmed the soul, really. He wasn't sure which part he liked the most Å Granger mulling this over for a day and then apologising, or Granger admitting sheíd been wrong, or Granger appreciating him. Instead of dismissing her note from the Jotter, Draco saved it in one of the back pages. Heíd have to ask Potter how rare Granger apologies were, and whether he ought to have it framed. ØDraco, darling, youíre distracted.Æ His motherís voice, with an undertone of reproach, called him back to reality. Reality was an unfortunate place Å tea in the stuffiest parlour at the Manor, with his mother, her friend Madame Delphine Delacroix, and Madame Delacroixís daughter. Todayís debutante was Rosalie Delacroix. Beauxbatons educated, Pure-blood, objectively beautiful. Draco put the Jotter away. ØPardonnez-moi, mesdames. Que disiez-vous?" ØVous avez un Jabbering Jotter!Æ exclaimed Rosalie. ØThose have only just begun to cross the Channel to us in France. We canít get enough of them. Even my mother, who is so traditional, adores hers.Æ ØIndeed,Æ nodded Madame Delacroix. ØI couldnít get my husband to reply to an owl, not for love nor money Å but these make it so easy. A true innovation. England should be proud of these Å Weasleys, was it? These Frres Belette?Æ When the conversation drifted away from him, Draco sent a response to Granger: Do advise of future scorpion pushups. P.S. Apologies from you may become my new drug of choice. -D Draco looked up, a vague smile on his face, to find Rosalie chattering about an upcoming gala that her father was going to host. Draco had missed the beginning of it. In support of orphans, or something, probably. ØWe would be so delighted to see the two of you there,Æ said Rosalie, her hands clasped into a knot of entreaty. Iíve compiled quite a list of your illicit activities, responded Draco. Ø...Quíen pensez-vous, Draco? Would that suit you?Æ Draco raised his head. Madame Delacroix had asked him a question that heíd only partially heard, something about his schedule in March. He answered in the affirmative Å of course, yes, heíd be happy to clear his calendar for such a noble cause. His mother beamed at his easy acceptance and indicated that she, too, would be delighted to come. Granger answered, If only illegal extension charms were the worst of my sins. No, responded Draco, I know the real extent of your depravity. She anticipated him. I see that the library visit will haunt me. Draco grinned into his teacup. His mother saw the smile and, encouraged by what seemed like his good mood, asked the ladies if theyíd like to see the gardens. Rosalie declined, claiming to have caught a bit of a chill. Madame Delacroix and Narcissa left for the gardens. Draco sobered up as he found himself in a forced tte--tte with Rosalie. The pretty witch spoke charmingly of anything she thought might catch his attention Å Quidditch, his job, the weather. Draco listened with only one ear, because it wasnít about hags wanting to use his skull as a sippy cup and it was therefore rather dull. He found himself wishing to be continuing a conversation with another witch, one whose newest message had just buzzed in his pocket. The talk turned to mutual friends, to upcoming dinners, to other frivolities. Rosalie agreed enthusiastically with every point Draco made, no matter how inane, instead of whipping counter.arguments back at him. She laughed at his mildest jokes instead of retorting with something snappy. She clung to his every word Å uncritical, eager Å instead of challenging him. She complimented him to excess. It made for rather weak conversation. When Draco realised who he had unconsciously made into Rosalieís foil, he was taken aback. Since when had Granger become the yardstick by which he measured female company? The conversation Å such as it was Å lasted twenty minutes. Eventually, Rosalie convinced Draco to add a page to his Jotter for her, under the pretext of sending more details for the gala. Draco shrugged in absent-minded agreement. (It was a careless moment he would later regret, as Rosalie was a proficient Jotter and wrote to him incessantly thereafter.) The ladies returned from their tour. Smiles were smiled, goodbyes were said, and Draco sighed in relief when Henriette the house-elf escorted their visitors back to the Floo parlour. Later that night, Narcissa drifted into Dracoís study to probe. ØRosalie is a sweet girl, isnít she? You seemed like you were getting along.Æ There was such a quiet optimism in her voice, Draco wondered if it wouldnít be kinder to lie. But that would give his mother hopes, and dashing them later would be all the crueller. ØI suppose we got along well enough,Æ he said. She detected the lack of enthusiasm immediately. ØBut?Æ ØRather a milquetoast kind of girl.Æ Narcissaís thin hands clutched before her in disappointment. ØOh.Æ Quarrelling with his pale, sad mother was never high on Dracoís to-do list. He tried to be gentle as he admonished her. ØWeíve had this conversation before. I donít need, nor want, you to hand-pick witches for me.Æ ØI only want to help you.Æ Narcissa's thin fingers clasped at each other. ØI want you to find someone who is educated, and lovely, and who will be a devoted companion, and give you children, and fill this great empty house with laughter again. Rosalie would be all those things. Any number of the witches Iíve introduced you to would be all these things.Æ She paused, then added, ØI only want you to be happy, Draco.Æ ØI am happy.Æ Another sigh from Narcissa. ØAt your age, your father was married, you know Å and had you Å you were four or five by then...Æ ØI am not my father.Æ Narcissa, seeing that she would make no further headway here, glided towards the door. ØI donít think she exists,Æ she said over her shoulder as she left. ØWho?Æ ØThe perfect witch youíre apparently waiting for.Æ ~ Granger wrote to Draco about a week later, advising him that she was speaking at a Muggle conference that Thursday. Where? he asked. Magdalen College, Oxford, said Granger. Thurs 2-5pm. Iím on at 2.30. I doubt I shall be murdered but leave your attendance to your expert judgement. Yes, Granger, thank you for the cheek: he would use his expert judgement. Audience? he asked. Muggle doctors, said Granger. How many? asked Draco. 150, said Granger. Dracoís eyebrows rose. He sometimes forgot quite how small the wizarding world was. There were probably less than a hundred fully fledged Healers in the entirety of the UK. Maybe three or four hundred if you included Mediwitches and other field medics. Iíll look in, said Draco. Spiffy prof. look please, said Granger. Donít embarrass me. Draco didnít deign to answer her. The day of the conference came around. Draco Apparated a small distance away from Magdalen College and walked to the auditorium Granger had specified, at the time she specified, wearing the clothing that she had specified. Bossy sort of witch, was Granger. The volunteer at the registration table was gently Confunded into thinking that Draco was a registered participant, and he was let in with a name badge and a programme. He took stock of the building, discreetly casting revelation spells when Muggles werenít looking. The foyer, cloak room, and toilets were free of any naughtiness, as were the back rooms. The handful of people he covertly cast Legilimency on were who they were meant to be Å brainy Muggles, here to become even more brainy. Draco found a shadowy alcove near the front of the auditorium from which to survey the place. He was about twenty metres away from the stage, from which vantage he could see Granger seated at a long table, along with three of her fellow experts. They were chatting amongst themselves as the crowd filed into the auditorium. From a risk assessment standpoint, Granger couldnít have been more exposed, sitting as she was under a literal spotlight. Draco pried through the minds of the people in the front row and found nothing but eagerness to begin and high levels of admiration directed towards the doctors on the stage. Then he peeked through the minds of the unseated people idling on the stairs at the edge of the auditorium, as he was, and found only volunteers, students sneaking in, and a bearded gentleman wearing large ear-coverings, whose chief role seemed to be managing blinky boxes with wires coming out. There wasnít a single witch or wizard present, as far as he could tell. Satisfied that there were no immediate threats, Draco cast precautionary wards across the front of the stage and settled into his alcove. As the conference began, he flipped open the programme. It informed him that todayís panel would feature international leaders in immune cell engineering and immunotherapy. This told Draco very little, of course. Cancer was a decidedly Muggle ailment. Wizarding folk were rarely afflicted with it, and when they were, it was quickly resolved. However, it seemed that this was not the case with Muggles, for whom it was a serious condition, and nigh incurable in some forms. Enter Granger and her cohort of fellow brains. Their talks today included such thrilling questions as FL and CLL: A New Care Paradigm and Hodgkinís Lymphoma: Mitigating Toxicity while Preserving Cure. Draco decided that it would be quite safe to leave Granger to her CLLís. The only threat in this room was death by obscure acronym. Just as he was about to leave Å he rather fancied a nap Å the mediator announced that Dr. Grangerís presentation was next. Draco watched her walk across the stage and decided to stay. She was a small figure on the stage, by far the shortest amongst the panellists. She smiled at the audience as she approached the Muggle voice-amplification device on the podium. Her movements were confident and poised. She didnít have notes, but the large screens behind her projected diagrams and bullet points. She made a few opening remarks that included a joke that went completely over Dracoís head, but the auditorium filled with laughter. Her presentation focused on the advancements of something called CAR T cell therapies in B-cell malignancies. She made eye contact with everyone, took questions throughout, was challenged, counter-challenged the challengers, and defended her position without skipping a beat. She was confident, she was clever, and in this room, she was important. Granger in her element was a rather impressive thing. After finishing her presentation, Granger returned to the table and her fellow panellists discussed her talk. At some point, Draco wasnít even certain that Granger and her interlocutors were even speaking English anymore, as they cross-examined each other on EBV and TNK-cell lymphomas and their diagnostic challenges, and the architecture of liquid biopsy research. Granger made a pun about MALT1 degradation which was, apparently, uproariously funny. Draco entertained himself by performing Legilimency on the panellists. Not a single one thought that Granger was an insufferable swot. He found only respect, admiration, and a surprise crush from the male doctor on her left. Draco learned that something called quantitative radiomics analytics existed. Their predictive values were discussed at length. These Muggle doctors were another thing entirely, brilliantly working through the impossible, dedicating their entire lives to it. How had he ever thought Muggles base and ignorant? Draco shook his head. Granger mustíve caught the movement of his white-blond hair in the shadows. When saw that it was Draco, she gave him a brief smile of acknowledgement Å bizarre feeling, that Å and carried on with her current dissertation, her hands making wide arcs as she explained something. At length, conclusions were drawn, closing remarks were made, and the conference ended with much applause. The participants milled about, with Granger and the panel at the centre of the crowd. Draco, feeling rather mushy in the brain from all the new words and Legilimency he had performed (perhaps the Keeper wouldnít need such a thick straw after all), decided that he was no longer needed here. He made for the exit, but Granger caught him as he passed the stage. As usual, small talk was dispensed with. Instead, he found his Muggle disguise being assessed by her, her gaze accented by a raised eyebrow. ØPassable,Æ was the pronouncement on the outfit. ØWhy the eyebrow?Æ asked Draco. Granger pointed at the name tag that the Confunded volunteer had affixed to Dracoís lapel.ØHello, Professor Takahashi.Æ ØAh,Æ said Draco. ØYes. Thatís me.Æ ØAnd how is Tokyo at this time of year?Æ ØVery nice,Æ said Draco. ØProfessor Takahashi is from Kyoto,Æ said Granger. Her arms were crossed, but there was amusement in her eyes. ØRather ballsy to pose as one of Japanís most renowned clinical oncologists.Æ ØI am nothing if not ballsy,Æ said Draco, flipping his hair. ØDid you know that the good Dr. Driessen fancies you?Æ Now Grangerís second eyebrow joined the first, at her hairline. ØWhat?Æ ØHeís going to ask you out for drinks tonight.Æ ØNo.Æ ØYes. ØI always do.Æ He felt Grangerís hand on his chest twitch. ØWell Å lovely to see you, as always, Hermione,Æ said Dr. Driessen. ØGoodbye, Johann,Æ said Granger with a rather fixed smile. Act natural.Æ Granger cleared her throat and let her hand slide down Dracoís chest, taking the nametag with it. She stepped away from him slowly and tried to make it look natural. (It did not.) Draco himself vacillated between amusement at her discomfiture and alarm at how nice the curve of her hip had felt against him, and how good she smelled (again). ØI had to come up with something, and you were conveniently Å there.Æ ØUse me as a prop anytime,Æ said Draco, scanning the crowd instead of looking at her. The Turf Tavern was a stupidly busy place, especially so when the conference had disgorged hundreds of thirsty participants into Oxfordís streets. Draco found a table while Granger got them drinks (G&Ts all around) and they found themselves crammed on a bench amongst a dozen of the finest immunologists and oncologists in the world, getting progressively more drunk. Draco was asked what he did for a living. Granger had gone rather worried-looking when the question was posed (tch Å had she no faith?) but Draco had a well-practised cover story at the ready. Tonks insisted on every Auror developing a few Muggle biographies, and some backup wizarding ones, and she quizzed them on their covers routinely to keep them on their toes. Draco shared his favourite. This evening, he was a pilot. Few Muggles knew very much about the technicalities of flying, so, unless he encountered a real pilot Å unlikely, surrounded by tipsy doctors Å he was safe. And, of course, he had a genuine passion for wizarding flying, which lent a certain veracity to his tales of aeroplane heroics. ØFlying isnít that hard, you know,Æ he said to the table. ØKeep the blue side up.Æ Laughter. The doctor beside him indicated that such simple principles applied in medicine, too: keep the guts in. More laughter. She looked away, nonplussed. When asked when heíd arrived in Oxford, Draco said, ØThis morning.Æ When asked what he was doing in Oxford, he said ØDoctor Granger.Æ Granger choked on her drink. More laughter. When Draco next stole a glance at her, Granger looked like she was going to lure him into a lonely alley and, there in the darkness, strangle him. Who knew that teasing the swot of the century could be such a glee-filled activity. Someone else joined their table to raucous cries of welcome Å the real Professor Takahashi. Narcissa Malfoy wouldíve had a heart attack just looking at the greasy things, oozing with three different kinds of cheese. Granger passed Draco the platter in an ambivalent kind of way, as though she expected him to turn his nose up at Muggle pub food. Draco took one. It was the best filth heíd ever eaten. Others appeared amused at the timing, and eager to join in. ØI love pub quizzes,Æ said the grey-haired woman on Dracoís right. However, he did know how many keys a baby grand piano has (88), and what year Cessna was founded (1927), and which countryís national anthem had 158 verses (Greece; all 158 had been sung at the last Quidditch World Cup). There were a few biology and science questions which the doctors hammered into the ground with an unnecessary level of detail. Draco learned that Picolax was used for something called colonoscopy preparation; one of the doctors nodded grimly and said, ØNight of a thousand waterfalls,Æ and Draco was too frightened to ask for clarification. ØWe never stood a chance.Æ Granger, still stuck on a problem, looked vexed. Then geography, then music, then zoology, which Granger was solidly capable of handling with occasional assistance from her colleagues. The doctors handed over theirs to the young engineers who had come in second, saying it hadnít been a fair fight, since their combined years at uni were greater than the poor engineersí years alive in the first place. The evening wound down. ØObviouslyèÆ The mystery was unlocked and she scribbled out the solution on her napkin. Then she threw down her pen with finality and stood up. ØLetís go,Æ she said. Draco rose to his feet with care, waiting to see how much the drinks were going to catch up with him. It wasnít too bad, considering. The cheese toasties turned out to be rather absorbent. ØHave a nice night, you two,Æ said the barkeep with a wink at Granger. Granger gave him a rather sickly smile and all but ran for the door. Her dark eyes observed him like he was a fresh maths theorem to unpuzzle. On one hand, it flattered his ego to have intrigued the Great Brain. On the other, given her propensity for solving things, it rather frightened him. He didnít want to be solved. ØGood night, Malfoy.Æ Draco stepped into the fire. Chapter7: Ostara; Contrarinessof Granger Dracoís next warding visit to Grangerís house was marred by what was, in retrospect, a slight lapse in judgement. As weeks had gone by and he had made little further headway on discovering the nature of her research project, his mind turned to a certain Object of Interest in her study: the tattered grimoire on the plinth. The one that she had threatened to cry about. And so, one morning in early March, when Draco was preparing for his perennial visit to Grangerís cottage, he sent her a note indicating that heíd be going into her house, if that was all right, because he hadnít warded the windows individually, and it was bothering him. Granger acquiesced with a dry, If you really find it necessary. Yes, he did. Draco timed his visit to coincide with one of Grangerís lessons at Trinity, to ensure that he wouldnít be disturbed as he snooped. When he arrived, her cat Å perhaps sensing something nefarious afoot Å took up a position of power on the roof, and stared at him as he recast the outside wards. ØOnly doing my job, cat,Æ said Draco, making a great show of it. The cat regarded him with cynicism. He entered the cottage and warded the first floor windows with alacrity, then bounded upstairs to do the others. Grangerís bedroom was done first, with minimal looking about, because the cat was at the door, and watching him. Then the yoga room.Then, finally, he came to the study. The grimoire was still on its plinth, open in the middle, still surrounded by the green glow of stasis charms. Draco warded the window under the watchful eye of the cat and drifted towards the tome. The catís stare grew more penetrating. Draco peered at the visible pages. Through the stasis charm, the words were blurred and seemed to dance. The script was laboured and heavy. It wasnít English Å in fact, bits of it looked French Å Anglo-Norman, perhaps? In that case, this was an old book Å five centuries, at least. From the bits that he could understand, he was looking at an elaborate description of a landscape: a green hill under dancing bluebells, and gleaming thistle-down, and the velvet-soft leaves of Faliís Gossamer. That was all that Draco could make out, the rest was too damaged. He remembered Grangerís moment of volubility at the Mendip Way, something about descriptions of flora giving her clues for her mysterious pursuit. None of the plants mentioned here had featured on her list, however. This must be a different site. He dearly wanted to see the bookís cover. He glanced at the cat. The cat all but shook its head. ØJust a quick look,Æ said Draco to the cat. ØI might be able to help her, you know.Æ The cat whisked its tail in disapproval. Draco did it anyway. Using his wand as a lever, so that he didnít touch the book at all, he lifted the cover enough to peek at the front. It was entitled, Revelations. The cat meowed a wrathful meow. Draco let the cover fall back into place and left the cottage rather quickly. ~ Draco didnít know how, but Granger suspected something. First his Jotter went off with a series of messages, questioning him about whether he had touched the book. Draco denied, denied some more, and then Stunned the Jotter so it would stop buzzing. Then Granger somehow got hold of Boethius, and used Dracoís own owl to send him increasingly heated queries. Draco sent Boethius off with a missive to a friend in Italy, which would keep him out of Grangerís hands for at least a week. Then a Howler landed on his lap in the middle of a briefing with Tonks. It got as far as, ØMALFOY, DID YOUÅÆ before Draco incinerated it. Tonksí eyebrows rose. ØWas that Hermione?Æ ØYes,Æ said Draco. ØThat explains that,Æ said Tonks. She gestured to the Foe Glass behind her. One of the shadows looked rather familiar in form: a slender woman, a pile of curls on her head, her hands on her hips, silhouetted against the grey. ØI suppose sheís having violent thoughts about me by proximity to you,Æ said Tonks. ØWhat did you do?Æ ØNothing,Æ said Draco, which was essentially true. Tonks stared at him for a long time, her fingers tapping against her desk. ØI will assume that whatever you did was done in your professional capacity as an Auror, to ensure her continued protection.Æ ØThat is always my primary objective,Æ said Draco. Tonks gave him another long look, then turned back to his report on the Dark artefact smugglers. ØBe careful, Malfoy.Æ Thus dismissed, Draco returned to his cubicle. He had barely sat down when a silver otter dove at him out of nowhere. It called him a nosey prat and a bloody liar, and advised him to jump off a bridge. Draco sent his own Patronus back with a request to Granger to kindly keep her loud otters to herself: he was working. For a short while, that was that. Draco kept an eye on Grangerís schedule to spot breaks in her calendar during which she might decide to come and find him in person. She did not, possibly because she was saving lives or other such tomfoolery. That was when he noticed that another of her asterisk holidays was coming up rather soon Å that weekend, in fact. So Å Ostara is coming up, he Jotted casually that evening. Her response was instantaneous, if off-topic. That book was NOT yours to touch. Where are you going at Ostara? asked Draco. You are NOT invited, said Granger. Donít need an invitation, said Draco. I do not need supervision by prying nitwits, said Granger. See you soon, said Draco. She didnít respond. Bit pouty, sometimes, was Granger. ~ The Not-Invited Prying Nitwit had a lovely lie-in on Saturday before getting ready to Apparate to Granger. Frankly, after her hijinks with the Keepers of the Well, she had lost any privileges she mightíve had to make calls about whether or not she needed Auror supervision. Draco had no faith that Granger wasnít about to throw herself into a den of vampires to get her hands on some other obscure flask. Those virtuous reasons aside, the timing of Grangerís weekend escapades continued to serve Dracoís purposes. Today, Grangerís frolic, whatever it was, coincided with one of his motherís luncheons. Draco was glad of the excuse to be away, even if his mother promised that she had no ulterior motives and that the presence of any young, eligible witches would be coincidental. Draco Flooed to the Mitre, the usual Cambridge pub, and from there he Apparated to Grangerís ring, which brought him to her kitchen. Only the cat responded Å a pitiful meow at his mistressí absence. ØYour witch is a pain in my arse, you know that?Æ The cat curled itself into a sad orange loaf at Dracoís feet. Draco pocketed Grangerís ring with a mutter. Then he pulled out his wand and cast his tracking charm. Good thing he made contingency plans. In front of him glowed a map, and on that map were points of light brighter than the rest. Grangerís old trainers remained, it seemed, in her laboratory at Trinity College. The tea mug was somewhere in this cottage. The handful of her hairpins that Draco had charmed were rather scattered Å some at the laboratory, some at St. Mungoís. A single hairpin was currently gambolling through Uffington, for reasons unknown. Reasons that Draco was rather eager to discover. Draco Apparated to the hairpin. ØSurprise,Æ he said as he materialised before Granger. She jumped a metre into the air, which was satisfying, and then swore at him, which was even more satisfying. Draco looked around to find himself at the top of a green, wind-swept hill. It was a strange kind of formation: tall, but flat across the top. The turf below his feet was rich, green, and deliciously springy, except where it was interrupted by large splotches of chalky white. All around him undulated a lovely vista of rich pasture-lands, meandering hedgerows, and wandering sheep-paths. Dragon Hill, where legends tell of St. George killing a dragon, whose blood burned the turf to bone-white chalkiness where it ran. (photo: nationaltrust.org.uk) Now Draco turned his attention to Granger herself, who was all kitted up in her Muggle walking gear. He took them on a few low circles around the shed to get acquainted with Old Glory. The broom was a stiff old harridan, but she was doughty enough to make headway through the northern wind, encumbered with the two of them. She was steady in the air, far more so than Dracoís flighty models at home, which twitched away at the touch of a finger. For a voyage over this arm of the North Sea, Old Glory would do well. Slow and steady. Draco informed Granger of this fact in an attempt at reassurance. A gurgle was his only response. Given that Grangerís hands were occupied with strangling the broom, Draco cast wind-breaking spells over the two of them, so that they might hear each other talk. He also cast warming charms, which made Granger shudder gratefully against him, which felt interesting. Dracoís final adjustment was having a passenger, which was a rare occurrence for him. The weighting felt different and the steering trended downward. The few times heíd doubled up on brooms had been for dates and those flights were succeeded by landings in a secluded location and a good snog. Draco rather doubted that there would be sexy bum wiggles against his groin on this flight: Granger clung to the broom like grim death, unmoving, as though she had been Petrified onto it. Only her hair eluded the stiffness. The few strands that escaped her bun softly touched his face. She smelled like shampoo and antiseptic. Draco leaned forwards and put his hands on the broom in front of Granger, ready to go. Granger, eyes closed and all, felt the change, and expressed violent wishes with regard to Dracoís fate in this world and the next, which would have made a more delicate man weep. Draco merely said, ØSteady on, Granger,Æ and slowed them down by 0.01 percent. ØTo the Holm of Eynhallow, old bird,Æ said Draco, giving the broom a pat. Next stop: the sea. Chapter10: TheOrkney Isles Draco had enjoyed a great many flights in his young life, but that trip across the North Sea ranked as one of the most savagely beautiful heíd ever experienced. He was almost glad for the old broom Å it forced a level of care in his flying, and attention to the winds, that his newer brooms didn't. The flight was quite technical. The cross-winds were many, and the weather capricious, so Draco chose a low flight path about ten metres above the surge. The air was salty and cold and splashed across their faces like kisses from ghostly mermaids. As they reached open waters, a Great Skua joined them in their flight. It observed Draco with a beady eye, its wingtip a mere metre from his face. Then it dropped to the seaís surface, skimmed wings with its dark watery double, and soared away again. As they flew northwards, the skies cleared to reveal a fragile scatter of stars in the sky. Below them, the reflected constellations spilled and plunged into the waves. The sight was sublime. It made Draco feel small and inconsequential. The Calming Draught mustíve kicked in, because Granger felt a hair less tense between his arms, though her mittens were still twisted hard around the broom. As far as Draco could tell, her eyes were still closed and she was missing all of these breathtaking vistas. But, he supposed, whatever got her through it. Something big broke the water below them. ØGranger Å look! Thereís a Hippocampus! No, thereís two of them! Hippocampuses! Hippocampi?Æ ØOh!Æ gasped Granger, finally opening her eyes. She looked down to where the enormous, sleek heads of the horse-like creatures had split the waves. One disappeared again, but the other breached, its huge tail arcing just below them, then vanishing without a splash into the waves. Draco slowed, wanting to turn back and observe them, but the first Hippocampus had appeared again ahead, closely followed by its mate. He urged the broom to catch up. The creatures picked up speed and Draco matched them, skimming the waves just at the height of their manes. They raced. Draco asked the broom for more. The majestic creatures moved below and beside them with no sign of exertion save the pearling mist that burst out of their wide nostrils. One, slightly smaller, was sea-glass green, her mane as white as the foam cresting around her. The other was larger, blue as the sea-swells, and just as swift, keeping close to his mate. Saltwater soaked them. Draco pressed on, and he was a wave, and the sea-horses were waves, and they flew and crashed and foamed, and they surged on, and now they were the wind, and now they were the brine, and now they were seafoam before the storm. The wave-riders turned west towards the open ocean. Their pale eyes peered at Draco and Granger, and the male threw back his gorgeous head, as though challenging them to follow towards unknown shores. Draco knew that he could not. The pair disappeared like swift-finned spirits, a vision fast-fading against the elusive sea. Masterpiece by the wildly talented nikitajobson. Then there was only Draco, breathless, and Granger, shuddering, and the cloud-foamed waves. Neither of them spoke. The broom resumed her course. Now, on their left and right, there loomed the dark forms of landmasses. They had entered the Orkney Isles. The wind grew less cutting and the seas less rough. Ahead of them, a small island glowed like a jewel amongst the dark seas, alight with Beltane fires. The broom, sensing her destination near, put on a fresh burst of speed. Draco spotted a flattish rock face by starlight and came in for a landing. Granger mustíve closed her eyes again, because when her toes hit the ground, she squeaked, and wouldíve tumbled off the broom but for Dracoís arm around her waist. Draco dismounted. Grangerís activity wouldíve been more accurately described as a kind of sagging tumble into the moss. ØThat was brilliant!Æ Draco spun under the stars, holding his arms aloft. ØExhilarating. Fucking magical.Æ Granger said nothing. Draco cast a Lumos at her. She appeared to be hugging the earth. ØYou all right?Æ ØJust a moment,Æ gasped Granger. Draco left her to compose herself. He cast a few spells inland, which informed him that there were about a hundred witches and wizards on the island, and almost an equal amount of fires, great and small. Granger had regained her feet. Draco, seeing how bloodless she still looked, offered her his arm in a kind of gentlemanly automatism. She took it, her own grasp all a-tremble. They advanced towards the centre of the island with the Beltane fires and the sound of a cheerful fiddler guiding them in. As they walked, Draco began to notice immense shapes on either side of them, only perceivable because they were a black opacity, permitting no light of the stars through them. ØStanding stones,Æ said Granger. ØThere are henges this far north?Æ asked Draco. He didnít actually care whether or not there were henges this far north, but questions of that nature were sure to awaken the swot in Granger and distract her from her jitters. He was right. Granger began in a weak kind of voice which gained in strength and enthusiasm as she progressed. ØYes Å this is one of the oldest stone circles in the UK. The megaliths are thought to date back to about 3200 BCE. Theyíre around three metres tall Å absolutely breathtaking in daylight, Iíd imagine. This henge is called the Ring of Eynhallow.Æ ØWeíll have missed most of the merrymaking, I think,Æ said Granger as they grew close enough to the crowd to hear voices. ØToo bad. Iíd hoped to see some of the rituals in personèÆ ØWhich rituals?Æ ØOh, old protection magicks. Handfastings. Offerings to the Aos s. Lots of jumping over fires and other silliness, too. I donít know why wizards think thatíll impress a witch, but then, wizards do a great deal of things I donít understand. Like viper neckties.Æ Now Granger fell silent, mulling over that particular bout of idiocy. ØBut, well Å at least Iíll have got what I came here for.Æ They were near the centre of the ring now, walking amongst many peat fires and carousing witches and wizards. Granger was staring at the fires in restrained excitement. Her grasp on Dracoís arm grew tight. As Grangerís attention was elsewhere, Draco pointed his wand at a few passersby and cast nonverbal Legilimency. He was satisfied that this was a low risk situation Å the general mood was festive and tipsy and no one cared who they were. The peak of the celebration was over and things were drawing to a jolly close. Tents were being put up here and there on the periphery of the fires, while around others, groups were settling down for some whisky-fueled philosophy. Draco and Granger were accosted by friendly merry-makers and invited to join their fires. Granger politely declined and steered them to a quieter end of the henge, where a small fire burned low. ØLetís wait for this one,Æ she said. ØI suppose it has to go out naturally?Æ asked Draco. ØNo dousing charms?Æ ØNo dousing charms. Beltane ash at its most primitive.Æ Granger Transfigured two stumps into cushy ottomans, which she and Draco pulled towards the fire. After the bitterly cold flight, the heat was positively magnificent. Draco sat close, but Granger was near enough to roast her knees and set fire to her hair. She pulled off her mittens and held her hands close to the flames. ØOf the thousands and thousands of Beltane fires tonight, why these ones, specifically? In the most desolate corner of the UK?Æ asked Draco as his face began to thaw. Granger had a ready answer, of course Å and seemed delighted that heíd asked. ØBecause the fires on this Holm are from a very specific fire Å the very one that Cerridwen used for her cauldron. I donít know if you remember her taleèÆ ØOnly whatever was on her Chocolate Frog card,Æ said Draco, vaguely recollecting a witch with masses of dark hair. ØLooked rather like you, come to think of it.Æ ØPsh,Æ scoffed Granger. ØI can only dream of becoming a fraction of the witch she was. She was a mistress of Transfiguration, amongst many things Å she could transform into any creature at will. She makes todayís Animagi look jejune. Anyway Å Iíll spare you the treatise Å you mightíve noticed that these flames look a little redder than normal fire?Æ Draco nodded; the flames were indeed ruddier than usual. ØI assumed it was the peat.Æ ØNo. Theyíve kept her legendary flame alive, for generation upon generation, in these islands. Isnít it incredible?Æ Grangerís eyes were bright. ØWhat a thing to witness. What a thing to feel, on my own hands. Itís surreal. Itís extraordinary.Æ ØWhat do you need the ash for?Æ asked Draco, since she was being so voluble. Granger clamped her mouth shut. Draco shrugged. It had been worth a try. He dug into the pockets of his cloak to pull out the provisions from Thurso. He passed the cured meats and cheese to Granger and tucked the flask of mulled wine against the fire to warm up again. Granger looked surprised, though whether it was at the foresight or the unexpected kindness, Draco wasnít certain. She tore the packet open. ØIím actually starved. Thank you. This was so thoughtful of you, IÅÆ Draco cut her off to waylay further fluff. ØDidnít bring any Banoffee pie in that anorak?Æ ØNo,Æ said Granger. She fished about in one of the pockets. ØI do have a few protein bars, though. They might be a bit squishedèÆ Draco didnít know what a protein bar was, but it tasted like cheap chocolate, which was glorious on his tongue after all the sea salt. They ate. Granger was mannerly about it, taking small bites interspersed with further commentary on Cerridwen. Draco wondered, for the first time, what her family was like, and whether they were well off Muggles? She had a sense of decorum and a kind of innate dignity that spoke of good breeding. ØHippocampus would be correctly pluralised as Hippocampuses, I think,Æ said Granger. ØI think Hippocampi would be an incorrect attempt at regularising Latin Å Hippocampus is a Greek word. Technically, you could say Hippocampodes, I suppose? Although Hippocampus is now an English word, so really, Hippocampuses is quite correct, too.Æ ØIíll take your word for it,Æ said Draco, fetching the warmed mulled wine. His mother might actually like Granger. ØThis wine has been heated by the flame of Cerridwen. We arenít sucking it out of a flask like sixteen year olds behind The Hogís Head.Æ Granger Transfigured the wrappers into handsome golden goblets. Draco wouldíve informed her that she was quite the mistress of Transfiguration herself, but he didnít want her to develop an inflated ego. She nevertheless caught the way he tested the weight of the goblets. She smiled into her scarf. ØNice sheen on the gold,Æ he admitted. ØA pretty illusion,Æ said Granger, looking pleased. ØBut thank you.Æ She paused and hesitated before adding, ØI heard youíve an interest in Alchemy, so your approval means more than the average wizardís.Æ ØMy approval should mean more than the average wizardís in all things,Æ said Draco, studying the goblet in the firelight. Granger raised her eyes to the night sky. by the incredible RunningQuill_art Draco filled their goblets with the mulled wine. ØWhile we're on the subject of Alchemy Å youíd tell me if your project involves the creation of a Panacea, wouldnít you?Æ ØLetís not get ahead of ourselves,Æ said Granger, though she was grinning. Draco was seized with sudden excitement, because if anyone could, from what heíd learned of this witch over the past five months, it was probably her. ØAre you creating a Panacea?Æ he asked, leaning towards her.
