He had a plan to pry the information out of her, however. He simply had to be patient. The fire crackled on, eating away at its remaining peat. They stared into it, and, as the night wore on, found themselves almost hypnotised by the dance of the flames. The fiddlerís song turned mournful and grave. The fire, the peat smoke, the earth Å it smelled like history, like new becoming old, and old becoming new. Perhaps it was the wine, perhaps the late hour, perhaps the lingering potency of Beltane night, but the moment took on a dreamlike quality to Draco. Granger became a chiaroscuro-painted vision of a witch, her windblown hair melding into the shadows behind her, her eyes catching the red firelight. Her hands were stretched to the fire and it seemed to Draco that the flames were attracted to her, and that she could have stroked them, if sheíd wanted to. Granger yawned and the spell was broken. Her sleepiness wasnít a surprise. It was edging towards Dracoís usual bedtime, which meant that it was far past Grangerís. She put her mittens back on and cast a warming charm around herself and Draco. The fire was low, but still burning. Peat fires, Draco realised, took a very long time to go out. Granger fell asleep on his shoulder. Draco, who had himself been growing tired, suddenly found himself alert and ill at ease. This was an entirely new display of vulnerability that he wasnít prepared to deal with. Her breathing was slow and steady, her mittens curled into her lap. Dracoís Transfiguration skills were decent, but not good enough to Transfigure a tent out of the remains of a cured meat packet. He settled for elongating Grangerís ottoman into a kind of lopsided chaise longue. She slid into the new configuration without waking. Then, because she seemed small and even more vulnerable lying supine under the open sky, he threw his cloak over her. He topped this off with another warming charm over the two of them, since the dying fireís warmth was decidedly giving way to the nightís chill. He cast a few wards, in case his own fatigue took over and he, too, fell dead to the world. It was most certainly excessive prudence, as the other celebrants had retreated into their tents, but Draco hadnít survived this long by being careless. He sat with his back against Grangerís chaise longue and watched the last of the flames turn to embers. After another hour, the edge of the pit had turned to ash. It stirred in the silent breeze, then settled, white upon white. art by Ghoulsed ~ Dawn broke fresh and bright, spilling gold over the Orkney Islands under the cries of wheeling seabirds. Draco awoke with a crick in his neck and a nose gone numb from the cold. As for Granger, she looked perfectly comfortable, tucked up in his cloak. Draco wondered when he had become such a virtuous fucking martyr, sacrificing his own comfort for bloody Granger of all people? He stomped off on frozen feet to take a piss. When he returned, Granger was up and examining his Transfiguration handiwork. After the first sip, Draco felt slightly less inclined to murder everyone. Granger annoyed him afresh by not being where he had left her. After a brief, wand-grippy search, he found her a few fires over, speaking with a couple dismantling their tent. She forestalled his lecture with news: the ferry back to Thurso would be here in fifteen minutes. To Draco, this was merely good news, as he didnít fancy another flight in his sleep deprived state. To Granger, it was excellent news. She even asked to carry Old Glory to the dock, wanting to return the broomstick to the ferry master, and rid herself of it forever. They wandered through the weathered standing stones to the vestigial docks. Granger was lively and bouncy and gave Draco an unasked-for history of Orkneyís Neolithic peoples, using the broom to point at areas of interest on the monoliths. Seeing that Draco did not match her enthusiasm, she gave him her own coffee to pep him up further, and most of her croissant. The sea breeze picked up as they neared the shore, a beautiful mix of salt and sand and new grass. They boarded the ferry. Old Glory was reunited with her master. Draco said to keep the deposit. He and Granger had a dispute over whether or not she owed him any money, as she tried to pay him back. He shut her down by threatening to buy the broom outright and kidnapping her for further flights if she didnít leave off. Then, as the ferry reached open waters, he kipped down on a bench for a well-deserved nap. Granger quietly Transfigured the benchís wood top into plush velour when she thought heíd fallen asleep. ~ ØWho knew the Knob would offer such an excellent breakfast?Æ exclaimed Granger, piling scrambled eggs onto a piece of toast. Draco choked on his coffee and asked her to warn him when she was going to say things like that. Granger looked prim and said it wasnít her fault that he interpreted innocent remarks as boorishly as possible. She began a spontaneous lecture on cervical muscle spasms, pondered the health of his spinal accessory nerve, described in detail what she would do to his sterno-cleido-mastoid, if only he would let her, and generally badgered him until he was no longer enjoying his eggs. ØFine,Æ snarled Draco, shrugging off his cloak and pulling his robes aside to expose his neck. You wouldíve thought heíd given her a great gift, permitting her to help him. That was not a feeling that Draco liked; in fact, it was a real manifestation of his nascent trust in her that he allowed it at all. Her being nice gave him the heebie-jeebies. ØGo home, Granger,Æ he said. Granger threw a fistful of powder into the flames. ØOkay. Iím glad you came. Iím goingtogonowthanksagainbye. The Mitre.Æ She didnít meet his eye and turned away into the flames. A few minutes later, Draco was dusting soot from his cloak, in his own parlour. He was very much looking forward to a bath and bed. Henriette, who had materialised upon his arrival, was sent off to run the bath, as hot as she could make it. He sent her the note. Chapter11: DracoMalfoy,Oblivious Idiot Chapter Notes Earning the oblivious idiots tag pretty hard in this chapter. The dissertation on heat therapy notwithstanding, Draco had little contact with Granger through the merry month of May. He and his fellow Aurors were kept occupied by new and exciting criminal behaviours throughout the country (a wizard who had Imperiused the entirety of a Muggle village and lived as their king; werewolves targeting infants; a theft at Gringotts; a few kidnappings for variety). Mid-May found Draco cleaning up a messy case Å a potioneer in Sheffield, posing as something called a Ølove psychic,Æ selling love potions to Muggles. Draco was in the midst of confiscating a stash and Obliviating a Muggle when his wand hummed an alarm at him. That specific alarm signaled that someone was setting off Grangerís wards. And not her office or her laboratory: her home. Draco finished up with the Muggle briskly and Disapparated to the nearest Floo. That took him to the Mitre, followed by an Apparition to Grangerís cottage, wand out and Disillusioned. Between his wandís alarm and his arrival, Draco estimated that three minutes had passed. But it was three minutes too late; whoever had been poking about had left. Dracoís revelation spells indicated no human presence nearby save for Grangerís Muggle next door neighbour, who was napping. Draco cast a delicate magic detection spell. His warding around the property glowed brightly under it, but he ignored that in favour of examining the ground around Grangerís cottage. He held his wand aloft until he found what he sought: a faintly visible trace in the air, left by a being who had used magic here moments earlier. The faintly glowing trail ended suddenly in the middle of the field behind Grangerís cottage: a Disapparition or Portkey, perhaps. Draco did not like this. It might only have been a curious wizard, or even a thief Å that was the best case scenario. It might also be a first indicator that someone had their eyes on Granger and that Shackleboltís paranoia wasnít for naught. Draco sent a quick note to Granger: Someone set off your wards. We need to talk. When Granger didnít respond immediately, he checked her schedule. She was currently lecturing in Muggle Cambridge. Draco decided to join her there Å he was essentially next door, anyway. Iím coming to you, he wrote. Still Disillusioned, he Apparated to Trinity College. ~ Grangerís lecture had been on the cusp of ending. Draco only had to wait for ten minutes outside the door of the small classroom. A half-dozen students filed out as he, all but invisible to Muggle eyes, slipped through them into the room. The chalkboard indicated that the dayís topic had been åConjugated monoclonal antibodies.í Draco was pleased that these antibodies knew their verb tenses, if nothing else. Granger, unaware of his presence, was packing papers (sans wand) into a briefcase. She wore a pinstripe blouse tucked into high-waisted trousers Å pieces that Draco wouldnít have immediately thought of as complementary, and yet, on Granger, the ensemble was rather flattering. As the last student filed out, Granger pulled her Jotter out of her pocket. It gave Draco a strange pleasure to watch her flip the Jotter open and grow visibly interested when she saw that it was a message from him. She read the note and drew her eyebrows into a frown. She was bouncing one of her feet under the desk, as she was wont to do when she was irked. Again, Draco was reminded of the annoyed swish of a catís tail. She wasnít Occluding, because at some point in the past few months, she had begun to trust him. In fact, right now, Granger was in an utterly unguarded state, her gaze openly meeting his. Draco remained in his chambers until the whirlwind of Henriette and her fellow elves had abated and the first guests had arrived. Narcissa managed her guest lists with a strategy and refinement developed over decades of serving as the perfect hostess. Todayís invitees were a mix of higher level Ministry employees and academics. For Granger, the group would permit comfortable mingling with a familiar crowd. Delicately wrought silver tables, piled high with finger sandwiches and cakes, spilled out onto the terrace. Guests were shielded from the sun by floating lace parasols that drifted over them. Draco recognised some old school mates and wandered over to make small talk with Terry Boot (Department of Magical Accidents and Catastrophes), Davies (Magical Transportation), and Padma Patil (University of Edinburgh). He had half expected her to arrive in Muggle attire to make a point. However, she had gone to the effort of wearing robes for the occasion. They were a light grey-blue in the French style, accentuating her collar bones and slender neck, and tailored high about the waist. Granger looked pale but seemed preternaturally calm as she spotted Draco and asked, ØWhy am I here, again?Æ Draco enumerated possibilities on his fingers: ØA sudden interest in building bridges. Their suitcases were in the boot, their snacks were in the back seat, and the car stereo played something that wasnít Austrian folk music. Draco found it to be altogether a pleasant drive through olive groves, vineyards, and hilltops dotted with medieval ruins. Perhaps there was something to be said for the Mugglesí scenic routes, rather than the immediacy of Apparition. Granger was full of a kind of nervous energy that manifested itself in a stream of informative babbling paired with peppy driving. As they whizzed down the road, Draco remarked, ØI didnít think this car had that kind of verve. What did you put in her for petrol, Pepperup? No.Æ ØAnd you called me unsporting?Æ ØI only gave us a bit of a boost,Æ said Granger, with a triumphant glare back at the other car through the rearview mirror. Draco observed her. ØGrangerís Paradox.Æ ØI beg your pardon?Æ ØYouíre a speed demon, and yet you hate flying.Æ ØIím not a speed demon,Æ scoffed Granger. ØIím just a bit impatient.Æ ØYou ski, too. Isnít skiing rather an extreme sport? You launch yourself down the Alps at high velocity?Æ ØOnly if you put it that wayÅÆ ØFrom the top of a mountain,Æ said Draco. ØThousands of metres in the air. Brooms will take you two hundred metres up, at the most extreme.Æ ØItís different when thereís nothing below you.Æ A lengthy argument ensued. Meanwhile, the country around them grew wooded. They took a slip-road off to a rural road, winding down through gorges and then back up again. They passed through convivial medieval villages and then down a sinuous country lane, which eventually brought them to vast flatlands striped with lavender fields and, finally, the sea. ØOh, how beautiful,Æ sighed Granger, in a moment of uncharacteristic softness. ØA balm upon the soul,Æ said Draco, with enough of an edge to suggest cynicism, to cover the fact that he meant it. The picturesque town of Saint-Maximin came into view under the afternoon sun. ØWeíll stay at the hotel tonight,Æ said Granger, ØAnd weíll do the walk up and the Å the other activity Å tomorrow morning.Æ Draco felt her give him a sidelong look, to which he quirked an eyebrow. ØWhat?Æ ØThe nicer hotels were all booked up, so donít be a prat about the quality of the place. Itísè older. The restaurant is apparently lovely, though.Æ ØIs the hotel run by ogres?Æ ØOf course not Å this is a Muggle town.Æ ØThen itíll be fine.Æ ØYouíve stayed in a place run by ogres?Æ ØYes,Æ said Draco. ØA stakeout in Budle. I did learn a bed bug extermination spell as a result, so weíll be sorted if you feel anything scurrying up your legs tonight.Æ ØEurgh,Æ shuddered Granger. Grangerís mobile, which had been serving as a kind of live map for the duration of the drive, suddenly announced that the Hotel Plaisance was coming up on their right. The hotel was old and tired, but beautifully situated.The small foyer was packed with other arrivals, all of whom were being served by a single, hard-of-hearing old woman, who moved with all of the agility of a mollusc. Eventually, it was their turn, and the woman gave them the key to their room and took their names down for a dinner reservation. The tiny room had a bed of questionable structural integrity, a lamp, a caved-in sofa, and an afterthought of a bathroom. There was a vague, fusty scent to the room, as though someone's great aunt had sprayed perfume and then died there in sad circumstances. ØAll mod cons, Granger,Æ said Draco as they took stock. ØSea view, at least?Æ said Granger, banging open the shutters to air things out. The bed squeaked as Draco sat on it and then sank almost to the ground, with intimations that it was planning on collapsing entirely under his weight as soon as he was asleep. Granger observed Draco where he sat, his knees almost at his chin. ØThe bed is yours,Æ she said with what sheíd no doubt intended to sound like generosity. It sounded rather strategic to Dracoís ear. She had her eye on the sofa. ØIíll Transfigure this into something serviceable for myself.Æ ØSomething serviceable,Æ repeated Draco, as Granger undertook a complex, ten minute Transfiguration exercise, turning the sofa into a lovely, cushy-looking bed, in a regal burgundy. Granger missed the raillery. ØThat should do it,Æ she said, slightly breathless from the magical exertion. ØNow. I should like a shower. What are your plans for the evening? Dinnerís at eight.Æ ØMy job,Æ said Draco, already warding the window. ØIím going to have a wander. Iíll meet you back here at quarter to.Æ ØAll right,Æ said Granger. She had pulled out a list. ØWhatís that?Æ ØMy itinerary for the evening,Æ said Granger. Ø...You only have three hours,Æ said Draco. Even from across the room, the list looked long. ØI know. Iíd better crack on. There are so many lovely little museums and bookshops Å and of course, the basilica.Æ Granger grappled with her luggage, pulled out a change of clothes, and stepped into the bathroom. Draco left her to it and stalked the hotel's dingy halls, warding as he went. He did not find any baddies. There were only Muggles present. Grangerís plan, at least for Day One, was unfurling smoothly. Tomorrow was an entirely different story, of course. Draco returned to their room to read the tome on warding heíd brought with him. Granger had already left Å all the better for him to squeeze in another bit of studying. He kicked off his shoes and stretched out on Grangerís bed, the book floating above him as he flicked through pages. Draco had been focusing his study on warding techniques on the Continent, but especially on the work of magical religious orders. He hoped that his readings on the warding systems of Cistercian and Dominican Monks would, at the very least, give him a clue tomorrow when he uncovered whatever the Benedictine Sisters had cast around their beloved relics. As promised, Granger returned at a quarter to eight. She saw him reading and immediately made a beeline for him. ØOoh, whatíve you got there?Æ ØStudying for tomorrow,Æ said Draco. ØGive me a minute Å I found something interesting.Æ Granger approached the bed to read the title of his book. Out of the corner of his eye, Draco saw that she had changed into a white, breezy sundress. Her hair was tied into a plait, though it was slowly unraveling. She smelled like sun on skin and something sweet. He took a deeper breath. Almonds? She was chewing on something. Draco held out his hand, his focus still on the book. ØNone left,Æ said Granger. Draco floated the book lower so he could look into her eyes. ØLiar.Æ Granger sighed and pulled out a crinkly bag. ØDatte fourre  la pte d'amande.Æ Draco took the proffered marzipan-stuffed date. It was exquisite. ØMm,Æ said Draco. ØBless the French.Æ He resumed his reading, but only for a moment, because Granger was hovering over the book in a jealous sort of way. He floated the book lower again. ØYes?Æ ØCould I have a look?Æ asked Granger. ØYou can have it after dinner,Æ said Draco, floating the book back up again. Granger perched a thigh on the side of the bed. ØCan I help you?Æ asked Draco, observing this activity. ØBudge up,Æ said Granger. ØWe can both read.Æ ØNo, we canít. Personal space, Granger,Æ said Draco, making a shooing motion with his hand. ØThis is my bed,Æ noted Granger, quite rightly. Draco shifted over with a grumble (there wasnít much room to shift). ØWeíre about to go to dinner.Æ ØBut you found an Interesting Thing,Æ said Granger. Her eyes were alight with curiosity. She squeezed onto the bed next to him. The book floated above them. ØThis isÅÆ began Draco. ØQuiet,Æ said Granger. ØIím reading.Æ Draco lapsed into annoyed silence. Granger did not read, by the way Å Granger devoured. Her reading speed outpaced Dracoís by fifty percent at his best guess, and he was himself a fast reader. However, he did not turn the pages to cater to her pace; he gave her a moral lecture about the importance of Absorbing the Information and Savouring the Text instead. She responded with a long and dramatic sigh. Draco felt the expansion of her chest against his side. That made him aware that Granger was there in a different way than her impatient presence. It made him alert and twitchy, because he was lying down on a bed with a woman, and that woman was Hermione Granger. If heíd ever been mad enough to imagine such a scene, he wouldíve pictured a moment of recoil, of distaste, probably, at this level of closeness with his childhood enemy. Instead, she felt warm, and she smelled like sunshine and almonds, and her hair was touching his neck, and it was intimate and strange. He felt a kind of pleasurable paralysis, of not wanting to breathe, of not daring to move and accidentally touching her too much, or worse, causing her to move away. He turned the page, with no idea what he had just read. His eyes kept drifting from the book above them to her legs, which were bent at the knee, with one leg crossed over the other. Her dress was bunched up at her thighs, covering anything of interest Å there was nothing indecent about any of it, really Å and yet it felt illicit and thrilling, to see Grangerís legs from here. She had kicked off her sandals to join him on the bed. He could see the delicate arch of her bare foot, the tan lines where the sun had kissed her and then worked around the straps, the pink-painted toes. The delicate foot started to bounce. ØYouíre doing it on purpose to annoy me, being this slow,Æ said Granger. Draco snapped his eyes back to the page. ØNo, I am being attentive.Æ Granger waved her wand to tell the time Å it was eight on the dot. ØUgh. We've got to go.Æ She rose and slipped on her sandals. ØThe Caleruega warding technique sounds terribly sensitive. Do you think the Sisters might be using that?Æ ØThey might be,Æ said Draco. (He found that his brain was working in a kind of slow motion; it was still processing her thighs and the bunched-up dress, and had not yet joined him in the present.) ØWeíll have to be ever so careful tomorrow, if those things are as hair-trigger as this text suggests.Æ Granger was redoing her plait. Draco got a whiff of her shampoo. That brought his brain back to the present, because it liked it. She was still going on about the chapter they had just read, and whether Draco felt that he needed more preparation, and whether they should revisit the plan, and if so, which parts they should modify. Perhaps she should feign illness in the monastery to distract the Sisters while Draco went into the crypt, to buy him more time? But no, he hadnít studied the maps as she had; it had taken her weeks to memorise the labyrinthine paths, &c. &c. Which was excellent, as it gave Draco time to Get a Grip. What the fuck was wrong with him? He went to the bathroom to splash some cold water on his face, and hopefully some sense into his brain. They made their way downstairs to dinner. ~ The restaurant was all a-bustle. It was a lovely outdoor installation on a long kind of wharf that extended out into the sea, crammed with as many tables as possible. Draco and Granger threaded their way through the other patrons to a table for two at the end of the wharf. It being midsummer, the sun was still hanging over the horizon at this late hour, tinging the sea gold and orange. It was an utterly gorgeous June evening; the breeze played lazily with their hair, the sea splashed along the edge of the wharf in musical little wavelets, the sea-birds weaved their wheels above. As it turned out, the half-deaf old lady who had taken their reservation had creatively interpreted their names. The slate placard indicated that the table was reserved for Hormone et Crotch. A solemn waiter came by to light the candles on the table. Grangerís lips were pressed tightly together. Draco felt an uncomfortable bubbling of hilarity. ØMonsieur, the wine list,Æ said the waiter, handing it to Draco. ØMerci,Æ said Draco. The waiter recommended the red; Draco went with that, because all of his brain power was focused on not bursting into a scream of laughter. Grangerís eyes flitted back to the placard. She let out a gurgle that she turned into a kind of cough. The waiter enumerated the eveningís menu. Granger nodded her approval of the buttered sole while Draco croaked out a yes for the fillet mignon. Granger was biting one of her knuckles. Draco heard her undertaking a deep breathing exercise. Finally, the waiter left. Granger collapsed onto the table. ØCrotch,Æ she gasped, attempting to breathe. ØH-Hormone?Æ wheezed Draco. Granger was a boneless mass of restrained laughter. Her shoulders shook. Draco fell back into his chair and actually felt himself disintegrate into mirth. ØMy god,Æ breathed Granger. ØOh my godè whyè whyèÆ Draco attempted to sober up, but then he looked at the placard again, and Crotch looked back at him in a beautiful flowing script, and he brought his napkin to his mouth to muffle himself. A few heads from the tables around them turned her way. ØRight.Æ Her face still hidden in her hands, Granger breathed. ØHide the placard. I canít see it again. I will die.Æ Draco flipped the slate so that it was face down. ØDone, HÅÆ ØDonít say it,Æ said Granger. The waiter returned, bearing bread, butter, and wine. ØMerci,Æ said Granger, wiping at a tear. As for Draco, he could hardly feel his cheeks. He gestured to the waiter to leave the wine bottle. After a bit more breathing, both of them had regained their self-control Å well, mostly. Granger was avoiding looking anywhere near the slate. The sea caressed the rocky edges of the wharf below them. The patrons chattered, as did the gulls. The sun tilted lower. The bread was split and buttered and Draco poured the wine. ØCheers,Æ said Granger. ØTo success tomorrow,Æ said Draco, tilting his glass into hers. The final trace of amusement vanished from Grangerís face. She grew serious. Draco eyed her. He cast a silencing charm around them. ØYouíre nervous.Æ ØYes,Æ said Granger. Anxiety tightened the corners of her mouth. ØA lot could go wrong and, to be honest, it frightens the bollocks off of me. And if it doesnít, youíll set fire to the place and we can go steal a better skull. Æ An amused huff escaped Granger in the face of this cavalier attitude. He shared two or three of his favourite stories, which prominently featured his own heroics and wits. Granger was not the captive, eyelash-fluttering audience that he usually shared these tales with, however. She was analytical and inquisitive, and asked some rather penetrating questions. Why didnít he Silence the Sirens first? The knife fight was thrilling, but how did he let himself get disarmed in the first place? Why didnít his emergency kit include blood replenishing potions? Shouldnít all Aurors have a basic knowledge of the properties of Aconite? Why hadnít he used a nerve agent on the troll? Why, indeed? Draco parried, and countered, and justified, and defended, until Granger was satisfied. He poured himself a second glass of wine, finding himself rather wrung-out and thirsty after the interrogation. His tales were usually followed by praise and gushing, and starry-eyed gaspings about his bravery and sagacity. With Granger? Not a chance. ØAt least one of us will be feeling confident, which is better than none,Æ was her closing remark. She drained her glass of wine. Draco offered to refill it and she acquiesced, saying that she needed it for emotional support. The waiter arrived with their orders. It was about time; Draco was ravenous. ØI think I can see the monastery.Æ Draco turned around in his chair to look at the sandy-grey protrusion jutting from a distant cliff, above the tree-line. ØMy word. The witch across from him was now holding back a grin. ØYouíre the inventor of the bloody Jotters?Æ ØYes,Æ said Granger. ØNo.Æ ØYes.Æ ØNo.Æ ØYes.Æ Granger looked terribly amused. ØExplain,Æ said Draco. Granger settled into a pose that Draco could only describe as professorial. She crossed her legs and held up her fork, ready to point to an invisible blackboard. ØInstantaneous communication systems really took off in the Muggle world about 10 years ago. They already had a leg up over wizards with the telephone for the entirety of the twentieth century, but when email became common, and texting, and, later, instant messaging, wizarding communication methods went from old fashioned to utterly archaic. Iíd already experimented with rudimentary Magical communication methods as a child Å those Galleons, during the war Å but I knew there had to be something more elegant, that retained that tactile feeling of parchment or a notebook, but that would be far more immediate than Owling.Æ Here Granger was interrupted by the waiter removing their empty plates. She accepted the dessert menu, then continued. ØI love owls; I find them so quaint and dear, but so slow. Donít look cross, they are slow Å you said so yourself not a moment ago. And Flooing is only convenient if youíre near a connected hearth. I created the Jotters to supplement those means of communication, not replace them Å I do love writing a good letter. I never expected them to be as popular as they are. The twins helped me bring them to market and they get a percentage of the profits.Æ Draco kept his features schooled into something neutral. The other option was a bug-eyed stare. Not only was this woman frighteningly intelligent, but she was also absolutely minted. Everyone had a Jotter. His own mother had a Jotter and was, judging by the buzzing in his pocket, rapidly gaining proficiency. Granger must be rolling in Galleons. Small wonder she handed off a sackful to a hag without a second thought. ØSo this is how youíre funding your bloody project,Æ he said at length. ØAmongst other things, yes. Iíve spent enough time under the tyranny of granting agencies to enjoy the independence.Æ ØBut Å everyone thinks the Weasley brothers invented the Jotters. Why arenít you claiming credit? Theyíre revolutionary.Æ ØTheyíre really not,Æ said Granger. ØMuggle equivalents are ever so much more advanced Å they can send each other photos and media and data of all sorts. They can have live calls with hundreds of participants. The Jotters are rudimentary. An improvement, but rudimentary.Æ Here Granger gave a shrug. ØThe bar was rather low. And as for the credit Å Iíve had my time in the limelight. Iím not in it for the glory. I saw a problem that was in my capacity to fix.Æ ØIs that what your project is about, too?Æ asked Draco. ØA problem that is within your capacity to fix?Æ ØExactly.Æ Granger regarded him seriously, now. ØI neednít tell you that Iíd prefer the truth about the Jotters to remain between us. ØNo. Developing new therapies is terribly expensive.Æ ØIs it?Æ ØYes.Æ Granger began to enumerate costs on her fingers, until she ran out of fingers. ØMaterials, space, laboratory staff, medical leads, legal staff, protocol writers, data scientists, staticiansè testing for safety and efficacy is spendy too, of course Å pharmacokinetic studies, preclinical toxicology testing, bioanalytical testing, and the clinical trials themselves. And the financial outlay to meet every requirement of the GCP, the GMP, the GLP, the MHRA, and the EMA is eye-watering.Æ Draco, whose eyes had largely glazed over, said, ØOh.Æ Granger shifted in her seat in a discontented sort of way. ØMy project involves complex biologics that are commercially unattractive and nigh incomprehensible to the monumentally idiotic wizards who hold the national purse strings for magical research. So I am very much on my own. On my own and, frankly, at rather an embryonic stage. Iím still doing in vitro research, trying to confirm that my target can actually be affected by an exogenous compound in the first place. Money doesnít solve all problems, unfortunately.Æ The waiter returned to take their dessert orders. Granger flinched out an apology, having forgotten to even look at the menu, and made a haphazard selection of crme caramel. Meanwhile, Draco was struggling to understand the paradoxical phenomenon that was Granger. She could have been wealthy Å extravagantly so. And yet, she chose to fund her research instead of enjoying a life of leisure. She worked approximately twelve jobs. She couldíve had her own country house, but she lived in a cramped cottage in the outskirts of Cambridge. She could have a full staff of house-elves, but she only had a cat and a grim tin of tuna in her cupboards. It made no sense. And yet, as Draco considered what he knew of the witch in front of him, it sort of did. She was too driven for a life of leisure. Too grounded for the extravagance of large homes and house-elves. Too much of a Do-Gooder to do anything but Good with that money. It was all terribly laudable. Dreadful, really. Granger cleared her throat. Draco realised that heíd been staring at her, and that the waiter was staring at him. ØMonsieurís dessert selection?Æ ØWhat sheís having,Æ said Draco. ØUne crme caramel pour Monsieur Crotch,Æ said the waiter, inscribing this precious information upon his notepad with care. Granger touched eyes with Draco. She held a hand to her mouth. The waiter left. Granger squeaked out a giggle, struggled to control it, took a large breath, and was still. ØHormone,Æ said Draco. Granger collapsed into a fit of uncontrollable giggling. ØI told you not to do that,Æ she gasped, coming back up for air. ØThere is something gratifying about making you utterly lose it.Æ Granger sniffed and dabbed at her lashes with a napkin. ØItís a rare sight, I hope youíre appreciating it.Æ ØI am,Æ said Draco. And he was. Grangerís dark eyes were bright with laughter. Her cheeks were flushed, her lips were reddened by wine. Her hair in its loose plait snaked down to her waist, a dark line against her white sundress. Her legs were curled under her; she looked dainty and fragile, and small enough to fit perfectly into a man's lap, if a man were thinking about such things. (Draco certainly wasn't.) And the candlelight loved her. It kissed at her forehead and flickered warm touches across her collarbone. It danced in her eyes. The effect was enchanting. Draco sank, unaware, into a state of soft fascination. An accordionist began to play, somewhere near the hotel, filling the air with romance. ØMonsieur, your crme caramel.Æ The return to reality was jarring. ØMerci,Æ said Draco, instead of sod the fucking crme caramel. Granger was eating her dessert, blissfully unaware of Dracoís reverie, thank the gods. He decided to blame the wine for making him such a daft, moon-eyed cretin tonight. That and too few recent shags, clearly, if he was going to faff off and daydream about Granger, of all of the witches in his life. It would help if she didnít look like a lovely Grecian dryad tonight, about to join Artemisí retinue. Since when was Granger beautiful? What an aggravating development. ØAre you all right?Æ asked Granger. ØWhy?Æ asked Draco, injecting some irritation into the syllable, to sound Absolutely Normal. ØRather uncharacteristic.Æ There were other things going on that were rather uncharacteristic, but if that was the only one that the Brain was catching on to, that was fine by Draco. ØIím savouring it,Æ said Draco. He took a slow bite to demonstrate. Grangerís eyebrow twitched. ØStop that.Æ ØStop what?Æ asked Draco. ØBeing indecent with the spoon.Æ ØI am using the spoon. Anything else is a figment of your imagination.Æ Granger narrowed her eyes at him. Draco took another slow bite, maintaining an obnoxious level of eye contact. Granger looked away. ØYou arenít going to finish?Æ ØNo. Do you want it?Æ ØIíd rather you choke it down and have strength for the monastery. Draco found himself observing her now with a critical eye. When he had first met her, in long-ago January, he had been struck by the exhausted thinness that had made her face severe and gaunt. It seemed to him that she had a slightly healthier mien, now Å but only slightly. She was a little less bony, a little rosier in the cheeks. Granger gestured to the waiter for the bill. ØLíaddition, síil vous plat.Æ Her raised arm made Draco realise that her dress had left her arms bare, something that Grangerís choice of attire normally trended against. And now, precisely because it was trying not to catch his attention, her left forearm caught his attention Å there was a Notice-Me-Not charm there. He deliberately looked at the table next door, allowing Granger and her arm to slip into his peripheral vision. There: a blur across the skin of her inner arm. He realised what the glamour was covering with a sickening plummeting sensation in his stomach. A vivid memory returned, of Bellatrixís handiwork, stark against Grangerís skin. Of Granger, limp and wrought-out, lying like a dead thing on the drawing room floor. Of the blood oozing out of the fresh-carved letters. Draco had never used the word Mudblood again, after that. Now there was something terribly sorrowful in Grangerís habit of wearing long sleeves. In the discreet glamour that sheíd cast to be able to wear a pretty dress. Draco hid his own inner arm shame from the world, but he wouldíve thought that Granger, of all people, wouldíve been able to heal hers away. Clearly, she still bore the mark of Bellatrixís knife. ØMalfoy?Æ Draco blinked. ØHm?Æ ØYouíve gone quiet.Æ Granger had settled the bill with Muggle money. She was rising from her chair. Draco rose with her. ØJust thinking about tomorrow.Æ But really, he was thinking about a distant yesterday, when this witch had been mutilated in the halls of his home. And she still bore the scar, and she hid it, from him, and from everyone, but it was still there. A daily reminder for her, of cruelty and sick hate. Of how close sheíd come to death. Of how near their world had come to a point of no return. He wished to say something to her Å words of sorrow, or of apology Å but such words did not come easily to him, and he couldnít see such a conversation go anywhere but difficult, awkward places. As they weaved their way through tables back off the wharf, Draco concluded that this was not quite the moment. But, watching the blur of the glamour brush against her skirts as she walked, he determined that there would be A Moment, and he would find the words. Not tonight, but some night. The sun was finally setting, languidly, lazily, on this gorgeous evening, Midsummer less a day. Granger was looking wistfully along the rocky beach. ØThereís meant to be a marker along there, where the Magdalene wouldíve first set foot in France.Æ ØI suppose that was on your itinerary?Æ ØIt was, but I ran out of time.Æ ØLetís go,Æ said Draco. Granger looked at him in surprise. ØYouíd come?Æ Draco gave her his most nonchalant shrug. ØI fancy a walk.Æ Grangerís surprise turned to a prudent kind of delight. ØAll right, itís about a fifteen minute ramble, that way. So the guidebook said, anyway.Æ They clambered and slid down large boulders to the rocky beach, where they found a kind of coastal path. Granger led Draco along, pointing out features of geological or historical interest as they went. The views became progressively more dramatic as they left the shallow bay that the hotel was nestled in and made their way around the headland. The tide began to come in. Draco rolled up his trousers and his shirtsleeves (ensuring, on the latter point, that his own glamour was in place), then tied his shoes together, and slung them over his shoulder. Granger carried her sandals hooked through her fingers. They splashed through salty rock pools, as warm as bathwater. The sound of the accordion on the wharf faded away; now it was only the heart-pulse of the waves. They meandered into a flock of hundreds of seabirds, which took off around them and unravelled into the skies in a whirr of wingbeats and sea-cries. It was a startling moment of sublimity that took a bit of their souls with it. Granger watched the birdsí disappearance into the blue with a soft sigh, her fingertips on her collarbone, her lips parted. Granger said, ØBeautiful,Æ and Draco said, ØYes,Æ but they were not talking about the same thing. They continued. The marker for the Magdaleneís point of arrival was a modest stone, half-buried in sand, at the tip of the headland. A few cut flowers were scattered about, as well as candles gamely fighting to stay lit in the breeze. Granger furnished Draco with a great many details about the legend of the Magdaleneís expulsion from the Holy Land, and what disciples were with her, and when she had reached this shore. Draco cared little for the details, but he was glad of the excuse to keep his attention on her, on the way the wind wended her plait hither and thither, on her bare legs trickling with seawater. At one point, she almost lost her balance on the wet stones and her fingers touched his arm. They were quickly withdrawn. Draco said he supposed that there were worse places to land than Provence. Granger said she thought so, too. Draco asked whether the Magdalene wouldíve eaten marzipan-stuffed dates when she was here. Granger fancied that she was the one who had brought the recipe over from the Holy Land, in the first place. Draco said that stealing the credit for such a sublime culinary creation was a classically French thing to do. Granger agreed. Then they lapsed into silence and they stood where the land met the sea, and breathed the sweet air, and were tickled by the salt breeze. Little waves strained to reach past their knees before atomising into brine. Draco found a seastar. Granger was delighted by the discovery and squatted down to look at it, and interrogated Draco on what species it was, and Draco said he hadnít a sodding clue. They turned to walk back to the hotel, splish-splashing through the warm tide pools, wavelets clinging foamily at their ankles. Their hands brushed a time or two, and they said sorry, and stepped away from each other, and kept walking, and then their elbows brushed, by accident, because theyíd drifted together again. The large boulders near the wharf presented more difficulty for Granger on the way up than down; she stood, irresolute, grasping the wand in her pocket, but there were Muggles about, and her plans to Transfigure a stairway were interrupted. Draco came up behind her and lifted her in one smooth motion, and received an indignant squeal and a face-full of sandy skirts for his troubles. Her waist felt narrow and taut between his palms, and warm. He didnít need her help to clamber up behind her, but he nevertheless accepted the small hand she reached towards him and took amusement in the serious effort she put into her pull. They meandered back towards the hotel. The sun poured gold across the horizon. With her hair strewn with crypt dust, and a purplish line of Mind Flayer residue across her cheek, and her torn-up walking kit, she looked rather like a wild-eyed archaeologist, seeking answers amongst the endless sands. The effect was quite fetching. Thoughtful sort, that front desk witch. Deciding that Granger had been sufficiently provided for, Draco went off to shower. Beastly. ØAm I ready? No. But I must get on. I have so much to do, now that I have the fragment. And Crooks will be waiting, you know.Æ Draco strode to the mirror to cover his mild disappointment. (Granger stuck her head into the bathroom and corrected this non-error.) It took about a quarter of an hour for the clothing to be sent up Å Draco supposed that the unusual request mustíve sent the hotelís house-elves all of a dither. Eventually, their dinner things faded off of the table, and two tidy packages surfaced. The staid establishment had sent up equally sedate attire. The clothes were in the traditional style, with a great many buttons for Draco, and a great deal of lacing up for Granger. ØWell,Æ said Granger, eyeing her dark blue robes, ØItíll do to get me to the Floo, anyway.Æ ØLook Å underthings,Æ said Draco, holding up a spectacularly unsexy pair of bloomers. ØYou can look like my great aunt Auriga.Æ ØUgh Å no.Æ Draco added the bloomers to the orphan burning pile. Granger went into the bathroom to change, while Draco made relatively quick work of his new attire, save for the buttons, which were too finicky for him to fasten using his wand. He did them halfway to his throat, and then decided that he didnít care enough to do them up further. They were only making themselves decent for a walk across the foyer to the Floo, after all. Granger popped out of the bathroom with a similar problem, though hers consisted of ribbons and laces. ØI see that these robes come with an assumption that the wearer will have a lady-in-waiting. Would you help?Æ Draco, having no idea how to tie proper knots for a ladyís robe, opted to grasp a handful of the ribbons and shove them into the back of the dress. And he did not spend a moment thinking about how Granger was not wearing underthings, thank you. ØThat doesnít feel quite right,Æ said Granger as the ribbons were stuffed in. ØNo. Itís utterly shambolic.Æ ØThe naughty bits are covered, thatís what matters.Æ They stopped in front of the mirror for a look before they descended to the foyer. Draco said that Granger looked terribly like a Pure-blood wife, off to drop off the sprogs at Kingís Cross, in 1961. Granger said that Draco looked like heíd just exited Scotland Yard in 1825. The mirror chimed in to register its opinion that they were ØAn exceedingly handsome couple.Æ Granger shuddered; Draco ran away. The Senecaís foyer was irritatingly busy. Draco, doing mental arithmetic involving two sunsets, realised that it was only Saturday night in London. The crowds made more sense, then; the Senecaís dining rooms were the place to be for a certain slice of Londonís wizarding set. The Floo hearth was just across the foyer, crackling cheerfully at them as they exited the lift. Grangerís stride lengthened. ØWe can finally put this surreal day behind usÅÆ Then she stopped, and grasped Dracoís arm in her pinchy grasp, and whipped him towards her. ØWhatÅÆ ØShush,Æ said Granger, flattening herself against the wall and manoeuvering Draco to stand in front of her. ØStand there.Æ ØWhat are weÅÆ ØBe big. Why are you always large and in the way, except when I need you to be?Æ asked Granger in a peevish whisper. ØShield me.Æ ØFrom who?Æ asked Draco, dearly wishing to turn around and assess the Secret Assassin, and perhaps murder him in cold blood. ØCormac.Æ ØMcLaggen?Æ ØHow many other bloody Cormacs do you know?Æ asked Granger. She raised her hands to Dracoís robes and pulled up his collar, as though the flaps would afford her more privacy. ØWhatís he done?Æ ØOh, heís only been infatuated with me for years. Tenacious kind of man. Sticky. Viscous, really. Stay there, his group is about to go into the dining rooms. No Å wait Å theyíre still talking. Iím going to cast a Notice-Me-Not. Oh no Å Derrickís just spotted you, I think. Itís your stupid hair. Like a beacon across the bloody Pennines. No. Theyíre coming this way. I was never here. Goodbye.Æ With that, Granger slipped under Dracoís arm and attempted to dart back towards the lift, but it opened and a veritable flood of ladies and gentlemen ready for dinner poured out, and battered her to the side like a piece of flotsam. Granger Disillusioned herself and asked why the lift was a bloody bollocksing clown car. Draco, having gathered that his role was now to distract and deflect, turned around and greeted the approaching Derrick with a handshake (ØPeregrine, my little lamb chop, how are you?Æ) and McLaggen with a very long double handshake: ØHello; I donít believe weíve met, Draco Malfoy Å yes, I know I need no introduction Å are you here for dinner with this scoundrel? And besides, Granger had felt unsafe and fled from this man, and given that his Principal had felt threatened, Draco was well within his rights to investigate. This sound reasoning completed, Draco touched at McLaggenís mind to see if this wanker had the slightest training in Occlumency. He did not. Draco made a few remarks on the recent win of the Kestrels over the Cannons. McLaggen was certain that Draco had been snogging someone, and almost certain that it had been Granger. The cognitive dissonance of the latter point rang throughout the memory. Then Draco found associated memories: Granger speaking at the Ministry a year or two ago and then fleeing from McLaggenís amorous attentions; Granger giving McLaggen flustered excuses to avoid a dinner date as he grasped at her hand; Granger at a pub with her friends, cornered by McLaggen near the loo and fending off his drunken kiss, something like fear in her eyes. Every memory was tinged by McLaggenís mounting frustration, longing, and a chilling ongoing obsession with Granger. Draco fought a very real urge to break McLaggenís handsome jaw. Any further delving into the wizardís memories would bear the risk of discovery. Draco retreated from his mind and seamlessly rejoined the conversation with a snide remark on the performance of the Kestrelsí Chasers. Meanwhile, he added McLaggen to his blacklist. They said their goodbyes. Draco grinned as he shook McLaggenís hand. ØEnjoy your dinner. See you at the pitch, I very much hope.Æ Derrick and McLaggen left. Draco stalked off in search of Granger. ØHere,Æ hissed a familiar whisper as Draco passed the lifts. Grangerís voice led to a sort of conference room, just off the corridor. It was dark. ØIs he gone?Æ asked Granger. ØSoon,Æ said Draco. ØTheir reservation is at half-past. Where are you?Æ ØHere.Æ Granger undid her Disillusionment. ØWhy do you look so murderous?Æ ØWhat? This is my usual face.Æ ØNo: your eyes are flashing.Æ ØWe had an argument about Quidditch.Æ Grangerís dark gaze studied Draco in the penumbra. She had a hand on her hip. ØQuidditch.Æ ØYes.Æ So focused was Grangerís study of his eyes that Draco reflexively Occluded, even if Granger wasnít a Legilimens. Granger saw the change and her focus grew even more acute. ØYouíre lying.Æ ØLetís go to the Floo.Æ Granger refused to be distracted. ØWhat happened?Æ ØHow long are you going to let McLaggen terrorise you before you curse off his balls?Æ ØI knew it,Æ said Granger in a shrill mix of triumph and annoyance. ØYou used Legilimency on him. You canít do that.Æ ØI can and I did.Æ ØThose are private matters. They have nothing to do with you.Æ ØHeís a danger to you.Æ ØWhat exactly did you see?Æ ØEnough to decide that heís a threat.Æ ØA threat?Æ repeated Granger. ØHeís just a handsy idiot. I can, and always have, handled him, in the way I deem most appropriate. "And don't say his penis." ØDisgusting. I canít even walk across a room without wizards falling into my lap.Æ Draco recognised an echo of some of his own claims during their dance at the Delacroix party Å at this very hotel, in fact. She turned and disappeared into the fire. And, briefly, it felt like there was less gravity in the room. Chapter17: The Dinner/ Draco MalfoyAlmost Causes The Next MurderSensation Chapter Notes In which Draco continues to be a terribly unreliable narrator. Featuring more of my favourite fanfic moments. Please mind the slow burn tag! This is the Dramione story I wanted to read and UST is my kink (emphasis on the U). Expect false starts, hesitation, all manner of idiocy, and an eventual denouement in the endgame. This fic is not for short- or medium-term gratification seekers but rather for masochists who like to suffer. If that is you, then please read on and let us suffer together. 'The Seneca' by the wonderful RunningQuill_art ~ To Dracoís devious pleasure, McLaggen took him up on the invitation to pop by the pitch a few weeks later. An unfortunate sequence of events occurred, which had positively, absolutely nothing to do with Draco Å wet conditions, terribly aggressive Bludgers, temperamental brooms Å that resulted in McLaggen taking a tumble off his broom from 30 metres up. ØI say,Æ said Davies, watching McLaggen being carted off the pitch by mediwitches. ØThat Bludger had it in for the bloke.Æ ØI didnít even hit it that hard,Æ said Zabini. ØPoor old egg,Æ said Draco. ØFirst time back on the broom in a while, as I understand it.Æ ØPerhaps Bludgers can smell fear,Æ suggested Zabini. ØHope it doesnít put him off the sport,Æ said Davies. ØWe need a decent Keeper. Bickfordís moving to Spain.Æ The general mood was a little subdued after the accident. The players decided to call it quits for the night, said their goodbyes, and Disapparated off to shower. All except for Draco, who found, to the contrary, that the accident had had a stimulating effect on his morale. He left the pitch feeling quite invigorated. ~ Granger had a grievance to air. The timing was hideous; Draco was on a sensitive stakeout in Fowlmere, about to apprehend the notorious Thomas Talfryn. ØYou! She wanted to throttle him as much as he wanted to throttle her. Her blush carried up from her cheeks to across the bridge of her nose. Draco, feeling utterly thrown off-kilter by the Moment, cleared his throat, cast about for a thing to say (nothing was forthcoming), and then said heíd best be off, as it was getting dark. Granger looked anywhere but at him and said, ØRight.Æ Mutually satisfied with this mature, robust conclusion to their quarrel, they stepped apart even further, and Granger made as though to shut the door. There was a long, sustained meow from somewhere in the garden. It was a stark and aggravating contrast to the typical witchís reaction to a dinner invitation from Draco Malfoy, which was usually breathless yeses and a great many giggles. Not that he was inviting her to that kind of dinner. He was simply observing the distinction. The smell of burnt fish pie wafted from the bin and settled around them in a gentle, tragic aura. It spurred Granger to action. She crammed the binís lid on tightly, turned, and made for the stairs. Women didnít run from Draco, as a general rule Å quite the contrary. It was an unfamiliar and unpleasant sensation. ØOi,Æ said Draco, vexed. ØIím going to change,Æ called Granger. ØIím not going to the Manor in my house things. Besides, I stink like burnt.Æ Draco, as he watched her and her bum sprint up the stairs in her short Muggle shorts, vaguely wanted to say that he didnít object to the house things, and that it was only her and him at dinner, so it didnít matter, and plus, she often smelled a bit like candle smoke, and it didnít bother him in the least. However, Granger was upstairs, so Draco kept these mawkish sentiments to himself. He waited for her to change, which took approximately two business days. Then she came came aítumbling down the stairs, wearing a red summer dress. ØThere, now Iíll be presentable.Æ ØPresentable for whom?Æ ØI donít know,Æ said Granger, pulling her hair into a low side bun that was, somehow, both elegant and messy. ØBeing with you attracts chaos; I half expect Shacklebolt to decide to pop in for a chit.chat.Æ Draco felt that the chaos attractor was her, but, nevertheless. ØI rather hope he does. He can tell Tonks Iím building a rapport with my Principal and not being a frightful bully.Æ ØYou arenít a bully. Youíre just pushy,Æ said Granger, sliding on strappy sandals. ØIím pushy?Æ ØBit bossy, really.Æ ØOh, this is rich.Æ They Apparated to the Mitre, and from there Flooed to the Swan, and from there they Apparated to the Manor. It was the same trajectory that they had taken on that fateful night when Granger had popped into existence on the Manorís Quidditch pitch, only in reverse Å and in less frantic circumstances. This thought also seemed to have crossed Grangerís mind, as they materialised at the Manor. Just as Draco was stealing a glance at her, Granger met his eyes. Then she held up her hand. It was trembling only slightly. ØProgress,Æ said Granger. Draco said, ØWell done,Æ with quiet sincerity. The Manorís large front doors swung open at their approach. One of the younger house-elves scurried across the entrance hall with a high-pitched word of welcome Å and then he saw Granger. He squeaked in surprise, Disapparated, and then his high voice echoed from the kitchens: ØMaster is home! And he brought a lady! Make whipped cream!Æ Then the elf Apparated before them again, as though he hadnít gone anywhere in the first place. ØWelcome, Sir and Miss.Æ ØThank you, Tupey. Could you tell the kitchens that I will be joined by my colleague, Healer Granger, for dinner?Æ Draco might as well have broken the house-elfís heart with this clarification. ØOf course, Sir,Æ he said, his large eyes filled with sudden devastation. ØAnd we want to dine on the south terrace,Æ added Draco. Tupey bowed and Disapparated. Distantly, his shrill voice echoed with a request to cancel the whipped cream. Granger looked bemused. Ø...Whipped cream?Æ ØNever mind that,Æ said Draco. ØLetís have an aperitif to start. I think weíve just launched a panic in the kitchens.Æ Granger was not quite so fixated on her feet as sheíd been during her last visit. She glanced about, taking in the white walls, the enchanted clusters of candles floating every few paces, the fires ablaze in the many hearths. The new Manor was a sight less dreary than the old. Draco steered her to one of the salons, which was well-supplied with all manner of snacks. They had twenty seconds to select a seat and pick at olives before Tupey materialised again, desiring to know what theyíd like to drink. ØA cognac for me,Æ said Draco. ØAnd for Colleague Healer Granger?Æ asked Tupey. ØRed wine, please.Æ ØCabernet Sauvignon? Merlot? Pinot Noir? Malbec?Æ asked Tupey. Granger appeared paralysed by the onslaught of choices. ØEr Å Iíll try the Malbec. Thank you.Æ Tupey bowed and Disapparated. Next came Henriette, who was slightly better at concealing her excitement (only her quivering ears gave her away). ØMademoiselle Granger,Æ she said with a curtsey, before proffering a tray. ØRoulades de courgettes, noix pices au piri-piri, blinis de saumon et de chvre au pesto.Æ The tray of amuse-gueules was set to hovering next to Granger. Henriette Disapparated. Granger opened her mouth to say something, but there was another crack, and Tupey Apparated with the drinks. Draco was given his with the usual amount of politeness, but Grangerís was placed in her hand with the utmost care. Tupey Disapparated. Draco opened his mouth to speak, but a third elf Apparated from the kitchens to ask whether Colleague Healer Granger had any allergies or preferences the kitchens should be aware of? She did not. The kitchen elf Disapparated. Granger attempted a comment, but Henriette cracked into existence with serviettes and tiny forks, and Disapparated again. Draco and Granger eyed each other warily as silence descended upon the salon, half expecting another loud crack to interrupt their next attempt at conversation. ØThey are a bit Å a bit intense, arenít they?Æ said Granger. ØThey are positively itching for guests,Æ said Draco. ØWhen my mother is away, there arenít any functions to host, and thereís only me to feed.Æ ØThis entire tray is enough for dinner,Æ said Granger, selecting a salmon blini. ØEr Å no. Save your appetite.Æ They meandered towards the south terrace. It was an exquisite summer night, warm, but blessed with a sweet, playful little breeze. The breeze toyed with the escaped tendrils of Grangerís hair and tugged at the hem of her dress. Not that Draco was looking at her. The grounds were illuminated at night by enchanted candles and lanterns at the foot of every tree, and strung along all of the footpaths. In some ways, the effect was even more magnificent than during the day Å the fountains and water features shimmered and the flowers were luminous, as though glowing from within. Draco left Granger to admire a prospect of the gardens while he strode ahead to see if the table was ready. He was satisfied by what the house-elves had put together on such short notice: a silver table and two chairs, a surfeit of summer flowers lending their perfume to the night air, and a real extravagance of lanterns and fairy lights. It was, however, terribly romantic. Henriette was laying it on rather thick, given that Draco had specified a colleague. He had had countless dinners and drinks with colleagues and collaborators al fresco over the summer, and Henriette had never once seen fit to decorate with roses. Red roses. ØHenriette?Æ he called. ØOui?Æ replied Henriette, cracking into existence at his side. ØYou are a scallywag.Æ ØJe ne connais pas ce mot,Æ said Henriette, shrugging her lack of comprehension. ØThe roses, Henriette.Æ ØWhat about them, Monsieur?Æ ØThey are too much.Æ ØToo much what, Monsieur?Æ ØToo much everything, Henriette.Æ ØIl faut se laisser ensorceler, Monsieur.Æ Which was just what Draco was asking for, really Å unsolicited mysticism about allowing himself to be bewitched. ØTake them away, Henriette.Æ ØThey are at the peak of their bloom, Monsieur. It seems a pity to waste them.Æ ØNevertheless, Iíd likeÅÆ ØOh!Æ came Grangerís voice. ØThe roses!Æ Henriette gave Draco a long look which suggested that, as always, she knew best, and if heíd stop second guessing her, he would also stop making a fool of himself, the silly boy. Granger was clutching her hands together, standing before the table. ØHow beautiful! Iíve never seen this variety Å is it double-flowered? Å and the colouration, itís so deep!Æ ØItís the Apolline,Æ said Henriette. ØThe rose garden is quite resplendent with them, Mademoiselle. You should go for a walk after dinner. I am sure Monsieur would be pleased to escort you, in Madame Malfoyís absence.Æ The Monsieur in question gave Henriette a quelling look in the face of this fresh impertinence. Granger, however, found a vast delight in the idea, and said that she would adore it, and asked where the Apolline had come from, and how long they had had her, etc.? ØFood first, then feminine ecstasies about the rose garden,Æ said Draco. Granger and Henriette both regarded him coolly and made Draco feel the weight of their Low Opinion of him. Henriette indicated that she would fetch their first course. Granger took her chair with a sniff. ØI wouldnít want my feminine ecstasies to get in the way of your masculine appetites, of course.Æ Draco hid a smile in his cognac. ØAnd what do you know about my masculine appetites?Æ ØOnly that they are unrelenting.Æ ØAccurate.Æ ØAnd impair your judgement.Æ ØSometimes.Æ ØWe can only hope they will be satisfied by Henrietteís entre, then perhaps we can have a civil conversation about roses, uninterrupted.Æ ØPartially satisfied, probably.Æ Granger regarded him with narrowed eyes, as though she was detecting the double-entendre, but wasnít quite sure he meant it. Draco decided to let her stew in the incertitude. ØTourteau frais, dcortiqu par nos soins,Æ announced Henriette, as she and Tupey arrived with crab and herbed butter. They ate. Granger was dainty about it, as she tended to be, and easily distracted by long looks out past the terrace and onto the candle-lit grounds. Now she had her chin propped onto the back of her hand, and was gazing at the evanescent beauty of a row of poplars, whose young leaves shivered in the breeze like silvery medallions. Draco half wanted to interrupt her and bring her back to the important things (himself), but it was also rather nice to sit in companionable silence and sip their drinks. Dinners at the Manor were normally agenda-driven affairs, with either the guest or Draco having something to gain or something to lose. This one was unique for its lack of any of those pressures; Draco had no manoeuvering to do and knew he wasnít being manipulated against. They were merely eating together as he made small reparations for a burnt fish pie. Granger had no designs on his fortune or his person. Sometimes, being with her was easy. ØRisotto au basilic,Æ said Henriette, sweeping away their crab and depositing a plump dollop of risotto in front of them instead. Basil wafted deliciously off of it. ØHow do you know so much about roses, anyway?Æ asked Draco. ØMy mum used to grow them,Æ said Granger, with a kind of laboured insouciance. ØUsed to? Sheís quit the hobby?Æ ØI donít know. I havenít seen my parents in a few years.Æ ØOh.Æ Given that she was trying to look unaffected, Draco did not question her further on the subject, which he thought showed great delicacy. However, Granger continued. ØI Obliviated both of them during the war. Sent them to Australia to keep them safe. By the time Iíd found them again, it was too late to reverse the spell without risking damage to their minds. Theyíre living quite happily in Adelaide and have no idea that they ever had a daughter.Æ Ah, yes, just what Draco had been hoping for. Some light-hearted reminiscing about wartime tragedies. He didnít bother with words of sympathy because he didnít really do those, and she wouldnít believe he was being sincere, anyway. ØThis explains how careful you are with Obliviate,Æ said Draco. ØOh, yes. It was a hard lesson to learn. Minds Å memories Å arenít to be tampered with lightly. And I systematically dismantled eighteen yearsí worth. That had repercussions.Æ ØIt kept your parents alive,Æ said Draco. ØIt did. At a cost.Æ Granger finished her wine. ØAnyway Å itís too beautiful an evening to be maudlin. Letís talk of other things.Æ Draco finished his cognac so that her empty glass didnít feel lonely. He was sensitive that way. He eyed Granger. ØYou rather look as though you have a subject in mind.Æ ØBroken promises,Æ said Granger. Accusation lingered in the statement. Draco raised an eyebrow, feeling rather targeted. ØIíve broken a promise?Æ ØYes.Æ ØHave I? Illuminate me.Æ Just as Granger was about to speak, Tupey cracked into existence to suggest a Sauvignon Blanc for the next course, given that it was fish. Draco and Granger agreed. Tupey served the wine and Disapparated. ØThe archaeologistsí report on the Celtic ruins,Æ said Granger. ØThe ones you found under the dungeons. You never sent it to me.Æ Draco sat back in theatrical shock. ØYouíre right. I never did. Caught en flagrant dlit.Æ ØJe sais,Æ said Granger, looking grave. ØA terrible breach of trust.Æ ØWill you ever forgive me?Æ ØNo. I quite fancy holding a fearsome grudge about it. Perhaps launching a wholescale feud.Æ ØYou say that as though the houses of Granger and Malfoy werenít already feuding,Æ said Draco. ØTrue,Æ said Granger. As Granger mulled over this new complexity, Draco waved Henriette towards them and sent her to fetch the archaeologistsí report. Henriette returned with a thick roll of parchment in her hands and a quizzical look on her face. She then offered to fetch other reading materials more suited to a summer evening, such as some books of French posie? ØNo, thank you, Henriette Å that will be all,Æ said Draco. ØMademoiselle has peculiar literary tastes and prefers to read about dead monks.Æ Henriette Disapparated with a shake of her head. Granger accepted the scroll with a smile playing across her lips. ØWhen you put it that way, I most certainly sound peculiar.Æ Draco shrugged. ØPeculiar is, at the very least, not boring.Æ ØI accept your poorly delivered, backhanded excuse for a compliment,Æ said Granger, unfurling the scroll. ØI wouldnít want you to become conceited, you know.Æ ØNo. You are unerringly vigilant on that front.Æ Granger sank into the report, leaving her risotto to grow cold on her plate. She occasionally remembered Dracoís presence, which was signaled by an ØOh!Æ and then a sharing of some fascinating snippet or other. Henriette Apparated with the next course and gave Granger a reproving look when she took stock of the situation. ØMademoiselle! Jíose vous rappeler que vous tes  table.Æ Granger jumped and blurted out an apology, and tucked the scroll away. She looked abashed as Henriette took away the risotto (now a congealed lump) and replaced it with the fish. ØTurbot pol, artichauts poivrade et citron confit.Æ Henriette deposited Grangerís fish and artichokes with particular firmness, with intimations that if she did not consume it all, there would be Words to be had. The effect of Henrietteís menacing looming was somewhat lessened by the fact that her nose barely came above the table. However, Granger, wide-eyed, said the turbot looked utterly delicious, and crammed a few forkfuls into her mouth under Henrietteís watchful eye. Satisfied, Henriette Disapparated. Granger choked down her fish with the help of some wine. Draco was holding back laughter. ØYou look properly terrorised.Æ ØShe is frightening.Æ Granger cast a furtive glance over her shoulder and then looked back at him. ØAnd Iím sorry Å that was terribly rude of me. Whatever for?Æ ØI have a feeling yours will now take the shape of an eighty year old French house-elf and Iíd like to confirm the theory.Æ Granger bit at her lip to keep from smiling. ØYou think youíre terribly funny.Æ ØI am,Æ said Draco. ØAnd what form would yours take, should we go Boggart-hunting?Æ Draco sat back and steepled his fingers together. ØNow, thatís a question. I havenít encountered one since the war. She was reminiscing. ØHe nearly suffocated Ron, once. Laid down across his face while he was sleeping. Henriette returned to inspect Grangerís progress. Granger said that it was all delicious, and that the artichokes in particular were the most perfectly prepared that she had ever had the pleasure of eating. Henriette said, ØParfait. ØThere was also a box of crackers, only a few years old. Bit dusty, but still good.Æ Henriette and Tupey both looked at Granger and seemed ready to weep. ØBecause theyíre usually filled to bursting, arenít they?Æ Heíd been waiting for Grangerís under-the-table kick, and it came. He snatched her ankle in his hand and tutted. Granger tried to regain possession of her foot, but Draco informed her that being kicky meant that she had lost foot privileges. Henriette was oblivious to the exchange, too busy being distraught about why nobody was helping Mademoiselle and her empty cupboards? Tupey seemed on the verge of hyperventilation. ØI have a Modest Proposal,Æ said Draco. Grangerís leg twitched. Dracoís grasp held firm. And that was all it was Å a grasp. Her ankle was bare and soft under his palm, and his fingers were curious about the delicate shape of her bones, and what it would feel like to trace them, but he did not partake. It remained a grasp. Because this was Granger. And he had no interest whatsoever in caressing her ankle. And if he had any interest in doing so Å which he didnít Å it would be the fault of the artichokes. Granger didnít quite seem to dare demand her foot back out loud in front of Henriette, because that would lead to uncomfortable questions about why she had attempted to kick Monsieur at the dinner table, which was a gaffe far greater than reading. ØThe house-elves are bored out of their skulls without my mother and her ftes Å why donít you give them permission to pop in once or twice a week, and fill up your cupboards? Henriette and Tupey swiveled towards Granger as she spoke, hearts in their eyes at the thought of her empty pantry just waiting for attention. Henrietteís hands were pressed against her breast; Tupeyís were folded into a kind of supplication. Their swimmy eyes positively shimmered. Grangerís voice died. ØMademoiselle would never be so rude as to turn down Monsieurís offer. ØReturn my foot before I turn you into that cockroach.Æ Draco relinquished possession of her foot, probably a bit slower than necessary, the tips of his fingers brushing at her ankle as he let go. She noticed. There was a flush of pink across her cheekbones. Possibly the wine. Possibly other things. ØSheíd only spent five minutes in your precious company, not long enough to discover how endlessly aggravating you are. Like imposing house-elves on me, of all people. ØThat is a private matter and it would be best for you to forget it.Æ ØHm,Æ said Granger, studying him over her glass. They finished their desserts. Henriette materialised and kindly reminded Monsieur that he was meant to take Mademoiselle through the rose garden. Then she stood, her small hands curled onto her bony hips, and stared intimidatingly at him until he got up and offered his arm to Granger. Grangerís touch on his arm was light, at first, but after a few steps, her grip tightened. ØShit. Is the ground a bit wobbly, or am I completely battered?Æ ØWe are both steeped to the tonsils in wine,Æ said Draco. ØTupeyís attentions were Å unrelenting.Æ It was a miracle neither of them had said something tipsy and stupid yet Å but the night was young, and the path to the gardens beckoned, and the possibilities for stupidities glimmered like the candles that lined the way. They wandered through a double row of lilacs heavy with bloom. On their right stood the greenhouse, its warm glow dappled by the riot of mauve blossoms. The breeze made the blossoms quiver in a butterfly-tremble; the light shimmered across the path. In the mingling shadows, Granger held up her hand so that it was silhouetted against the light of the greenhouse. It was steady. It was her left hand that she held up. Her arm was bare and against the skin of her inner arm lay that blur. Granger turned, intending to continue down the path, but Draco interrupted by committing the first of the eveningís stupidities. Later, he would blame the wine. He took her wrist Å gently, but she nonetheless flinched Å and pulled it toward him. Granger was shocked. ØWhat are youÅ?!Æ ØI didnít realise you still had this,Æ said Draco. He turned her wrist so that the blur of the glamour caught the vacillating light. ØWell Å I do.Æ Her voice was uncertain. She stared at him with a wide-eyed wariness Å a wild thing about to pull away and run. She smelled like the sweetness of the Sauternes. Two heavy words that Draco had been carrying since Provence came out with difficulty. ØIím sorry.Æ ØIt was your mad aunt, not you.Æ ØI did nothing to stop her.Æ To this, Granger gave no answer. ØI suppose that if there was a way to heal it away, you wouldíve found it,Æ said Draco. ØI wouldíve.
