ØUgly thing.Æ The old injury stood clear on her skin, as raw as the day it had been carved. It glistened still. Dracoís mouth was cottony and dry. For a moment she was 17, lying as though dead on the drawing room floor, mere metres from where they stood. Then she was Granger again, a burning intelligence, a world-changer, but still, for all that, marked. Dracoís hold on her wrist grew a little tighter Å shame and sorrow. ØDoes it still hurt?Æ asked Draco, because it looked too raw not to. ØSometimes. Iím used to it, now. Or I just forget.Æ Draco had never had any intention to show her his own inner arm shame Å all the more shameful because it had been willingly acquired. And yet, he found himself unbuttoning his cuffs and rolling up his sleeve. What was left on his arm was a distorted, half-faded Mark. It was a grotesque mix of black flesh and raised scar tissue, now, from failed attempts to remove it. ØOh,Æ gasped Granger. ØMineís uglier. In every way, Granger. I wanted it.Æ The gasp had been shock more than horror. She was observing the twisted flesh with a Healerís eye, one that had seen worse things. Granger was silent for a long time. At length, she said, ØBut you donít want it any more.Æ ØNo.Æ ØThatís what matters.Æ ØIt doesnít erase the past,Æ said Draco. The defiled arm he held between them was an eloquent attestation of that. ØNo. But the choices youíve made since define you more than those you made then.Æ ØDo they?Æ ØYou were sixteen years old. You were Å we were all Å child soldiers flung into a war, trying to do what we were taught was right. Trying to protect our loved ones.Æ ØMust you be so terribly forgiving?Æ ØItís been fifteen years,Æ said Granger. She lowered her own arm. She looked weary. ØI can assure you that Iíve ruminated on the matter at length. Iíve forgiven those who deserve it.Æ ØIt rather interferes with my wallowing in self-reproach.Æ ØWallowing isnít productive.Æ Now it was Grangerís turn to take his wrist. She pulled it to a triangle of light amongst the shadows and leaned in to observe the Mark more closely. Draco wanted to pull away Å but she had been brave enough to let him look at hers, so he mustnít be a coward now. Her finger brushed over scarred ridges and half-melted flesh that had never felt the touch of another's hand. She looked heartsick. ØYou tried to curse it off?Æ ØYes,Æ said Draco. Amongst other things. ØYears ago, now.Æ His arm twitched under her scrutiny. He wanted to put the Mark away again: it was so ugly, so misshapen, so full of hideous memories and shame. ØI donít think thereís much I could do with this one, either,Æ said Granger. ØIn terms of Healing, I mean.Æ The thought seemed to make her sad. ØMine is a memento of some terrible decisions. Itís well deserved. Yours Å yours is a wretched tragedy.Æ ØIt is,Æ said Granger. Then she added, ØWell, theyíre both tragedies in different ways.Æ More righteous forgiveness. It made Draco want to flee. They stood in silence. And now she knew some of his griefs and he knew some of hers. There was an intimacy to it. To being seen. It was unfamiliar, tender to the touch, unnerving. They stood in silence and yet it wasnít silence, it was thick and dense and whirling. It weighed upon their eardrums and chests like a pressure. by RunningQuill_art ØI should like a pithy conclusion, or words of wisdom,Æ said Draco, to cut through it. ØYes, please,Æ said Granger. She seemed relieved. ØI meant from you.Æ Granger clasped her hands before her and looked up to the stars, as though she might find the pithiness there. ØThe Ennervated cadaver of a man who gave us these scars is quite dead.Æ ØAnd weíre alive.Æ ØI think thatís good enough.Æ Draco rolled his sleeve back down and did up his cufflinks. Granger glamoured her scar back to the discreet blur. ØItís too beautiful an evening to be maudlin,Æ said Draco. ØI do not sound that swotty,Æ said Granger. ØYou do. Shall we have a look at the roses? Have your feminine ecstasies at the ready.Æ They wandered down the path that curved through the candlelight until they reached the rose garden. At their feet, Midnight Violets peeped, here and there, lured out by the crescent moon. Their footsteps were slow and drunk and deliciously aimless. It was perfect; Draco knew too little of roses to give a real tour and Granger was content to meander from one to the next without plan nor purpose, touching at their loosely cupped petals. Pretty names fell from her lips as she recognised a few: Annabelle, the Wildfire, the Apolline, the Duchess, Ivory Kiss, Claire, Crimson Romance. Fairy lights twinkled amongst the rose bushes. Petals drifted onto the path. A nightingale sang and fountains gurgled. Granger, with a kind of dreamy-eyed tipsiness, said it was like being in an enchanted glade. Draco wanted to have a go at her for being sentimental, but he found himself also in a soft, mellow kind of mood. The kind of mood in which he might tell a witch that yes, the roses were sweet, but she was the sweetest thing in the garden, just to see her blush. He did not, because he was made of stronger stuff. Fragrances, delicate and elusive, teased at their noses. Granger tried to name the scents and held up the roses to Draco, so that he could try, and he stood next to her, closer than necessary, and they made idle guesses together Å apple, vanilla, clove, myrrh, honey Å amongst the damask. His wine-drenched mind collected impressions. Delectable nearness. Being close enough to feel her giving off warmth. The rose she held to his face, so close that his lips brushed its petals. The moonlight on her skin. The escaped curls of hair at her nape. The corner of her mouth. The biting of her lip. Eyelashes against a cheek. They moved to the next rose. This one, Granger was convinced smelled of apricots. Draco came to stand behind her and leaned over her shoulder. To him, it was tangerine. Granger smelled it again, and said no, apricots, most certainly. And Draco leaned in closer and said no Å tangerine, donít be silly. Granger theorised that they mightíve found an Amortentia rose; that would explain the discrepancy. Draco said he would be sure to record this discovery. They moved on to the next one, a splendid white rose. Granger cupped its heavy head and drew it out. Draco came behind her again and they both smelled it at the same time, and her cheek brushed his chin. He caught himself, just in time, as he was about to put a hand on her waist. That way lay madness. The curve of her skirts brushed against the front of his trousers. Her hair tickled at the side of his face. Granger said it was coconut and dared him to disagree. Draco disagreed as a matter of course Å it was kiwi. Kiwi?! repeated Granger. Kiwi, said Draco. Granger said that she would have him sent to an otolaryngologist, if he didnít stop this nonsense. Draco said the only nonsense here was the word otolaryngologist. The sweet paralysis was coming over him again, of not wanting to move, of lightness in his veins, of limbs feeling weightless and eyelids feeling heavy. He wanted to put his chin where her neck met her shoulder and just stand there. He wanted to say things in her ear and feel her shiver against him. He wanted to linger here, being stupid about kiwi, for an age or two. He wanted to float. It was the wine, certainly. And the artichokes. They moved to the next roses, small wild things that grew in bunches and smelled like vetiver. Granger asked if she might pick one. Draco did so for her; it seemed ungentlemanly not to. And he gave it to her, his arm wrapped around her from behind, and their fingertips touched, and that was as close as they could get Å touching fingertips over a rose. She looked over her shoulder at him to say thank you, and their eyes met, and hers were dark and curious and his were light and keen, and it was universes colliding. It was all of those contrapositions of Light and Dark and Muggle-born and Pure-blood and Order and Death Eater and terrible incompatibility after terrible incompatibility. The violent polarities that made them who they were. They fell into each other a bit, in that moment of collision, a bit drunk, a bit soul-tangled. She slid the rose into her hair and turned away. They came to the end of the rose garden, where the hedges grew thickest and stupidities might be said most freely. Where terrible incompatibilities stopped meaning so much, because, here amongst green boughs and the rustling breeze, they were just a man and a woman, meandering through a garden, being idiots about roses. They found a seat on a stone bench near a fountain adorned with chubby Cupids. Granger curled her legs under herself. The rose in her hair was askew, so Draco reached over to fix it, expecting to do so suavely but finding instead that he was transfixed by a feeling of exquisite nervousness, the likes of which he hadnít felt since he was a teenager. Granger breathed out a thank you. Her cheeks were pink. They talked of things trivial and not, of roses and cupboards and scars and war and artichokes and fish pie. They looked up at the smouldering stars, and the nightbirds warbled their unearthly cadences, and roses dropped their petals in beautiful melancholy. An hour drifted by, and then two, and then three Å though it felt like theyíd only just sat down beside each other on this damp bench amongst the roses to witter away the evening. The memory of that night would remain with Draco for a long time afterwards, moon-kissed and sweet. The light in her eyes, the taste of wine, the glitter of starshine in the fountain, the slow seduction of the roses. Il faut se laisser ensorceler. Chapter 18:Amends Please join me in squealing at this gorgeous rose garden commission by makiblue_art! It is also my pleasure to share my highly professional art direction for the commission, so that you appreciate the final product even more by contrast. Chapter 18 I have something for you, came a Jot from Granger a week later. His hair was reddish-blonde, as was his rather impressive beard, and his eyes were a penetrating blue. He was a Viking in a three-piece suit. There was probably more curly chest hair peeking over the top of his collar than Draco had grown since puberty. Draco decided that he didnít like him. Still Disillusioned, he slid into the caf after an exiting customer and leaned against a wall to eavesdrop. Larsen responded something about checkpoint inhibitor therapy, to which Granger replied with vast enthusiasm. The Vikingís eyes were riveted on Granger in a way that Draco didnít like. There was something predatory about it Å something hungry. And Granger was gesticulating away and too busy being excited about nanobiologics to notice. Suspicions began to percolate: was this meaty Muggle going to try to steal her ideas? Make money off her? Literally eat her? There was only one way to find out. Dracoís dive into the manís mind was over as soon as it began. He found himself butting against extremely sophisticated mental barriers, the likes of which only a highly trained Occlumens would have in place. So Granger was wrong. ØHaving you Apparate into Tesco in your full Auror kit would be terribly amusing to witness, anywayèÆ She walked him to the door and leaned against the frame as Draco readied his wand for his Disapparation. ØFor the book.Æ ØAmends,Æ shrugged Draco. ØA fair and just reparation for the damages caused.Æ ØIíll keep the head for next time.Æ Granger laughed. ØMake it the bouquet, rather.Æ ØFine.Æ ØBye, Malfoy.Æ A general lightness of being always accompanied Disapparation, didnít it? Chapter19: The Nundu / TryingTimes forDraco Malfoy Draco was feeling good. He and four other Aurors had set up a broad Anti-Apparition Ward half a kilometre away from the fort, with the old ruin at its centre. The team had been briefed by the Magizoologists accompanying them on the dangers of the Nundu Å its lethal venom with no known antidote, its aggression, its wicked agility. The Aurors were to deal with the naughty wizards and the Magizoologists with the beast. At Draco's signal, they began their stealthy assault on the fort. The Magizoologists were a well-trained bunch who kept well behind the Disillusioned Aurors, per their instructions. Two half-asleep watchmen were Stunned, Silenced, and immobilised with cuffs. Then the Aurors moved into the fort proper, after Draco had rid the door of wards and Buckley had taken care of the rather complex Magical locking mechanism. ØGerman-made,Æ Buckley muttered by way of apology for taking so long. Now they advanced down poorly lit corridors rife with ill-cast wards. Draco took care of the latter as Buckley raised his wand in a detection spell. He signalled two more guards ahead, which Goggin and young Humphreys crept onwards to take care of. They came across a guard room, which was a mess of decrepit furniture and new sleeping bags, old food and heaps upon heaps of the ballistic syringes that had proven so critical to track Talfryn down. Two Aurors stood sentinel as Draco and the others pulled in the Magizoologists to inspect the syringes and their contents. They concluded that it was etorphine hydrochloride Å a Muggle opioid. ØVery potent,Æ said the most senior of the Magizoologists, a witch called Ridgewell. ØMuggles use it to take down rhinoceroses. A squirt of this will stop a human heart in half a minute.Æ ØBlimey, theyíve got enough here for two dozen rhinos,Æ said her younger partner. ØOr one very large Nundu.Æ They discovered a stash of small crossbows in a dark corner. After a brief conference, two the Magizoologists equipped themselves with them: ØWeíve got our own sedatives,Æ said Ridgewell, Øbut we know these will work, if ours donít.Æ ØWait here,Æ said Draco as he opened the door to the next passage. ØWeíll go ahead and clear the way. And whatís that bloody smell? Is itè?Æ Ridgewell sniffed at the air, looking rather like an English Setter about to point. There was a foul, pungent smell seeping into the room. ØThatíll be the Nundu,Æ said Ridgewell. ØMale, judging by the potency. If you spot it, donít make eye contact, move slowly, and come back to us. Iím not sure Disillusionment works on Magical felids.Æ Draco, who was rather more interested in Talfryn than the Nundu, slipped into the corridor, flanked by Buckley and Humphreys, with Goggin bringing up the rear. Fernsby was left to protect the Magizoologists. As they advanced, their detection spells suggested three more nearby human presences in the fort Å as well as whoever else might be behind the metres of rock ahead of them. And underneath themÅ ØSomething big,Æ said Humphreys, holding her wand to her ear as she knelt on the floor. ØItís growling, too Å I wonder if itís dinnertime.Æ ØHappy to let the Magis deal with that bugger,Æ said Buckley with a shudder. There was a cry of frustration up ahead. The Aurors crept up close enough to hear someone swear. ØI canít fucking Disapparate,Æ came a rough voice. ØYou try.Æ ØIdiot,Æ came a drawl. There was a moment of silence, and then, ØI canít either.Æ ØFuck!Æ came a third voice Å Talfrynís. ØAnti-Apparition Ward. Sound the bloody alarm, you idiot! Accio broom!Æ The Disillusioned Aurors slipped into a kind of inner courtyard. Goggin got a Stunner off on the drawly wizard just as heíd lifted his wand to raise the alarm. A broom whizzed by Draco in the dark. He hit it with an Incendio; it was a smouldering stick by the time it reached Talfrynís waiting hand. ØTheyíre here!Æ said Talfryn, backing into a corner behind a half-collapsed pillar. ØFinite incantatem! Finite incantatem! Hominem Revelio!Æ He was casting the spells in the general direction of the passage the Aurors had emerged from, hoping to hit someone and break their Disillusionment. His remaining acolyte joined him behind the pillar and did the same, forcing the Aurors to take up defensive positions behind piles of rubble. Talfryn swept his wand into the air to set off an intruder alarm. From a room behind them came footsteps, and then, suddenly, the courtyard was crawling with two dozen wizards. ØShit,Æ hissed Humphreys. Things had just become interesting. ØIíll take the left with Goggin,Æ muttered Draco to Humphreys and Buckley. ØYou two stay here and distract Å mind you keep moving so they donít pin you.Æ Now that they were heavily outnumbered, there would be no fighting nice. Which was excellent, because Draco preferred to fight dirty Å Disillusioned and with liberal use of Legilimency. Goggin was an excellent partner; the Irishman was a brawler at heart and loved an opportunity to get messy. Gogginís Disillusioned shape bobbed behind him as Draco weaved to the ragged line of men that was forming around Talfryn. He went ahead, softening the ranks with Stunners while Goggin cleaned up after. When he tired of garden variety Stuns and Petrificus Totalus, Draco added a little spice. Having identified the better fighters through observation or Legilimency, he cast a few of the more magically demanding Turncoat jinxes Å and briefly, those opponents fought for the Aurors, until their colleagues cursed some sense into them. Buckley and Humphreys pounded Talfrynís line with explosive spells and kept their enemiesí attention on the front of the courtyard. Aguamenti was being sprayed where things (or people) had caught fire and added a heavy steam to the atmosphere. This was ideal for Draco and Goggin, who were even more difficult to spot within. They continued their advance towards Talfryn. Goggin came behind Draco to Stun any who were still twitching after his passing. He secured them with the satisfying click of the cuffs. Dracoís Legilimency showed him one manís intent to collapse a pillar into the corner where Buckley and Humphreys had holed up. Fatigued by his repeated Stunners, Draco switched it up Å a flick of his wand took the wizard out at the knees. Then he blinded him. Then he severed his Achilles tendons. All non-lethal measures, of course; Draco played by the rules. Mostly. Gradually, their adversaries became aware of a growing stillness on their left flank as Draco and Goggin moved in, while Buckley and Humphreys hammered them with an unfriendly barrage of spells. An unlucky Finite Incantatem hit Goggin and revealed him. Goggin Disillusioned himself again immediately while Draco levitated the big man to a spot fifteen metres away, just before a Bombarda exploded where he had stood. ØCheers,Æ came Gogginís throaty whisper. They continued their advance. Stun, curse, Legilimens, dodge Finite, Stun, Turncoat Jinx, Impedimenta, dodge, blind, Legilimens, Stun. Their numbers reducing alarmingly, Talfrynís remaining men were now Disillusioning themselves as well, screeching Protego! and scattering across the courtyard. It was the Aurorsí turn to fire Finite Incantatem at will. The Anti-Apparition Ward was a double-edged sword. Draco dearly wished that he could Apparate to Talfrynís side and take him, but he was only two-thirds of the way there. At Dracoís count, there were only four opponents left, plus Talfryn. Buckley was hit with a Finite emanating from somewhere near Draco. Suddenly in the realm of the visible, he was forced to dodge behind piles of boulders before a friendly Disillusionment from the east side of the courtyard removed him from sight again: Humphreys. Draco systematically ploughed the ground near him with Petrificus Totalus until he got the Disillusioned wizard who had hit Buckley. Three left. ØYer manís goiní for that chain,Æ gasped Goggin. Draco whirled to see Talfryn launching himself at a dangling chain connected to an ancient pulley. The pulley was connected to a large grille laying across a hole in the ground. ØShit!Æ said Draco. Both Aurors aimed desperate long-range Stunners at Talfryn. By some miracle, Goggin hit the manís leg and Draco his shoulder, but Talfryn had already wrapped his arms around the chain and his Stunned body pulled it down. There was a grating sound as the grille was slid out of place. Then a rumbling growl shook the very stones under their feet. The Nundu leapt out of its underground prison and was now loose in the courtyard. A fetid odour accompanied it, enough to make the weaker-stomached men retch. The Aurors called out their retreat; they were not equipped to deal with this beast. Draco heard Gogginís breathless run at his side as they sprinted for the passage. The Nundu turned to them. As it transpired, Disillusionment did not work on Magical felids. Draco made a note to tell Ridgewell, should he survive long enough to speak to her again. The beast was tracking their movement, as well as that of a handful of other invisible-to-them figures in the courtyard. As the Nunduís eyes slid over him, Draco felt, for the first time in his life, what it felt like to be prey Å the yellow gaze had a paralysing effect. The creatureís movements were so easy and sinuous they were hypnotic. Its scarred, Magic-repelling hide, bristling with venomous spines, rippled as it walked. Dracoís wand felt as useless as a twig in his hand. He and Goggin stilled and looked at the ground, as they had been taught by Ridgewell. It was one of the singular most difficult things Draco had ever done. His every instinct was screaming at him to flee or to fire a Bombarda at the creatureís face. He could hear Goggin swearing a constant stream of fuck under his breath. There was a scuffle at the passage that led to the exit. Two of Talfrynís men were fighting to get through before the other. The Nundu leapt, crossing the courtyard in two graceful bounds. The menís Disillusionment dissipated as they died, one crushed by the creatureís weight, the other casually decapitated by the sweep of a paw. His head rolled to Dracoís feet like a gory Quaffle. The passage was too small for the Nundu to enter. It turned its attention back to the courtyard, its nostrils flared wide, venom drip-dripping from its muzzle. It was sniffing for something. Another of Talfrynís Disillusioned men made a run for it. He was summarily killed, cut in two bloody halves by a bite. That was, at Dracoís best count, the last of Talfrynís crew. Now there were only Aurors left standing. The Nundu turned its nose back to the wind. It found what it had been sniffing for: Talfrynís Stunned body. Talfryn was snatched up and tossed into the air like a childís plaything. He hit the wall with a musical crunch. Then the creature eviscerated him with an easy swipe and began to eat. Slowly, amongst the wet sounds of Talfrynís innards being slurped up, Draco and Goggin moved towards the passage. Draco hoped that the Disillusioned Buckley and Humphreys were doing the same Å no sudden movement, no eye contact, just an uninteresting drift towards safety. The Nundu raised its head. It looked to the east of the courtyard. Humphreys. The creature ambled towards the eastern corner with a kind of lazy anticipation. Draco couldnít blame the young Auror for the explosion of spells she cast towards the beast; he wouldíve done the same, had he been cornered. She sent something cutting at its face; it shrugged it off with a sneeze that scattered venom in a two metre radius. Draco raised his wand; Gogginís Disillusioned arm by his side did the same. ØConfrigo, as hard as you can go,Æ said Draco. They slashed their wands downwards with identical timing, causing their spells to twin together and hurl like a fireball towards the beastís flank. The spell exploded upon impact, leaving their ears ringing. Humphreys was hit by the percussive force of the blast; she hit a wall and lost her Disillusion. Draco could see her crawling away through the smoke. And the Nundu? It had been knocked sideways by the explosion, but now it regained its feet and shook its mane, as though this had been a playful shove and not a deadly spell. It turned the considerable weight of its attention to Draco and Goggin. ØShite,Æ said Goggin. They raised their wands. The beast leapt. Goggin hit it with a Bombarda in its open mouth, which bought them a moment of respite as it landed, mere metres from them, and hacked out a cough wet with venom. Dracoís Blinding Jinx was next, aimed at the eyes, almost at point-blank range. It did nothing but seal one eye shut Å and piss the thing off. Draco and Goggin scrambled backwards as pneumatic whizzing filled the air. The Magizoologists had come. They peeked out of the passage and peppered the beast with the ballistic syringes and their own tranquilisers. At this distance, half of the syringes were bouncing off the Nunduís hide. Buckley, limping badly, was dragging Humphreys towards the safety of the passage. Fernsby stood guard in front of the Magis and thickened the air with Protegos before darting out to assist Buckley. Ridgewell Conjured a herd of small leaping things which danced around the beast and distracted it for a moment, until it vomited out its venom and they all dissolved. It bought enough time for Draco and Goggin to pull themselves behind a boulder. The Nunduís attention turned to Humphreys and Buckley. A dozen syringes were embedded in its shoulder and neck Å to, so far, minor effect. The Magizoologists levitated half a dead doe, stuffed with tranquilisers, towards the creature. It batted the doe aside, having learnt in the course of its captivity not to trust any meat except what it had killed itself. The Magizoologists launched projectiles full of inhalant sedatives which exploded at the beastís feet. This had been their last-ditch plan, as the inhalant would be just as dangerous to the Aurors as it would be to the beast. Draco and Goggin cast Bubble-Head Charms at each other and staggered further away. The Nundu stepped through the purpling cloud with a hiss, and, finally, showed signs of slowing Å one eye jinxed shut, blood running from its mouth, sedatives in its bloodstream and lungs. Its remaining eye was fixed on the stumbling Humphreys and Buckley, who were both now being dragged by Fernsby. Draco saw the sweep of the tail and the lowering of the hindquarters that signaled an imminent pounce. He slashed his wand towards the chain and pulley and whipped the chain around the Nunduís hind leg just as it leapt. Goggin joined him, his wand crackling with effort as they pulled the chain backwards through sheer force of magic. The Nundu was forced back, its claws digging deep gouges into the rocky ground. The injured trio of Aurors collapsed into the relative safety of the passage, leaving Draco and Goggin to face the beast. The Magizoologists were scrambling to distract the creature, Conjuring a female Nundu (ignored), more meat (swatted aside), prey animals (ignored), a cage around it (smashed to bits), and, finally, launching enough immobilising agents to sedate twelve rhinoceroses. Draco believed the tales, now, of a single Nundu wiping out entire villages in Eastern Africa. The Nundu had half-collapsed Å the sedatives were finally working. Its remaining eye was bleary, its mouth hung open, its legs grew boneless. It bared its fangs at Draco and Goggin and a hot stream of venom jetted out at them. They dodged and were separated by a hissing black-purple flow. Draco was on the side of its remaining good eye. He aimed another Blinding Jinx just as the beast turned its heavy head to him and bared its fangs again. He got the beast in the eye, the beast got him across the throat with searing venom. The pain of it shocked his system. His Bubble-Head Charm vanished. He gasped out to breathe and took in a lungful of sedative-filled air. As the Nundu finally collapsed, so, too, did Draco. ~ Draco awoke to a white ceiling streaking past him, as though he or the ceiling were moving at high velocity. There were raised voices and indistinct words and sounds of general chaos. Running feet, clinking equipment, the whirr of wheels. Then there was a crisp voice of command. The voice was reassuring, somehow. It was the voice of Competence and Order, and it was Good. His body was no longer his body; it was a thing chiefly composed of pain. He could not scream. His ears caught words and communicated them to his numb brain. Envenomated. Respiratory depression. Paralysis of the diaphragm. Lethal dose. And then, distantly, he could hear a scream. But it was not his Å it was his motherís. ØGet her out,Æ said the Voice of Competence. ØIíll speak to her when Iíve saved this oneís life.Æ ~ Draco awoke to another white ceiling. This time, it was not whizzing by impossibly fast. He took this development to be good news. Other good news: he felt no pain. In fact, he felt excellent. He had never felt so wonderful in his life. Full of vitality. Full of joy. ØFull of painkillers,Æ came a kindly voice. ØYouíre stuffed to the gills with them, child. Donít try to get up. Iíll fetch your Healer.Æ The kindly voice belonged to a matronly sort of witch in light green St. Mungoís robes. A nurse. Draco watched her leave, giggling at the odd fish-eye effect occurring in his vision, which made her bum hilariously large. Then he blinked and the walls began to squeeze inwards. If he closed his eyes, he saw kaleidoscopes. An orange cat and a Nundu, whirling into each other, fighting one another in concentric spirals, on and on and on. He opened his eyes again. He was at St. Mungoís. He was alive. Shouldnít he be dead? ØYou should,Æ came the cutting Voice of Order. ØAm I saying everything Iím thinking?Æ asked Draco of the ceiling, with a deep philosophical curiosity. ØYes, and you will for another few hours, at least. Youíre on a little cocktail that affects neurotransmission. It was the only way to manage your pain during the procedure. Youíll probably experience hallucinations Å if you havenít already, of course.Æ The swot was strong with this one. In a kind of slow motion, Draco turned his head to observe the Healer. Her deep green robes swam into sight. Her mouth was set in a straight line, but her dark eyes were warmed by concern. She was beautiful. The light behind her glowed into a blinding halo. He thought he heard the sound of hymns. ØHow are you feeling?Æ she asked. ØPositively top-hole,Æ said Draco. ØAre you an angel?Æ The Angel-Healer did her utmost not to laugh Å which was an angelic sort of thing to do, and only further proved her secret identity. ØYou can trust me,Æ said Draco. He attempted to tap his nose but poked himself in the eye instead. ØIíll keep your secret.Æ The Angel-Healer did not respond; she was reading a chart. ØI had an operation?Æ asked Draco. ØWeíll talk about it later. When youíve slept this off a bit.Æ Something about her authoritativeness was terribly familiar. ØI know who you are,Æ gasped Draco. ØThatís good.Æ ØYouíre Hermione Granger.Æ ØCorrect.Æ She rose. Her robes danced about her in green swathes of colour. ØYour mother is frothing to be let in. She Flooed back from Italy as soon as we sent word. But I want you to sleep first. Iíd prefer you to have your mouth under control before you see her. All right?Æ ØFine,Æ said Draco. ØExcellent. Have a nap. Weíll talk again when you wake up.Æ Draco, with an effort, patted the bed. ØJoin me,Æ said Draco. ØNo.Æ ØWhy not?Æ asked Draco in a long sort of whinge. ØBecause you donít know what youíre saying,Æ said Granger. There was restrained amusement in her voice. ØI hope you donít remember this, for your sake.Æ Draco, with a distant thrill of horror, heard himself say, ØI want to kiss you.Æ ØNo you donít.Æ ØCome and sit in my lap.Æ ØGo to sleep, Malfoy.Æ Granger was a distant figure now, melding in and out of the shadows of the corridor. She shut the door behind her. Draco closed his eyes. The Nundu and the cat continued their whirling battle, on and on until he fell asleep. by RunningQuill_art ~ Draco woke up again. Something about the sun streaming through the window told him that it was the next day. Unfortunately, he remembered every word of his conversation with Granger. Where was the Nundu? Could it come and finish the job of killing him? The kind nurse was back. She fussed a bit over Dracoís sheets and then applied a paste that smelled strongly of pine to his neck. ØDittany?Æ ØVahlia. It should help with the scarring.Æ The nurse cast a few diagnostic spells on him and seemed satisfied by the results. ØYou are doing remarkably well, Mr. Malfoy, all things considered. Your mother is here. Do you feel like seeing her? You neednít if you donít want to.Æ Draco nodded. A few minutes later, his mother rushed in and embraced him in her thin arms. She looked frightfully shaken, pale, and tired. She perched next to the bed and fussed over him at length, enquiring about how he felt, how his neck felt, whether he could breathe, whether he could swallow, how he had slept, and so on and so forth, until Dracoís mouth grew dry and he had to call for water. Draco learned that his team had made it out of the fort with a mix of injuries, though none as dire as his. This was his third day at St. Mungoís. The Nundu had survived and been transported back to the wilds of Tanzania. And the baddies? The beast had taken its blood-soaked revenge on Talfryn and company. Many were dead. Those that had survived the courtyard massacre were awaiting trial. Narcissa squeezed Dracoís hand. There were tears in her eyes. ØBut enough about them. I am so Å so happy to see you well. I very nearly lost you. I donít know what I wouldíve done.Æ Narcissa stopped and took a deep breath to force down a sob. She didnít like to cry. ØIím going to be fine, mother,Æ said Draco. Narcissa straightened and dabbed at her eyes. ØDonít be cavalier about it. You almost werenít fine. You almost died. The Granger girl Å Healer Granger Å she was instrumental. Nobody knew what to do. That venom doesnít have a known antivenom. Most of the Healers didnít even know what a Nundu was. Neither did I, mind you Å what possessed you to go in pursuit of such a creature, I shall never understand. You were as good as dead. But she knew things. Muggle things, I think. She whisked you away for four hours Å I composed an entire eulogy in my head Å and when she came back, she said you were going to live.Æ Draco squeezed his motherís hand. He attempted humour. ØWill you write the eulogy out for me? I should quite like to read it.Æ Narcissa sniffed. She rose and strode to the window with her back to Draco. Her thin shoulders shook. ØCanít you take a desk job?Æ she asked breathily. ØQuit this terrible Auror business?Æ There was a knock on the door. Narcissa wiped away her tears. With her back ramrod straight and her usual severe expression back into place, she went to answer. It was Granger. She wasnít wearing her Healer robes, today Å it was her professorial Muggle attire. Another of those high-waisted skirts and silky blouses. ØOh Å er, Iím sorry for interrupting,Æ said Granger. ØI can come back later.Æ Draco couldnít really see what happened next Å his mother lunged into the corridor with her arms spread wide, and all he heard was an oof from Granger as she was, presumably, hugged quite hard. There was the sound of weeping. Some awkward words of comfort. Then his motherís heels click-clacked down the corridor. Her voice, thicker than usual, asked where the loo was. ØEr Å your left,Æ came Grangerís voice. Draco did not Å repeat, did not Å look at her bum as she walked away. For all he knew, some lingering trace of the cocktail would make him blurt out something stupid. All right, so he stole one glance when she was already well out of the room. Narcissa returned, nose powdered, eyes glamoured to be bright rather than red. ØA brilliant girl,Æ she said of Granger. ØQuite brilliant. But what in heavenís name was she wearing today?Æ Draco did not inform her that he rather liked it. Narcissa had endured a great many shocks already. Finally convinced that her only son, her treasure, the apple of her eye, wasnít about to pop his clogs, Narcissa retired to the Manor. Draco joined her there the next day and was near-suffocated by the joint attentions of his mother and the worried house-elves. For the next week, his every step Å amongst a steady stream of friends and well-wishers Å was haunted by either an elf or Narcissa bearing Vahlia ointment or restorative soups or warm compresses. He languished in delicious self-indulgence under their care for the first few days, and then grew tired of it, and took to hiding in distant reaches of the Manor grounds for the rest of his recovery. One morning, when Draco was feeling sociable enough to join his mother for breakfast in the dining room, he found her hard at work on a truly breathtaking floral arrangement. It was alive with movement Å hummingbird hyacinths fluttering, the glitter of ruby poppies, the dance of halla vines. ØYouíve outdone yourself, mother,Æ said Draco. ØDo you like it? Good. As the flames in the hearth resumed their normal colour, Draco was left to ruminate on the unexpectedness of his reaction at the thought of losing the Granger assignment. Perhaps because it was unnecessary. It was for the pleasure of it. It was Just Because. He watched her for a shift, for a sign of impatience, but there was none. Only a hip against the door jamb, an arm held loosely at her waist. She was talking about his mother now, asking him to tell her that she adored the flowers. He said something in return, something that she could respond to, to continue to stretch out the moment. She laughed at something. Their eyes met. Draco felt woolly-headed and vague. It was the anaesthesia again, the feel of the world in flux, a slow spinning. Granger was idly plucking a few strands of wisteria. He asked if that was the extent of her flower arranging. She said yes, was he impressed? And passed him the droopy bouquet. He said it was the loveliest thing he had ever seen. He reached to take it. He drew his fingertips against hers. In his veins, not blood, but lightness. His touch lingered probably too long. He wondered what to call this thing, this stealing of glances and touches and moments. The headlong giddiness impelled by the most platonic of hugs. The wanting to be near. He wasnít foolish enough to call it love, and it was too delicate for lust, but it wasnít nothing, either. It was Something. Yes. The air smelled of sweet grass and the delicate fragrance of the willows that bordered the lake. It was good to be outside again. As they came into Hogsmeade, Granger drifted towards the Three Broomsticks. ØIím properly famished. Have you eaten?Æ ØNo,Æ said Draco. ØNo lunch, either; the corpses put me off.Æ Granger wrinkled her nose. ØWell Å youíre welcome to join me, but it wonít be quite as recherch as the Manorís fare.Æ She tried the door of the Three Broomsticks, only to find a notice indicating that they were closed until September. They walked on to Madam Puddifootís, which was equally shuttered. Finally, they reached the Hogís Head. Granger hovered indecisively at the door. ØNot sure Iím this desperate. Iíve heard itís gone quite downhill since Aberforth retired.Æ ØWhat? Canít be that bad for a pint and bit of pub grub, can it?Æ It could. Draco and Granger were welcomed (if such a cheerful term could be used) by a man who looked more like a Skrewt than most Skrewts do. He looked irritated that they dared to give him business. That was red flag number one that this was going to be a uniquely terrible experience. They asked for a pint; they were told that there was no ale left on the premises. That was red flag number two. At this juncture, a wiser pair would have upped and left, but a kind of curiosity had been lit in them, to see how bad this could actually get. ØWeíll take what youíve got then, mate,Æ said Draco. ØAnd a bit of whateverís in the kitchen.Æ They sat themselves at a grubby table near what had probably been a window, once, except it was now coated with grime. The Skrewt dropped two smudged glasses onto the table and poured out something clear into them before stomping off to the kitchen. A powerful odour of turpentine washed over the table. Granger sniffed at her glass and her eyes watered. ØOh my Å itís going to be a proper sinus cleanser.Æ ØCanít be worse than Affpuddleís Absinthe, can it?Æ asked Draco. Ø88 percent ABV,Æ gasped Granger. ØBrilliant. I had just wanted to begin the week with a spot of alcohol poisoning.Æ ØItís fine,Æ said Draco in his broken voice. ØIt needed at least five more minutes under the hair dryer.Æ They turned their attention to Grangerís salad. ØLard Voldemort.Æ ØMy god.Æ ØThe mash looksè all right?Æ ØIt smells like hot cat sick,Æ said Granger, knocking away Dracoís fork. ØDo not try it. Nothing will come of this except cataclysmic diarrhoea.Æ Draco, who did not want a runny bum, set aside his fork. They looked at each other. Around the corner was a little grocerís, just about to close up for the evening. They raided the last of the bread basket and bought a small wheel of cheese to go with it. Granger found some cherries. Draco discovered an enormous, slightly squashed blackberry pie. Granger asked if they ought to buy a slice? Draco said he wanted two, personally. He could not trust them. She was his Principal. He grew dimly aware that he was heading for disaster. Granger, blithely unconscious of the turmoil caused by the back of her neck, pushed a final hairpin into place. art by the peerless nikitajobson Then she put the little basket of cherries between herself and Draco and sat next to him on the edge of the promontory, so that her legs dangled next to his. They talked. He tried not to look at her cherry-reddened mouth. Tried not to think about the wet rim of the cider bottle that they passed back and forth. Their shoulders touched now and again. He felt the occasional brush of her curls when the wind teased them his way. The breeze brought hints of her towards him, cider and shampoo and the salt of skin in summer heat. Would it truly be so terrible, not to quash, just for now? He had quashed for weeks, after all. He knew he could do it again. He could simply enjoy right now, and go back to quashing afterwards, couldnít he? It would be fine, wouldnít it? He had everything under control. They threw cherry pits into the shrubbery below the promontory. Granger said theyíd make a pretty grove of cherry trees, one day. Draco chased the pits with Herbivicus charms. His aim was true; here and there below them, the pits split and pushed forth small, tender leaves. Granger, delighted, cast a few Aguamenti. The evening light grew delicate and elusive. Granger leaned back on her hands and sighed. There was contentment in it. Happiness, even. ØIím glad you kept the protection assignment.Æ Now she wasnít teasing Å now she was sincere. Draco felt a smile, unbidden, flash across his face. A new and unfamiliar joy swelled in his chest. And it was beautiful, sitting next to Granger, with his arm brushing hers, here at the top of the promontory, which, at this moment, felt like the top of the world. Chapter 23: DracoMalfoy,NotoriousAuror Dracoís post-Granger afterglow lingered all night. When he returned to the Manor he wandered about, sighing and staring out of windows. He smiled vaguely at nothing. He thought about the back of her neck and where he would most like to put his mouth. He read some of her old notes on the Jotter. He indulged in a delicious daydream of her in the library, pushed up against the stacks. When he found himself floating towards the rose garden for a midnight stroll Å an unprecedented activity, for him Å Draco realised he was acting like a besotted imbecile. Again. His brain, which had been adrift amongst those stupid fluffy clouds, came plummeting back to earth, where it took up residence in his skull once again, but grumpily, as though heíd interrupted it in something important. As though there was anything remotely important about cherries and sundresses and Iím glad you kept the protection assignment. At the entrance to the rose garden, Draco pivoted on his heel and stormed back into the Manor. He closeted himself in his study, where he strode about, freshly perturbed. What the fuck was wrong with him? Seeing her had been a bad idea. He had done very well over the month of July, getting Granger out of his head. The crush had all but been quashed out of existence. But then, in her presence, his quashing had lasted all of an hour. An hour! This was troubling. Aggravating. Fuck Theo and his nonsense about absence making the heart grow fonder; he did far better when he was away from her. When he couldnít see her, tease and be teased by her, smell her, steal looks at the back of her neckè Draco half-lapsed into another daydream before catching himself at it again. Right. This was fixable. Grangerís next asterisk holiday wasnít until Mabon Å that was late September. That was ample time to let this thing wear off and disappear. Draco leaned against the unlit fireplace and rapped his fingers against it. She was his fucking Principal. And Å even more importantly Å he was Draco Malfoy. Highly eligible, perpetually unattached. He didnít do besotted imbecile. His Jotter buzzed. Draco waited an entire ten minutes before checking it, during which he paced in agitation while telling himself that he was playing it cool. It wasnít Granger Jotting him, anyway. And he was not disappointed in the least. It was Goggin, scheduling a training session for the next morning. Which would be an excellent outlet for these mad, frustrated energies that he was grappling with. Draco replied, No wands, to ensure that Goggin would knock a bit of sense into him. ~ The next day, Draco and Goggin knocked so much sense into each other that they both became quite venerable philosophers. This was marred by one minor hiccough: nobody could understand them through their fat lips. The world made do without their unutterable wisdom. A few days passed, during which Draco did wonderfully from a quashing perspective. Granger became a mere afterthought amongst various emergencies, missions, and brutal training sessions. Draco grew pleased with himself once more. Everything was going to be fine. His first contact with Granger was initiated by her, and it began with an insult, which was promising. Youíre mad, was Grangerís opener. Draco, who was Playing It Cool, waited for two hours before responding with: ? Iíve just spoken to Hippocrates, said Granger. Draco answered, Who? Hippocrates Smethwyck. The head of St. Mungoís. He showed me your letter. Did you mean to put that many zeroes?? Draco found it challenging to play it cool when there was a grin breaking across his face. Did my definition of large meet your expectations? E for Exceeds. Am properly gobsmacked. You wanted a swimming pool, said Draco. That takes another zero. That was idle daydreaming! answered Granger. Draco could all but hear her voice growing shrill. You didnít answer me re: madness. He didnít respond, because his first impulse was to tell the truth, which was that it brought him a real pleasure to make her idle daydreams come to fruition, but that was Å that was too Å eurgh. The truth was a saccharine mess. The Jotter buzzed again. Iím sat at work trying not to cry. Stiff upper lip, said Draco. Thank you for doing this, said Granger. Itís going to change so many lives for the better. That seemed like a strong concluding remark to Draco, who decided not to respond further. He cast an eye over the conversation and was pleased with himself: it had been overwhelmingly neutral. Well, except, perhaps, for the ådefinition of largeí thing, but that was just Å just flirting for sport. He hadnít even made the screamingly obvious joke when she said sheíd been gobsmacked by it. See? This was going fine. Everything was under control. There was no crush here. Later that night, Granger sent another note, this time, inviting him to dinner to thank him; she knew a good French place, if heíd like to come? Draco read the invitation with a stupid degree of longing. However, an in-person thanks would present challenges to the Quash, and also, no doubt, warrant another one of her hugs. The fewer of those he got, the less of a cretin he was. He said, Iíve got other plans. You neednít thank me, the gift was my thanks to you. Granger responded after a few minutes: All right. Do tell me if you change your mind. No. He wasnít going to change his mind about dinner with her. He was only going to daydream about it all evening, thanks. Days passed. Smethwyck sent Draco plans for a full-scale renovation of the Janus Thickey Ward, per his instructions. The plans were remarkably detailed and thorough. Architects and consulting engineers had been pre-selected, pending his approval, to reimagine the Ward and create a state-of.the-art retreat for long-term care patients. Consultation processes were outlined, as well as plans for specialist Healer and nurse training. A new structure for interdisciplinary collaborations and research focusing on long term ailments was proposed. The general whiff of competence that floated off the page was, Draco knew, atypical of St. Mungoís administration. It smelled like Granger. It occurred to him that he could ask her, but no Å it was safer to keep communications to a minimum. A witch summoned an Eldritch abomination in Northamptonshire. The cat stirred at the sound of her voice. It leapt off of Draco, using his face as a launch point, and sauntered towards its mistress. Granger asked Draco what he would like for breakfast. He requested a cup of coffee to wash away the piquant flavour of cat foot. Granger made a very decent cup of coffee. The morning passed in a flurry of meetings. The first was with Tonks, who they met at Auror Headquarters, to hear what she had discovered in the course of her interrogations. ØYou will be delighted to learn that No-Handsí hands were successfully reattached,Æ said Tonks as they arrived. ØToo bad,Æ said Draco. ØThatís what I said.Æ Tonks closed the door to her office and sat at her desk. ØMurderous little bastard. Right. Sit yourselves down. Robards cleared the use of Veritaserum on our friends last night, so we had a wee chat. Neither man knows the identity of the person who gave them their instructions. However Å I did discover something rather interesting. Both men are werewolves, and both participated in the harvest moon attacks.Æ Tonks placed a slip of parchment on the table. ØThe mediwitchís post-mortem confirms that the other one was a werewolf, as well.Æ Tonks looked from Granger to Draco and back again. ØThoughts? Reactions from the two luminaries before me?Æ The luminaries looked at one another. Granger shifted. ØI think itís time I told you what Iím working on. Malfoy, would you do the honours for the Vow of Secrecy?Æ The Vow was cast. Granger summarised her work and findings for Tonks, and Draco added the equally interesting (and distressing) discovery that Fenrir Greyback had returned and appeared to have reassembled something of his old pack. They were probably the ones responsible for the full moon attacks, on top of targeting Granger. ØBonkers,Æ gasped Tonks. ØThe whole jolly lot of them.Æ Granger concluded with the same cautionary language that she had used with Draco the night before, about uncharted waters and the uncertain outcome of the clinical trials. Tonks took the news of Grangerís lycanthropy cure with laudable neutrality, given Lupinís condition. Only her mohawk betrayed her, turning a few shades paler than its blood red. She gave Granger a long, adoration-filled look, whispered, ØIncredible,Æ and then she turned brisk. ØBring Potter and Weasley up to speed under the Vow. Weíll also have to inform Robards and Shacklebolt. That would be the extent of it at the moment, I think. Weíll involve others as needed. Take the conference room. Iíll join you in a moment.Æ Tonks shooed them out of her office. As they left, Draco stole a backward glance at her. Tonks was sitting at her desk, her hands clasped before her, her knuckles pressed to her mouth. Her eyes were unusually bright. In the conference room, Potter and Weasley were apprised of the situation under the Vow. Their reactions were predictable, but there was something comforting in their clasping of Granger, in their chest-puffing, gung-ho declarations to keep her safe, and in their table-pounding promises to find Greyback if it was the last thing they did. As the lead on the WTF, Potter looked to have found a fresh determination to catch the werewolves. There was a dangerous green light in his eye. Weasley was quite as goggle-eyed as Draco had been the night before on the potential for a cure for lycanthropy. His reactions consisted chiefly of repetitions of ØBlimey!Æ and ØBloody hell!Æ and ØYouíre brilliant, Hermione!Æ Granger gave him a quick smile. She and Draco were then quizzed on the break-in attempt. ØIíve got a recording of it,Æ said Granger, and she pulled out her mobile. It seemed that she had been able to preserve the camera footage in a kind of mini film. Curious in spite of himself, Draco rose to join Potter and Weasley in crowding around Granger and watching the small screen. He quelled a flicker of jealousy at the easy way that Potter propped an elbow on Grangerís armrest and came in close to her, and how Weasley casually threw himself around the back of her chair Å all while Draco stood a decorous and stiff armís length away. Granger played the film. Draco, having been Disillusioned for most of the skirmish, was largely invisible until the end, his whereabouts only indicated by bursts of spells and their effects on his opponents Å the severed arms, the sentry consumed by the Bombarda. The fight had lasted an age in Dracoís head, but it had taken less than a minute in real time. Weasley clapped him on the shoulder. ØYou gave them hell. Well done, mate.Æ Potter shook his hand. ØRemind me never to duel you.Æ ØPlay it again,Æ said Weasley. The replay was accompanied by much commentary by Potter and Weasley. ØThe killing curse right off the bat Å that fucking wanker Å can you imagine if theyíd got in? Hermione wouldnít have stood a chance. Oh! Look at the blood! Ha! Majestic spurt, that! Disarmingís meant to be Harryís thing, but youíve put a new spin on it, Malfoy. Dis-arming. Ha ha! That blokeís face when he realises heís trapped! Nice Quidditch kit, theyíll have a phobia of Seekers for lifeè is that the newest tincelle?Æ Draco left them to their several re-watches, resuming his seat on the other side of the table. He glanced at Granger and found that she wasnít looking at her mobile, but rather at him. Her expression took him a moment to interpret Å it was something serious, something studious, something pensive. She was puzzle-solving. Blast. She turned away when he caught her eye. Draco determined to keep catching her when she observed him, to interrupt her thought process and keep himself safely Unsolved. Tonks arrived, preceded by the sound of her combat boots stamping along the corridor. She looked as unruffled as ever and entered the room at full tilt. Her elbow collided vigorously with the back of Potter's head. ØSorry,Æ said Tonks. ØIt didn't ring hollow Å that's a compliment, Potter. Weíve all been brought up to speed?Æ Granger put away her mobile. ØYes, boss,Æ said the Aurors. Tonks sat at the head of the table. ØWeíve rather a lot to discuss, but let's begin with the most important bit: Hermioneís safety.Æ Potter and Weasley both leaned forwards, as though ready to seize Granger and carry her off to a distant tower, never to be seen again. ØRight,Æ said Weasley. ØWeíve got to get her out of here. What díyou fancy, Hermione? Madagascar? Greenland? Tibet?Æ Draco could not blame the man for the reaction Å he had had precisely the same reflex. Granger was tight about the jaw. ØI am positively not going anywhere.Æ An explosive argument ensued, of course. Potter and Weasley pushed for Grangerís immediate evacuation, the more remote the location, the better. They were motivated by genuine worry and the same anxieties that Draco suffered at the sound of Greybackís hated name. Draco, having already attempted that line of argument unsuccessfully, now sided with Granger. There was too much riding on her research Å if Greyback continued to be as wily as they knew him to be, they were looking at potentially hundreds, if not thousands of new infections over untold full moons, while work on Grangerís cure stalled. Tonks, with a look of saintly forbearance upon her face, allowed the argument to roil for four minutes. Then she clapped her hands. ØThank you, boys, for sharing your thoughts. Fortunately, none of your opinions matter.Æ Draco, Potter, and Weasley experienced ego death. Tonks continued as they clutched at the shredded remains of their psyches. ØThe Auror Office does not have the authority to tell the eminent Professor Granger what she can and cannot do. Our job is to keep her protected as she carries out her project, as requested by the Minister. So. First order of business: scheduling and housing.Æ Potter and Weasleyís cadavers twitched out some arguments, but Tonksí lips were growing more pursed by the minute, and they wisely gave it up. Together, the five of them drew up a draft schedule to ensure that, wherever Granger went, there would be someone with her Å either Draco or another Auror. Granger agreed to cut down on her public appearances. She also agreed, glumly, to suspend her duties in the Muggle world Å the shifts at the Muggle surgery and the teaching at Muggle Cambridge Å until Greyback had been caught. Non-magical locations were too difficult to protect. Magical locations were far safer by nature, but an Auror would heretofore accompany her at her lab and at St. Mungoís A&E. The discussion turned to housing. Granger agreed to move to a safehouse, as long as it was within Flooing distance of her laboratory. The dozen safehouses managed by the DMLE were discussed, each of which offered pros and cons (location, ease of travel, defensibility). Tonks and Draco shared a certain anxiety about the fact that the safehouses were necessarily known to many Aurors and DMLE staff. Other options were discussed. Creating a new safehouse? Complex and time-consuming, but an option. Potter and Weasley each suggested that Granger stay in one of their homes. Draco pointed out that moving Granger to the residence of either of her best mates was a blatantly obvious next move. In any case, Granger rejected the option point-blank: she wouldnít put their families in danger. She levelled the same objection at Potterís suggestion of Hogwarts Å children were not acceptable as potential collateral damage. ØChuck her in Malfoyís bloody Manor,Æ said Weasley, jerking a thumb towards Draco. ØNo one will look for her there.Æ Granger said, ØHah!Æ Potter laughed and then grew thoughtful. Tonks took the suggestion with a surprising degree of seriousness. She pressed a finger to her chin and said, ØWeasley has made a point.Æ Granger blinked. Draco felt a swell of confused anticipation. ØWe could set up decoy Hermiones in the safehouses and her cottage,Æ mused Tonks. ØTraps,Æ said Draco. ØI like traps,Æ nodded Potter. ØAnd ambushes.Æ ØIím brilliant,Æ said Weasley. Tonks nodded. ØIngenious, really.Æ ØNo,Æ said Granger, shaking her head. ØI have the same objection as with Harry and Ron Å I wonít put Malfoyís household in danger. If the Manor were attacked, and something were to happen to his mother, or the house-elvesÅÆ ØThe Manor is nigh impenetrable,Æ said Tonks. ØAs are most of those old estates. It took twenty ward-breakers three days to get in, during that last push in the War. It's ten times safer than our safest safehouseÆ ØTrue,Æ said Draco. He tried not to sound particularly eager. ØAlso Å my mother is spending the season on the Continent. She isnít at the Manor.Æ Granger, wide-eyed, turned to him. ØAre you agreeing with this idea?Æ Draco produced the worldís most careless shrug. ØI think itís an option worth considering.Æ Which was an understatement. He bloody loved it. It was perfect. Sheíd be protected by centuries-old magicks, theyíd have house-elves as secondary surveillance, and heíd be there every night. He was positively delighted by it. Weasley, who was looking on smugly, rose an entire inch in Dracoís esteem. Meanwhile, Potter was eyeing Granger. ØThe Manor isnít exactly the site of happy memories, is it? Youíd be all right with this, Hermione?Æ Granger was still staring at Draco in confusion. ØHm? Oh Å no, itíd be all right. Iíve been back since. One of Narcissa Malfoyís functions.Æ (Draco noted that she did not mention the dinner.) ØIt was Å fine. Objectively speaking, itís not an unreasonable suggestion, as a temporary measure. I only hesitate because it feels like a real imposition.Æ ØAn imposition? Pish tosh Å there are about fifty rooms in the Manor,Æ said Tonks, waving any real or imagined reservations away in her cousinís place. ØMalfoy wonít even know youíre there.Æ Grangerís glance passed over to Draco. Tonks, too, pinned him with an inquisitive eye. ØLetís do it,Æ said Draco, endeavouring for neutrality in his expression. ØItís an easy short term solution. We can always revisit Å or we can be creating a proper safehouse in the meantime, off the books.Æ Tonks rubbed her hands together. ØBrilliant. Weasley is absolutely correct Å Malfoy Manor is the last place on earth anyone would expect to find Hermione Granger.Æ The remainder of the meeting passed in a tangle of debates on logistics, timetables, and ambushes. Tonks took point on updating Robards and Å with a sigh Å Shacklebolt. ØHe wonít be happy about us keeping the harvest moon attacks under wraps. Heíll have to take that up with Robards. But at least weíve a plan to keep Hermione safe and well.Æ Draco escorted Granger back to her cottage to pack up for what Granger called a ØHopefully extremely briefÆ stay at the Manor. The excellent thing about moving Miss Dab Hand at Extension Charms was that it was an almost painless process. Draco hardly had time to send word to the elves to prepare one of the guest suites for Colleague Healer Granger when she announced that she was ready. Whatever belongings sheíd deemed indispensable (including both copies of Revelations) were in a Muggle rolly case thingy, magically Extended. Her cat was wrestled, hissing and scratching, into a carrier. ØIím instructing the elves to keep your stay utterly under wraps,Æ said Draco as they made their way to the cottageís front door. ØMy mother wonít even know about it until we decide that itís safe to say something.Æ Granger looked uneasy. ØWhen will she be back?Æ ØMarch, I believe. Sheís decided to skip the English winter altogether.Æ Grangerís unease persisted. ØRight. Good. The elves themselves, though Å if someone were to try to reach me in the Manor, and one of the elves got hurt? Or killed? The thought makes me sick.Æ ØDidnít you hear Tonks? Stop worrying. No one in their right mind would look for you there. And if they did, theyíd need two dozen ward breakers bashing away for days Å which I can assure you I would notice. This was one of Weasleyís brighter ideas.Æ Granger fell silent, but the frown that pulled her brows together told Draco that she had most certainly not stopped worrying. At the Manor, Henriette greeted them at the great doors and whisked Granger off to a guest suite overlooking the gardens. Grangerís cat was released into the suite, where it indicated its disapproval of the situation by streaking under the bed and hissing at anyone who approached. Draco followed at a distance as Henriette gave Granger a tour of the Manor. The old house-elf had understood the gravity of the situation. There were no coy looks in Dracoís direction, nor any mucking about with roses. Henriette was all business. Colleague Healer Granger must be made comfortable and kept safe. Draco watched the two of them walk ahead of him Å Henrietteís small form and Grangerís slender figure, bent attentively towards the elf as she spoke. Henriette pointed to Monsieurís study on her left and indicated in a whisper that Monsieur should be left alone when he was in there, as he was often grumpy about pestilential levels of incompetence and other things of that nature. Granger nodded gravely, then shot an amused look back at Draco when Henriette proceeded again. The doors to the library were gestured to, then Henriette moved on to the conservatory. Granger lingered at the closed library doors for a moment before hurrying to catch up, and it was Dracoís turn to be amused. The accompanying swell of fondness was quashed before it could make him smile. By the time Granger had been settled and oriented, and Draco and the elves had revisited the Manorís extensive wards for his own peace of mind, it was early evening. If Draco had hopes of a quiet dinner for two that night, they were obliterated by Granger. She had a shift at A&E that she positively refused to miss, as she was the only Healer on duty and her backup was suffering from Spattergroit. Today had felt like a long day, but, as Draco waited for Granger at the foot of the grand staircase, it also felt like it was just beginning. He supposed that he ought to get used to long bloody days. This was, after all, Granger. She trotted down the stairs, her freshly donned Healer robes fluttering behind her. ØIím ready. I suppose I neednít ask if you faint at the sight of blood. Are you all right with eviscerations?Æ ØYes,Æ said Draco. ØGood. One never knows what one is going to walk into at St. Mungoís.Æ Draco cast his most powerful Notice-Me-Not and Disillusion on himself to forestall questions about why an Auror was following Healer Granger about. They Flooed to St. Mungoís for what was to be the first of many shifts at Grangerís side at A&E. Draco performed Legilimency on every mind in the waiting room to satisfy himself that no one had fiendish plans, other than bleeding to death. When that was done, he settled into a corner outside the operating theatre, and proceeded to be moderately disturbed by the eveningís entertainment, which included unpronounceable diseases, a wizard who presented with a Muggle traffic cone protruding straight through his chest, and a truly inspiring amount of patients who had åfallení onto vaguely phallic objects, which were now stuck in various orifices. Draco cast silencing charms to muffle his alternating gasps of horror and laughter. Nothing rattled Granger, however. She dealt with his idiotic countrymen with a relentless professionalism that he couldnít help but admire. ~ If Draco had nursed any thoughts of a long and leisurely breakfast with Granger the next day, they, too, were doomed from the beginning. By the time he got downstairs at the (very respectable, he thought) hour of nine oíclock, Granger had done her yoga thing, showered, dressed, and eaten. He arrived just in time to see her off in the Floo parlour. She was to spend the day at the lab, where Weasley was on Granger-duty. Draco was scheduled to wring out the minds of No-Hands (now simply Hands) and Friend. Draco heard a muffled buzz emanate from Grangerís vicinity. It was her Jotter. She ignored it in favour of twisting her hands together in that anxious gesture of hers. ØI doubt that Greyback will be stupid enough to send anyone to the lab again so soon,Æ she said, sounding as though she was reassuring herself more than speaking to Draco. ØThe wards held beautifully last time. I neednít be worried.Æ There was another muffled buzz from her Jotter. ØItíll be perfectly fine,Æ said Draco. ØTheyíd never be stupid enough to attempt something in broad daylight. And Weasley will be with you. And youíve got the ring. Donít even wait to be sure thereís a threat to use it Å just use it.Æ ØRight.Æ ØNo hesitations. Iíd rather pop in ready to fight the postie than be too late.Æ ØYes. Of course. Thank you.Æ Again, Grangerís Jotter buzzed. Irritated, Draco asked, ØWhoís bloody Jotting you at this hour?Æ Granger hesitated before pulling out the Jotter to look at it. ØEr Å everyone.Æ ØWhy?Æ ØNothing important,Æ said Granger, who clearly never learned that the more she dismissed a thing, the more Draco wanted to know about the thing. ØTell me.Æ Granger looked an interesting mixture of annoyed and sheepish. ØItís my birthday.Æ ØAh,Æ said Draco. There was a longish silence. ØEr Å happy birthday, I suppose,Æ said Draco. Really? That was the best he could manage? Why was it that his suaveness utterly vacated his body when it was most needed? What was it about Granger? She was a murderer of suave. ØThank you,Æ said Granger. ØBut weíve got bigger things to worry about than birthdays, havenít we?Æ ØQuite.Æ Granger threw Floo powder into the fire. ØIíll turn the ring at the slightest provocation, I promise. Cambridge.Æ And then she was gone, and Draco was left to mull over the timeless brilliance of Er Å happy birthday, I suppose and suffer all by himself. Before he Flooed into the office, Draco asked Henriette to help him stage a recovery effort that evening, if Granger was back on the premises at a decent hour. Sheíd have a stupid bloody birthday cake, even if she was trapped in the Manor with a blundering fool. Draco spent his day conducting Legilimency on the two apprehended wizards, having received special permission to do so from the powers that be. The only memory of any real worth was the one he had found the night of the break-in. He spent long hours in Friendís brain in particular, combing through weeks and monthsí worth of memories. Greyback had been careful. A few bits of information about potential meeting locations for Greybackís werewolves were all that Draco gleaned. He passed them to Potter. That evening, his brain feeling more like a queasy, gurgling mix of porridge than actual brain, Draco left for home. Draco and Granger seemed to be developing a speciality for collisions when using magical means of transport. Granger Flooed back to the Manor from Cambridge at almost the same time as he did from London. His only warning was a witch-shaped blur coming at him amongst the hearths he was hurtling past, and the blur was whipped into him (with a shriek that confirmed that it was Granger), and they were both spat out onto the ashy flagstones of the Manorís Floo parlour. There was a tangle of green robes amongst black robes and much coughing up of soot. A shrill giggle echoed through the Floo parlour. By the time Dracoís head had escaped Grangerís skirts, Tupey had disappeared and the little voyeur could not be immediately reprimanded. Draco dropped back down with a groan. The beginnings of a colossal Legilimency headache tingled at the back of his skull. Granger appeared to have accepted the recurring issue of their collisions philosophically and directed no venom towards Draco. Instead, she said, ØRight,Æ and attempted to rise. She trod on her robe and fell over again. ØYou properly Tonksed that one,Æ said Draco. Granger made an exasperated sound and lay on the floor next to Draco, who had already given up. They looked at each other. Granger sighed. Draco tasted smoke. She looked exhausted. He hadnít even had a moment to ask her how sheíd slept during her first night at the Manor Å her fault for getting up so bloody early. ØAnything from the Legilimency?Æ asked Granger. ØOnly minor findings. Potential meeting places. Gave them to Potter.Æ ØDamn it.Æ ØNo trouble at the lab?Æ ØNo. And only one quarrel with Ron.Æ ØAbout what?Æ ØHe wanted to wee in a bottle instead of leaving me by myself for five minutes while he went to the toilet.Æ Draco snorted. ØDedicated sort of bloke.Æ ØHeís always been a bit full-on.Æ ØI can admire that.Æ ØShould we get up?Æ ØNo,Æ said Draco, pressing the back of his head into the cool stone. ØI quite fancy lying here until death takes me.Æ Granger reacted more casually than he would have liked in the face of this dramatic pronouncement. ØMm? Whatís the matter?Æ ØHeadache.Æ ØWent too hard on the Legilimency, did we?Æ ØI wanted answers.Æ ØI can help you with the headache. Bath first Å I've had a sweaty sort of day.Æ ØHenriette can Apparate us to our chambers.Æ ØOur chambers,Æ repeated Granger in an exaggerated drawl. It seemed to give her the courage she needed to push herself up. ØI shall make my way to mine under my own power.Æ ØGo forth and conquer,Æ said Draco. And she did. Dinner was a quiet affair. It began at the formal dining table, then Granger asked Henriette if she would mind terribly if they dined in one of the salons, which offered more scope for stretching out their tired carcasses on sofas. Henriette was delighted to accommodate. She soon had them set up cosily in the smallest salon at the back of the house, around a low table piled with foodstuffs. (Draco did notice the addition of a single red rose in a small vase, but given that there was only one, he decided that it was purely decorative.) Granger dragged a mixed pile of Muggle and wizarding books out of somewhere and took advantage of the moment to brief Draco on their autumnal equinox plans, given that Mabon was only two nights away. She had narrowed her search down to twelve potential sacred sites. Their objective was to identify the dolmen written about in Revelations. ØWe are getting so close to completing this,Æ said Granger, who seemed to take fresh vigour from the thought. ØQuite exciting, really.Æ ØWe? Piffle. Itís all you.Æ Granger looked up. ØYes, we. Youíve been with me on this since the beginning. Donít be modest Å it doesnít suit you.Æ ØAll right. Iíll take whatever reflected glory comes my way,Æ said Draco with a languid wave of his hand. He slid into his sofa until he was lying down and draped his arm over his eyes to block the light that made his head ache. He wanted to go to bed, but it was only eight oíclock. Grangerís hours were rubbing off on him and she'd only been there a day. Granger observed him. ØRight. Your headache. Let's have a look. Why didnít you say something, instead of letting me cram more into your brain for half an hour?Æ When Draco didnít answer (machismo seemed a weak response), Granger pulled out her wand and moved from her sofa to his. He budged up enough to give her room to perch herself beside him. She cast a diagnostic spell, studied the resulting pictorial, and tutted. ØThatís going to develop into a great bloody migraine,Æ she said. ØIíll attempt Solamentum. Itís delicate. Lie still.Æ Draco closed his eyes. He felt the tip of her wand against his temple. The sensation would normally initiate a stress response. He wasnít sure when he had begun to trust Granger this implicitly, but he didnít even crack open an eye. She whispered an incantation and a gentle soothing began to pour into his overwrought brain. ØGlorious,Æ muttered Draco. ØShh. Iíve got to concentrate.Æ ØMm.Æ ØHush.Æ ØMmm.Æ ØCan you stop moaning for a bloody minute?Æ ØNot when it feels this nice Å mff.Æ The warmth of Grangerís fingertip pressed against his lips. His eyes flew open in surprise. Above him, Granger was frowning in concentration, and she flashed him a warning look. He closed his eyes again. Now his other senses grew more sensitive. Against his side he could feel the push of Grangerís thigh and the curve of her bum. Upon his temple, the coolness of the spell. She smelled of something antiseptic, which shouldnít have been as terribly enticing as it was, but he wanted to bury his face into her and inhale. He wondered what would happen if he were to flick his tongue against the finger that was pressed upon his lips. Perhaps something betrayed his thought. Granger removed her finger from his lips and pressed it under his chin instead, tilting his head towards her. She moved her wand to his other temple and he heard the whisper of the incantation again, ØSolamentum.Æ The healing spell irradiated the cramping heaviness away. ØHow is that?Æ asked Granger. Draco did that thing heíd grown to like doing, of giving her answers that actually referred to her. ØGorgeous,Æ said Draco. ØIsnít it?Æ ØHeavenly.Æ ØGood.Æ ØSublime.Æ ØNow youíre just trying to provoke me.Æ ØNo. Itís true.Æ Granger looked to the ceiling in a gesture of mild exasperation and rose. She resumed her seat on the sofa across from Draco, which left him with a distinct feeling of Lack at his side. He wouldíve been perfectly happy for her to continue next to him and whisper complex Healing spells, in lieu of sweet nothings, into his ear. Right. The crush that he was meant to be quashing. He bound and gagged his heart and shoved it into some profound psychic abyss. Henriette materialised with the mealís pice de rsistance Å a small chocolate mousse cake, topped with a single candle. ØOh, merci! C'est trop gentil!Æ exclaimed Granger, a hand pressed to her collarbones. Draco had had a feeling that Granger wouldíve absolutely detested Happy Birthday being sung to her by himself and the elves (as riotous as it wouldíve been), so he had instructed Henriette to leave off the singing. Henriette merely said, ØJoyeux anniversaire, Mademoiselle!Æ and curtseyed out with a crack. ØYou really didnít need to do anything,Æ said Granger to Draco, looking genuinely touched. ØRather a rotten birthday otherwise, stuck in the Manor with me, with a horde of werewolves skulking about and trying to kill you.Æ Granger tugged the candle out of the cake and blew it out. (ØItís more sanitary,Æ she said in the face of Dracoís raised eyebrow.) ØWhat did you wish for?Æ ØCanít say.Æ Draco passed his hand through his hair. ØNothing, Iíd wager. Youíve already got me. What else could you possibly ask for?Æ She laughed, as expected (miserable feeling), and pulled the cake towards herself. ØDo you want some?Æ At his nod, Granger cut them each a gooey slice of the mousse cake. ØRon said heíd check the cottage for parcels for me, on the way home. Heís to drop them off tomorrow.Æ ØGood of him.Æ ØMm.Æ There was silence as the cake was savoured. ØWhat happened between you and Weasley, anyway?Æ asked Draco. As a general rule, he and Granger didnít do personal questions Å a healthy habit to cultivate between Auror and Principal. She had let one slip in Provence, about his schooling Å and now he permitted himself one, out of not-so-idle curiosity. Perhaps it was a query that Granger fielded regularly. She merely shrugged. ØWe wanted different things. We were young when we got engaged Å just out of the war. I had a great many plans that didnít involve building the Burrow II and popping out the next Weasley dynasty.
